topics.title: Milk Types, Composition, and Factors Affecting Composition of Milk; Physical, Chemical, and Thermal Properties; Reception, Storage, and Cooling of Milk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT615 - DAIRY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Milk Types, Composition, and Factors Affecting Composition of Milk; Physical, Chemical, and Thermal Properties; Reception, Storage, and Cooling of Milk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1: Milk — Definition, Types, and Composition

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Milk (KTU 2024 Standard)

As per the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)** and Codex Alimentarius definitions adopted in the KTU 2024 Dairy Technology syllabus, **Milk** is defined as:

> *"Milk is the normal mammary secretion of healthy milking animals, obtained from one or more milkings without either addition thereto or extraction therefrom. It shall be free from colostrum, and shall be cooled immediately after milking to a temperature of not more than **8 °C** (preferably **4 °C**) and held at that temperature until processed."*

The corresponding legal and physical definition used in the dairy industry emphasizes:
- It is a **biological fluid** of animal origin.
- It is a **complex emulsion–colloid–suspension** (an emulsion of fat in water, a colloidal suspension of proteins, and a solution of lactose, minerals, and vitamins in water).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Milk is a *polydisperse system* — fat exists as globules, casein as micelles, and whey proteins, lactose, and minerals in true solution. Recognizing this triphasic nature is the foundation for understanding every subsequent processing step.

## 1.2 Classification of Milk Types

Milk is broadly classified based on **species**, **processing**, and **fat/SNF composition**.

### A. Based on Species (Most Important for KTU Examinations)

| Species | Fat (%) | SNF (%) | Total Solids (%) | Key Features |
|---------|---------|---------|------------------|--------------|
| Cow | **3.5 – 5.0** | **8.5 – 9.0** | 12.0 – 14.0 | Golden-yellow tint due to β-carotene |
| Buffalo | **6.0 – 8.5** | **9.0 – 10.0** | 16.0 – 17.5 | Pure white (no β-carotene), rich in fat & protein |
| Goat | **3.5 – 4.5** | **8.0 – 8.5** | 11.5 – 13.0 | Smaller fat globules, easy digestion |
| Sheep | **5.0 – 8.0** | **10.0 – 11.0** | 15.0 – 19.0 | Concentrated milk; feta & Roquefort cheese |
| Camel | **3.0 – 5.5** | **8.5 – 9.5** | 12.0 – 15.0 | Low lactose, high insulin-like proteins |

### B. Based on Indian Legal/Commercial Standards (Agmark/BIS)

- **Toned Milk** — Cow milk + skim milk + water (3.0% fat, 8.5% SNF)
- **Double Toned Milk** — (1.5% fat, 9.0% SNF)
- **Standardized Milk** — Fat ≥ 4.5%, SNF ≥ 8.5%
- **Full Cream Milk** — Fat ≥ 6.0%, SNF ≥ 9.0%
- **Skimmed Milk** — Fat < 0.5%, SNF ≥ 8.7%

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Insight:** Buffalo milk dominates South Asian dairying due to higher fat recovery for ghee, while cow milk is preferred for fluid milk consumption because of β-carotene (pro-vitamin A).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three-Story Building" Model of Milk

Imagine milk as a **three-story building** sitting in water:

- **Top Floor (Emulsion Phase):** Fat globules — tiny oily droplets (0.1 to 15 µm) coated with a phospholipid-protein membrane (Milk Fat Globule Membrane, MFGM), floating in the water. Think of them as **olive oil droplets suspended in vinegar** (like a salad dressing).
- **Middle Floor (Colloidal Phase):** Casein micelles — calcium phosphate-bound protein clusters (~200 nm), like **cotton balls dipped in chalk powder**, suspended in solution.
- **Ground Floor (True Solution):** Lactose, water-soluble vitamins, minerals (K, Na, Cl), and whey proteins — fully dissolved in **water (87%)**, like sugar dissolved in lemonade.

This triphasic structure explains why milk turns rancid (fat hydrolysis), sours (lactose fermentation), or curdles (casein coagulation) — each floor reacts independently yet influences the others.

## 1.4 Standard Composition of Cow Milk (Reference Frame)

| Component | Percentage (%) | Key Sub-Components |
|-----------|----------------|--------------------|
| Water | **87.0 – 87.5** | Bulk dispersing medium |
| Fat | **3.5 – 5.0** | Triglycerides (98%), phospholipids, sterols |
| Proteins | **3.2 – 3.5** | Caseins (80%) + Whey proteins (20%) |
| Lactose | **4.5 – 5.0** | Disaccharide (Glucose + Galactose) |
| Minerals (ash) | **0.7 – 0.8** | Ca, P, K, Na, Mg, trace elements |
| Vitamins | Trace | A, D, E, K, B-complex, C |
| Enzymes | Trace | Lipase, protease, lactoperoxidase |

**Total Solids (TS)** = Fat + SNF → typically **12.5 – 14.5%** for cow milk.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Chemical Composition of Milk

### 2.1.1 Milk Fat
- Chemically: **Triglycerides (~98%)** of mixed fatty acids.
- Fatty acid profile: Saturated (60–70%) — butyric, palmitic, stearic; Unsaturated (30–40%) — oleic, linoleic.
- Short-chain fatty acids (butyric, caproic) give milk its characteristic flavour.
- Fat globule size: **0.1 – 15 µm** (average ~3.5 µm in cow milk, ~8 µm in buffalo milk).

### 2.1.2 Milk Proteins
- **Caseins (80%):** αs1-, αs2-, β-, κ-casein. Exist as **micelles** (~200 nm) stabilized by κ-casein and colloidal calcium phosphate (CCP).
- **Whey Proteins (20%):** β-lactoglobulin, α-lactalbumin, bovine serum albumin (BSA), immunoglobulins, lactoferrin.
- **Total Nitrogen = Protein N + Non-Protein N (NPN).**
- Conversion factor for protein from nitrogen (Kjeldahl): **6.38** for milk.

### 2.1.3 Lactose
- Disaccharide of **D-glucose + D-galactose** linked by β-1,4 glycosidic bond.
- Lactose content is the **most constant** major component.
- Hydrolyzed by **lactase (β-galactosidase)**; deficiency causes **lactose intolerance**.
- Fermented by lactic acid bacteria to **lactic acid**.

### 2.1.4 Minerals
- **Macrominerals:** Calcium (120 mg/100g), Phosphorus (95 mg/100g), Potassium, Sodium, Magnesium.
- Calcium exists as **colloidal calcium phosphate** (CCP) and **soluble calcium**.
- **Ca:P ratio in milk ≈ 1.2:1**.

### 2.1.5 Vitamins
- **Fat-soluble:** A, D, E, K.
- **Water-soluble:** B1, B2, B6, B12, C, niacin, pantothenic acid, folic acid.

### 2.1.6 Enzymes (Biologically Active Proteins)
- **Lipases** (spontaneous rancidity), **Proteases** (plasmin → casein breakdown), **Lactoperoxidase** (antibacterial, used in cold sterilization), **Alkaline Phosphatase** (pasteurization indicator), **Catalase**, **Xanthine Oxidase**.

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Composition of Milk

| Factor | Effect on Composition |
|--------|----------------------|
| **Species** | Most influential — buffalo milk > cow milk > goat milk in fat & TS |
| **Breed** | Holstein-Friesian (low fat, high yield); Jersey & Guernsey (high fat) |
| **Feed/Nutrition** | High roughage → ↑ SNF; green fodder → ↑ vitamin A; cottonseed → softer fat |
| **Stage of Lactation** | Colostrum (very high protein & globulins, low lactose) → Normal milk → Late lactation (↑ fat, ↓ yield) |
| **Season** | Summer (↓ fat, ↑ SNF); Winter (↑ fat); Monsoon (↓ SNF due to lush grass) |
| **Age of Animal** | Older cows → ↓ yield, slight ↑ fat |
| **Health** | Mastitis → ↓ lactose, ↑ Na, ↑ Cl, ↓ casein (alteration in salt balance) |
| **Milking Interval** | Shorter interval → lower fat; longer interval → higher fat |
| **Time of Milking** | Morning milk — lower fat; Evening milk — higher fat |
| **Completeness of Milking** | Strippings contain 4–5× higher fat than fore-milk |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The composition variation due to **mastitis** is a frequently asked question — remember the chloride-lactose ratio increases, which is the basis of the **mastitis detection test (chloride test)**.

## 2.3 Physical, Chemical, and Thermal Properties of Milk — KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Property | Cow Milk Value | Unit / Equation | Engineering Significance |
|----------|---------------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| **Density (ρ)** | **1.028 – 1.034** | g/mL at 20 °C | Used in weigh-vat calibration; for fat-corrected density → Gerber correction |
| **Specific Gravity (SG)** | **1.028 – 1.033** | Dimensionless | Lactometer reading; adulteration detection |
| **Viscosity (η)** | **1.5 – 2.0** | cP at 20 °C | Affects pumping, pasteurizer flow, cream separation |
| **Surface Tension (γ)** | **40 – 60** | dyne/cm (10⁻³ N/m) | Lower than water (72 dyne/cm); crucial for foaming, homogenization |
| **Freezing Point (Fp)** | **−0.522 to −0.564** | °C | Hortvet method for water adulteration |
| **Boiling Point** | **100.17 – 100.20** | °C | Slightly elevated due to dissolved solids |
| **Refractive Index (n)** | **1.3440 – 1.3480** | at 20 °C | Basis of optical milk-fat testers |
| **pH** | **6.5 – 6.7** | – | Buffering system of milk |
| **Titratable Acidity (TA)** | **0.10 – 0.18** | % lactic acid | Quality check for fresh milk |
| **Specific Heat (Cp)** | **0.93 – 0.95** | cal/(g·°C) | Heat load calculation in cooling/heating |
| **Thermal Conductivity (k)** | **0.0012 – 0.0014** | cal/(s·cm·°C) | Heat exchanger design |
| **Thermal Diffusivity (α)** | **0.0013 – 0.0015** | cm²/s | Transient heat conduction; α = k / (ρ·Cp) |
| **Electrical Conductivity** | **0.004 – 0.005** | mho/cm | Detects mastitis (↑ Na⁺, Cl⁻ → ↑ conductivity) |
| **Buffering Capacity** | High | – | Resistance to pH change; important for fermentation |

### 2.3.1 Master Equations (Without Pipe Symbols to Avoid Table Conflicts)

- **Specific heat of milk (empirical):**

$$C_p = 1.4198 - 0.00560 \cdot F + 0.00193 \cdot S \quad \text{(kJ/kg·°C)}$$

where F = fat %, S = SNF % (valid for 0–80 °C).

- **Freezing point depression (relation to water adulteration):**

$$\Delta T_f = -0.000 \cdot \text{Water Added (\%)} \cdot 0.008 \quad \text{(°C)}$$

Each 1% added water depresses freezing point by approximately **0.008 °C**.

- **Heat load for milk cooling (fundamental equation):**

$$Q = M \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T \quad \text{(kcal)}$$

- **Total Solids calculation:**

$$TS \% = \frac{(W_2 - W_1)}{W} \times 100$$

- **Solids-Not-Fat (SNF):**

$$SNF \% = TS \% - Fat \%$$

- **Buffering capacity (Van Slyke):**

$$\beta = \frac{dB}{d\text{pH}}$$

where dB is the small amount of strong base added per litre of milk.

- **Thermal diffusivity:**

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho \cdot C_p}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Property | Industrial Application |
|----------|------------------------|
| Density | **Flow meters, weigh bowls**, design of cream separators |
| Viscosity | **Pump sizing, Reynolds number** for pipe flow |
| Specific heat | **Refrigeration load** of bulk milk coolers (BMC) |
| Freezing point | **Hortvet apparatus** for detecting water adulteration (legal evidence) |
| Surface tension | **Homogenization efficiency**, foam control in pasteurizer |
| Thermal conductivity | **Plate heat exchanger (PHE)** sizing for HTST pasteurizer |
| pH & TA | **Quality grading**, fermented milk starter culture checks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why thermal properties matter:** A modern dairy plant handling 100,000 L/day at 4 °C requires precise heat-load calculations — an error of 5% in Cp can shift compressor sizing by 50 kW, costing lakhs in capital and recurring power bills.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Specific Heat of Milk from Component Fractions (Rigorous Treatment)

### Statement
The specific heat of milk is the **mass-weighted average** of the specific heats of its constituents, since milk behaves as an ideal dilute solution for thermal energy storage purposes.

### Given
- Water mass fraction: $x_w = 0.87$
- Fat mass fraction: $x_f = 0.04$
- Protein mass fraction: $x_p = 0.035$
- Lactose mass fraction: $x_l = 0.048$
- Ash mass fraction: $x_a = 0.007$
- $C_{p,w} = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- $C_{p,f} = 2.093 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ (fats — long hydrocarbon chains)
- $C_{p,p} = 1.500 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- $C_{p,l} = 1.250 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- $C_{p,a} = 0.900 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$

### Derivation

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{p,\text{milk}} &= x_w C_{p,w} + x_f C_{p,f} + x_p C_{p,p} + x_l C_{p,l} + x_a C_{p,a} \\
&= (0.87)(4.186) + (0.04)(2.093) + (0.035)(1.500) + (0.048)(1.250) + (0.007)(0.900) \\
&= 3.6418 + 0.0837 + 0.0525 + 0.0600 + 0.0063 \\
&= 3.844 \ \text{kJ/(kg·K)} \\
&\approx 0.918 \ \text{kcal/(kg·K)}
\end{aligned}$$

This matches the experimentally measured range of **0.93 – 0.95 kcal/(kg·K)**, confirming the theoretical basis.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Refrigeration Load for Cooling 10,000 L of Milk from 35 °C to 4 °C

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Given:**
- Volume: $V = 10{,}000 \text{ L}$
- Density: $\rho = 1.03 \text{ kg/L}$ (standard for cow milk)
- Initial temp: $T_1 = 35 \text{ °C}$
- Final temp: $T_2 = 4 \text{ °C}$
- $C_p = 0.94 \text{ kcal/(kg·°C)}$ (standard value used in industry)

**Step 1: Mass of milk**

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= V \times \rho \\
M &= 10{,}000 \times 1.03 \\
M &= 10{,}300 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Sensible heat to be removed**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{sensible}} &= M \times C_p \times (T_1 - T_2) \\
Q_{\text{sensible}} &= 10{,}300 \times 0.94 \times (35 - 4) \\
Q_{\text{sensible}} &= 10{,}300 \times 0.94 \times 31 \\
Q_{\text{sensible}} &= 300{,}142 \text{ kcal}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Losses (5% for equipment, 5% for radiation)**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{loss}} &= 0.10 \times 300{,}142 \\
Q_{\text{loss}} &= 30{,}014 \text{ kcal}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Total refrigeration load**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{total}} &= 300{,}142 + 30{,}014 \\
Q_{\text{total}} &= 330{,}156 \text{ kcal} \\
&\approx 330{,}156 \times 4.186 \text{ kJ} \\
&\approx 1{,}382{,}032 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Compressor sizing (1 TR = 3024 kcal/h refrigeration)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Chilling time required} &= 2 \text{ hours (typical for plate cooler)} \\
\text{Load per hour} &= \frac{330{,}156}{2} = 165{,}078 \text{ kcal/h} \\
\text{TR required} &= \frac{165{,}078}{3024} \\
\text{TR required} &\approx 54.6 \text{ TR}
\end{aligned}$$

> This shows why medium-scale dairies (10,000 LPD) need a **~55 TR refrigeration plant** — a calculation KTU examiners frequently test.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Detection of Water Adulteration Using Freezing Point Depression

### Theory
Milk has a constant freezing point of **−0.522 °C (Hortvet)** because the osmotic effect of dissolved lactose and salts is fixed. Addition of water dilutes these solutes and raises the freezing point.

### Calculation
If a sample freezes at **−0.460 °C**, find the % added water.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Depression observed} &= -0.460 - (-0.522) = 0.062 \text{ °C} \\
\text{Normal depression} &= -0.522 \text{ °C (Hortvet, } 0.522 \text{)} \\
\text{Fraction of water added} &= \frac{0.062}{0.522} \\
&= 0.1188 \\
\text{Percentage water added} &= 11.88 \%
\end{aligned}$$

Thus, about **11.9% water** has been added — violating FSSAI standards completely.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Cooling Methods at Reception Dock

| Cooling Method | Capacity (L/h) | ΔT Achieved | Energy Efficiency | Typical Use |
|----------------|---------------|-------------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Surface Cooler (Chilled Water)** | 5,000 – 15,000 | 30 → 10 °C | Medium | Small dairies |
| **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)** | 20,000 – 1,00,000 | 35 → 4 °C | High | HTST plants |
| **Bulk Milk Cooler (BMC)** | 500 – 5,000 | 35 → 4 °C (slow) | Low (direct expansion) | Chilling centres |
| **Tubular Cooler** | 10,000 – 30,000 | 35 → 6 °C | High | Viscous products |
| **Vacuum Cooler** | 5,000 – 25,000 | 35 → 4 °C (flash) | Very high | Removes off-flavours |

## 3.5 Reception Workflow — Mass & Volume Balance

**Given:** A chilling centre receives milk in cans. 200 cans × 40 L each.

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{total}} &= 200 \times 40 = 8{,}000 \text{ L} \\
M_{\text{total}} &= 8{,}000 \times 1.03 = 8{,}240 \text{ kg} \\
\text{TS recovered} &= 8{,}240 \times 0.13 = 1{,}071.2 \text{ kg of total solids}
\end{aligned}$$

These mass-balance calculations form the basis of **dairy plant material accounting** (KTU Module 1).

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Triphasic Nature of Milk (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[RAW MILK] --> B[Emulsion Phase]
    A --> C[Colloidal Phase]
    A --> D[True Solution Phase]
    B --> B1[Fat Globules 0.1 to 15 micrometers]
    B --> B2[MFGM Membrane Coat]
    B --> B3[Lipase and Phospholipids]
    C --> C1[Casein Micelles 200 nm]
    C --> C2[Calcium Phosphate]
    C --> C3[kappa casein Stabilizer]
    D --> D1[Lactose 4.5 to 5 percent]
    D --> D2[Water Soluble Minerals]
    D --> D3[Whey Proteins]
    D --> D4[Water Soluble Vitamins]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Factors Affecting Composition of Milk

```mermaid
graph LR
    F1[Species] --> X[Milk Composition]
    F2[Breed] --> X
    F3[Feed] --> X
    F4[Season] --> X
    F5[Stage of Lactation] --> X
    F6[Age of Animal] --> X
    F7[Health Status Mastitis etc] --> X
    F8[Milking Interval] --> X
    F9[Completeness of Milking] --> X
    X --> Y1[Fat Percentage]
    X --> Y2[Protein Percentage]
    X --> Y3[SNF Content]
    X --> Y4[Vitamin Levels]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Milk Reception Flow at a Dairy Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Arrival of Milk Cans or Tanker] --> R2[Weighing on Weigh Bridge]
    R2 --> R3[Sampling with Agitator Probe]
    R3 --> R4[Organoleptic Tests Smell and Visual]
    R4 --> R5[Platform Tests]
    R5 --> R5a[Alcohol Test]
    R5 --> R5b[Clot on Boiling Test]
    R5 --> R5c[Acidity Test]
    R5 --> R5d[Density Lactometer]
    R5 --> R6{Quality Acceptable?}
    R6 -->|Yes| R7[Pumping via Strainer to Chilling Tank]
    R6 -->|No| R8[Rejection or Downgrading]
    R7 --> R9[Cooling to 4 deg C in PHE or BMC]
    R9 --> R10[Storage in Silo at Less Than 4 deg C]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Plate Heat Exchanger Cooling Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    M1[Raw Milk In at 35 deg C] --> PHE[PLATE HEAT EXCHANGER PHE]
    PHE --> PHE1[Regeneration Section with Cold Milk]
    PHE1 --> PHE2[Hot Water Heating to 72 deg C]
    PHE2 --> PHE3[Cooling Section with Chilled Water]
    PHE3 --> PHE4[Final Cooling with Brine to 4 deg C]
    PHE4 --> M2[Chilled Milk to Storage Silo]

    HW1[Hot Water 85 deg C] --> PHE2
    CW1[Chilled Water 1 deg C] --> PHE3
    BR1[Brine Minus 8 deg C] --> PHE4

    PHE1 --> REG[Regenerated Milk Out at 18 deg C]
    REG --> PHE
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Storage Hierarchy in a Dairy Plant

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Raw Milk Reception Tank 4 deg C] --> S2[Raw Milk Silos 5000 to 50000 L]
    S2 --> S3[Processing Tanks]
    S3 --> S4[Storage of Finished Products]
    S4 --> S4a[Pasteurized Milk Tanks 4 deg C]
    S4 --> S4b[Cream Storage Tanks 4 deg C]
    S4 --> S4c[Flavoured Milk Tanks]
    S2 --> S5[Insulation with PU Foam]
    S2 --> S6[Agitator Slow Speed 30 RPM]
    S2 --> S7[Temperature Recorder and Controller]
```

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram 6 — Functional Flow of Cold Chain from Farm to Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Milking at Farm] --> F2[Filter and Strain]
    F2 --> F3[Immediate Cooling to 4 deg C BMC]
    F3 --> F4[Insulated Transport]
    F4 --> F5[Reception at Plant Dock]
    F5 --> F6[Re-cooling in Plate Cooler]
    F6 --> F7[Storage Silo Less Than 4 deg C]
    F7 --> F8[HTST Processing]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Each "node" uses alphanumeric IDs to ensure compatibility with Mermaid v10+ rendering engines. Labels are intentionally raw uppercase text to avoid markdown conflicts.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]
**Define milk. List four types of milk based on species with one characteristic of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Milk is the normal mammary secretion of healthy milking animals, obtained from one or more milkings without either addition thereto or extraction therefrom.

**Four Types with characteristics (1.5 marks — 0.5 each for type + 0.25 each for characteristic OR full 1.5 for any sensible classification):**

| Type | Characteristic |
|------|----------------|
| Cow milk | 3.5–5.0% fat, contains β-carotene |
| Buffalo milk | 6.0–8.5% fat, pure white, no β-carotene |
| Goat milk | Smaller fat globules, easily digestible |
| Sheep milk | High total solids (~18%); used for cheese |
| Camel milk | Low lactose (~4.4%), high insulin-like proteins |

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Part A]
**List the major constituents of milk with their approximate percentage composition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Component | Cow Milk (%) | Buffalo Milk (%) |
|-----------|--------------|------------------|
| Water | 87.0 | 83.0 |
| Fat | 3.5–5.0 | 6.0–8.5 |
| Proteins | 3.2–3.5 | 3.8–4.5 |
| Lactose | 4.5–5.0 | 4.5–5.2 |
| Minerals (ash) | 0.7–0.8 | 0.7–0.8 |
| Vitamins + Enzymes | Trace | Trace |

(Any four components with values = 2.5 marks; total constituents listing = 0.5 mark.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question 1A (14 Marks — Full Module-1 Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Jul 2023]
**"Milk is a complex biochemical system whose processing begins at the moment of milking." Justify this statement.**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block architecture diagram, explain the **triphasic nature of milk** and discuss how each phase responds to processing conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **factors affecting the composition of milk**, emphasizing the impact of feed, season, and stage of lactation. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** Milk is a polydisperse system containing fat as emulsion, protein as colloid, and lactose/minerals as true solution.

2. **Triphasic explanation (4 marks):**
   - **Emulsion phase (fat globules):** Diameter 0.1–15 µm; stabilized by MFGM; ruptures during homogenization; susceptible to lipase → hydrolytic rancidity.
   - **Colloidal phase (casein micelles):** 200 nm aggregates of αs1, αs2, β, κ-casein; bound by colloidal calcium phosphate; coagulates at pH 4.6 (isoelectric point) — basis for cheese-making and paneer manufacture.
   - **True solution phase:** Lactose, whey proteins, minerals — responds to heat (Maillard browning between lactose and lysine), ion exchange (de-mineralization), and fermentation.

3. **Block diagram (1 mark):** As shown in Section 4.1.

4. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Each phase dictates its own processing technology — hence the statement is justified.

### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Intrinsic Factors:**
- **Species (1.5 marks):** Cow vs. buffalo vs. goat — most important.
- **Breed (0.5 marks):** Jersey/Holstein variation.

**Extrinsic Factors:**
- **Feed (2 marks):** Green fodder → ↑ carotene; cottonseed → softer fat; dry fodder → lower SNF.
- **Season (1.5 marks):** Summer (heat stress → ↑ water, ↓ fat); Winter (↑ fat); Monsoon (lush grass, ↑ SNF).
- **Stage of lactation (1.5 marks):** Colostrum (immunoglobulins + high protein, low lactose); Mid-lactation (standard composition); Late lactation (↑ fat, ↓ yield, altered salts).

---

## 5.3 Part B Question 1B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"The receiving dock is the most critical quality-control gateway in a dairy plant." Discuss.**

**(a)** Describe the complete **procedure for reception of milk** at a dairy plant, including platform tests, sampling, and grading. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a labelled PHE block diagram, explain the **cooling, storage, and cold-chain management** of milk from reception to processing. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

1. **Tanker/Cans arrival (0.5):** Weighbridge weighing; documentation.
2. **Sampling (1 mark):** Agitator-probe from tanker; representative dipper for cans.
3. **Organoleptic tests (1 mark):** Smell, appearance, colour (no clots, no off-odour).
4. **Platform tests (3 marks):**
   - **Sediment test:** Visible impurities
   - **Alcohol test:** 68% ethanol — clot indicates acidity/mastitis
   - **Clot-on-boiling test:** 5 mL milk boiled for 2 min — clot indicates unstable protein
   - **Acidity test:** Titration with 0.1 N NaOH
   - **Density test:** Lactometer reading 28–33° at 20 °C
5. **Grading (1 mark):** Class I, II, III or rejection.
6. **Pumping (0.5):** Through strainer and filter to bulk tank.

### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

1. **Why cooling is essential (1 mark):** Bacterial growth doubles every 20 min at ambient; cooling to 4 °C stops mesophilic growth.

2. **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE) Sections (3 marks):**
   - **Regeneration section:** Pre-cools incoming hot milk by outgoing pasteurized milk (energy recovery 90%).
   - **Heating section:** Hot water heating to 72 °C (15 s HTST).
   - **Cooling sections:** Chilled water → Brine (–8 °C) → Final 4 °C exit.

3. **Storage in silos (1.5 marks):**
   - Insulated (PU foam) stainless steel silos.
   - Slow agitator (30 RPM) prevents fat separation.
   - Temperature recorder with auto-alarm at >5 °C.
   - Capacity: 5,000 – 50,000 L.

4. **Cold-chain summary (1.5 marks):** Farm BMC → insulated tanker → plant dock → PHE → silo → pasteurizer — temperature never breaches 4 °C for more than 2 hours (the "2-hour rule" of KTU food safety).

---

## 5.4 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Definition of milk (½ mark lost):** Students forget to mention "obtained without addition or extraction" — both phrases mandatory as per FSSAI.
>
> 2. **Specific heat units (1 mark lost):** Students write **cal/g°C** without qualifying "at 20 °C". KTU expects the temperature reference explicitly.
>
> 3. **Heat load calculation (2–3 marks lost):** Failure to add 5–10% loss factor for radiation and pipe-line losses makes the calculation incomplete.
>
> 4. **Freezing point sign error:** Forgetting the negative sign (−0.522 °C); using absolute value leads to wrong adulteration percentage.
>
> 5. **Confusing TS and SNF:** Total Solids ≠ SNF. Many students write SNF = Fat + Protein (incorrect — SNF excludes fat).
>
> 6. **Skipping buffer equations:** In "chemical properties" questions, examiners expect at least the **Van Slyke buffer index** formula; missing it = 2 marks lost.
>
> 7. **Neglecting mastitis effect:** A favourite KTU question — chloride content **increases** and lactose **decreases** in mastitic milk. Reversing these costs 1.5 marks.
>
> 8. **PHE diagram missing labels:** Examiner's pet peeve — every plate section must be labelled with **temperature, medium, and purpose**.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU Module 1:**

- ✅ **Definition of Milk:** FSSAI definition — "normal mammary secretion… without addition or extraction." Three-phase system: emulsion + colloid + true solution.
- ✅ **Five major milk types:** Cow (β-carotene), Buffalo (pure white, high fat), Goat (small globules), Sheep (high TS), Camel (low lactose).
- ✅ **Standard composition:** Water 87%, Fat 4%, Protein 3.4%, Lactose 4.8%, Minerals 0.7% — Total Solids ~13%.
- ✅ **Caseins 80%, Whey 20%:** κ-casein keeps micelles stable; rennet cleaves it → coagulation.
- ✅ **Lactose is the most constant component** — only major component not affected by mastitis direction reversal (mastitis ↓ lactose).
- ✅ **Mastitis signature:** ↑ Chloride, ↑ Na, ↓ Lactose, ↓ Casein, ↑ Conductivity.
- ✅ **Specific heat of milk ≈ 0.93–0.95 kcal/(kg·°C)** or **3.9 kJ/(kg·K)**.
- ✅ **Freezing point of milk = −0.522 °C (Hortvet standard)**; each 1% added water raises it by **0.008 °C**.
- ✅ **Density of cow milk = 1.028–1.034 g/mL; buffalo = 1.030–1.035 g/mL.**
- ✅ **Surface tension = 40–60 dyne/cm** (lower than water 72 due to fat/protein surface activity).
- ✅ **Refractive index = 1.3440–1.3480 at 20 °C** — used in optical fat analyzers.
- ✅ **Acidity of fresh milk = 0.10–0.18% lactic acid; pH 6.5–6.7.**
- ✅ **Reception workflow:** Weigh → Sample → Organoleptic → Platform tests → Grade → Pump → Cool.
- ✅ **Five platform tests:** Sediment, Alcohol, Clot-on-boiling, Acidity (titration), Density (lactometer).
- ✅ **Cooling requirement:** 4 °C within 2 hours of milking — the **Golden Rule of Cold Chain**.
- ✅ **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)** has 3 sections: Regeneration, Heating, Cooling.
- ✅ **Bulk Milk Cooler (BMC)** is the on-farm immediate cooler; **Silo** is the plant-level storage.
- ✅ **Heat load formula: Q = M·Cp·ΔT** — always add 5–10% loss factor.
- ✅ **Thermal diffusivity α = k / (ρ·Cp)** — controls transient cooling rate.
- ✅ **Buffering capacity** of milk is high — important for fermented products (yoghurt, cheese).
- ✅ **Enzymes to remember:** Lipase (rancidity), Alkaline Phosphatase (pasteurization indicator), Lactoperoxidase (cold sterilization), Plasmin (proteolysis).
- ✅ **Two-hour rule, four-degree rule, six-axis of quality control** — examiner buzzwords.

---

## 5.6 CO (Course Outcome) Mapping (KTU 2024 NEP Alignment)

| Course Outcome | Question Mapping | Bloom's Level |
|----------------|------------------|---------------|
| **CO1:** Recall milk types, composition, and properties | Part A Q1, Q2; Part B sub-parts on composition | Remember, Understand |
| **CO2:** Apply physical/thermal property equations to engineering calculations | Heat load derivation, Freezing point calculation | Apply, Analyze |
| **CO3:** Evaluate factors affecting composition for process decisions | Part B Q(b) on factors | Evaluate |
| **CO4:** Design reception, cooling, and storage protocols | Part B Q(a) and Q(b) on reception + PHE | Create, Design |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Types of Coolers and Cooling Systems; Cleaning – Basic Principles; Can Washing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT615 - DAIRY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Different Types of Coolers and Cooling Systems; Cleaning – Basic Principles; Can Washing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Coolers in Dairy Processing — What Are They?

In dairy technology, a **cooler** is a heat-exchange device used to reduce the temperature of milk and milk products rapidly after processing (typically from pasteurization temperature of ~72 °C down to 4 °C or below). The objective is to **inhibit bacterial growth, preserve quality, and extend shelf life**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A dairy cooler is a *heat exchanger* designed to transfer sensible heat from milk to a cooling medium (water, brine, glycol, or evaporating refrigerant) in a controlled, hygienic, and continuous (or batch) manner, complying with the **BIS/FSSAI hygienic design** standards.

### 🔎 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bucket of Cold Water vs. the Smart Plate Cooler"
Imagine you have a hot cup of tea. You can cool it by:
1. **Putting it in a bucket of cold water** — slow, uncontrolled, the cup's outer surface touches water directly (this is like an **immersion cooler**).
2. **Pouring the tea through a cold metal spoon** — faster, the metal acts as a heat-transfer bridge (this is the principle of a **surface / plate cooler**).
3. **Blowing cold air on the cup with a fan** — slowest, used for bulk tanks (**air-cooled systems**).

A dairy cooler is the engineered, hygienic, sanitary-stainless-steel version of these everyday cooling tricks — but operating at hundreds of litres per hour, with precise temperature control and CIP (Clean-in-Place) compatibility.

---

## 1.2 Cooling Systems — The Refrigeration Backbone

A **cooling system** is the *complete thermodynamic assembly* (compressor, condenser, expansion valve, evaporator + refrigerant circuit) that *produces and delivers* the cooling medium used by the cooler. The cooler is the *user*; the cooling system is the *producer*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction to memorize (frequently asked in KTU):**
> - **Cooler** = the heat exchanger (where milk gives up heat).
> - **Cooling System** = the refrigeration plant that supplies chilled water, brine, or directly expands refrigerant into the cooler.

### 🔎 Conceptual Analogy — "The AC, the Chilled-Water Pipe, and the Radiator"
Think of your home split AC: the indoor unit (evaporator) is the *cooler* for room air; the outdoor unit (compressor + condenser) is the *cooling system*. In a dairy plant, milk is the "room" and a plate cooler is the "indoor unit" — the refrigeration plant supplies chilled water or refrigerant to it.

---

## 1.3 Cleaning in a Dairy Plant — The Hygiene Foundation

**Cleaning** in dairy processing is the systematic removal of milk residues (fat, protein, lactose, mineral salts — collectively called *milk soil*) and micro-organisms from equipment surfaces, using **physical, chemical, and thermal energy** in a defined sequence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TACT Principle (Master Equation of Cleaning):** Cleaning effectiveness is the product of four variables —
> $$E = T \times A \times C \times T$$
> where **T** = Time, **A** = Action (mechanical/flow), **C** = Concentration (chemical), **T** = Temperature. If any one falls to zero, cleaning fails.

### 🔎 Conceptual Analogy — "Washing a Greasy Frying Pan"
You don't just splash water on a greasy pan. You (1) **soak** it (T = time), (2) **scrub** it (A = action), (3) **add detergent** (C = chemical), and (4) use **hot water** (T = temperature). A dairy CIP system automates the same four steps inside a closed pipe loop.

---

## 1.4 Can Washing — The Entry-Point Hygiene Step

A **milk can** (typically 40 L aluminium or stainless steel) is the traditional collection container used at village dairy collection centres. **Can washing** is the post-emptying process of removing milk residue, sterilizing, and drying the can before reuse — critical because a poorly washed can can re-infect thousands of litres of pooled milk.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Modern dairies use **bulk milk coolers (BMCs)** at village level, reducing can use. However, in Kerala's cooperative dairy network (Milma) and many small-scale operations, can washing remains a high-yield exam topic.

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics Used Across the Module

| Metric | Value | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Pasteurization exit temperature** | **72 °C** (HTST) | Milk must be cooled from here rapidly. |
| **Legal storage temperature** | **≤ 4 °C** | BIS/FSSAI mandate to inhibit psychrotrophs. |
| **First-hour cooling target (KTU keyword)** | **to 10 °C within 1 hour of milking** | Raw milk must drop to ≤10 °C in 1 h. |
| **CIP loop temperature** | **75–85 °C** (caustic phase) | Ensures fat & protein solubilisation. |
| **CIP loop flow velocity** | **1.5–2.0 m/s** | Turbulent flow for soil removal. |
| **Can-wash water temperature** | **≥ 82 °C** | Equivalent to boiling — bactericidal. |
| **Steam sterilization for cans** | **100 °C for 10 min** | Sterilization standard. |
| **Refrigerant (modern)** | **R-404A / R-507 / Ammonia (R-717)** | Low GWP options. |

---

## 1.6 Why This Module Matters in the KTU 2024 Scheme

This topic directly addresses:
- **CO1:** *Recall the principles of milk processing equipment* (mapped to Bloom Level 1 & 2).
- **CO2:** *Describe the construction and operation of dairy processing equipment* (Bloom Level 2 & 3).
- It is a **favourite area for 7-mark sub-questions** in Part B, and frequently tested via *draw-and-label* questions in Part A.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Dairy Coolers

Dairy coolers are classified along **three engineering axes**: (a) **construction geometry**, (b) **mode of contact**, (c) **flow arrangement**.

### A. By Construction Geometry

| Type | Construction | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Plate Cooler (PHE)** | Series of pressed stainless-steel plates with gaskets | Most common in HTST pasteurizer downstream |
| **Tubular Cooler** | Concentric or coiled tubes | Viscous products, fouling-tolerant |
| **Jacketed Vessel / VAT Cooler** | Outer jacket around a process vat | Batch pasteurizer cooling stage |
| **Scraped-Surface Cooler** | Cylinder with rotating scraper blades | Sticky/viscous products (cream, ice-cream mix) |

### B. By Mode of Contact

| Type | Contact Principle |
|---|---|
| **Surface (indirect) cooler** | Milk separated from coolant by a metal wall — **most hygienic** |
| **Immersion cooler** | Cooling coil immersed directly in milk (small farm scale) |
| **Spray cooler** | Milk sprayed as film over chilled surfaces |

### C. By Flow Arrangement

| Type | Direction |
|---|---|
| **Co-current** | Milk and coolant flow same direction — poor ΔT utilisation |
| **Counter-current** ⭐ | Milk and coolant flow opposite — **highest LMTD, most efficient** |
| **Cross-flow** | Flows perpendicular — used in compact PHEs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *"Counter-current is the king of coolers."* It is the **default and correct answer** in any "which is most efficient?" question.

---

## 2.2 The Major Cooler Types (Detailed)

### 2.2.1 Plate Cooler (Plate Heat Exchanger — PHE)
- **Construction:** Pack of stainless-steel (SS-304/316) pressed plates, each with 4 corner ports. Gaskets (NBR/EPDM) seal the plate pairs and direct fluids alternately.
- **Operation:** Hot milk enters one end, cold water/refrigerant enters the opposite end; they exchange heat through plate walls (typically 0.5–0.6 mm thick).
- **Advantages:** High heat-transfer area per unit volume (~250 m²/m³), easy to expand (add plates), easy to clean manually when opened.
- **Disadvantage:** Gasket failure causes cross-contamination; limited pressure differential (~10 bar).
- **Sanitary Standard:** Plates marked with **'PHE-S' or 'Food Grade' stamp**; complies with 3-A Sanitary Standards, USA.

### 2.2.2 Tubular Cooler
- **Construction:** A bundle of small-bore tubes inside a larger shell, OR a single long coiled tube.
- **Operation:** Milk flows inside the tube, coolant on the shell side (or vice-versa).
- **Use:** Often used as a *pre-cooler* before a plate cooler, particularly when milk is heavily soiled or fibrous.
- **Advantage:** No gaskets, fully welded, withstands higher pressure differential.

### 2.2.3 Surface (Can) Cooler — the Traditional Indian Dairy Workhorse
- **Construction:** A horizontal/vertical rectangular tank with a refrigerant evaporator coil or chilled water coil on its outer/inner surface. Milk cans are placed in contact.
- **Use:** Small chilling centres in rural India.
- **Drawback:** Slow, batch operation, large footprint.

### 2.2.4 Immersion Cooler
- **Construction:** A stainless-steel coil carrying chilled water/refrigerant is *immersed directly* in a milk vat.
- **Use:** On-farm or very small dairies.
- **Drawback:** Cleaning is harder; coil must be removable.

### 2.2.5 Jacketed Vessel Cooler
- **Construction:** An outer jacket welded to a process vessel; coolant circulates in the annular jacket space.
- **Use:** Batch pasteurizer cooling stage (e.g., after holding in a vat pasteurizer).

### 2.2.6 Bulk Milk Cooler (BMC) — *Direct Expansion Type*
- **Construction:** An insulated cylindrical/horizontal tank with a **direct-expansion (DX) evaporator coil** welded to the inner wall, or a chilled water jacket.
- **Operation:** Milk is poured in; DX refrigerant expands inside the wall coils, absorbing heat directly.
- **Capacity:** 100 L to 5000 L.
- **KTU Hot Question:** *"Explain the working of a Direct-Expansion Bulk Milk Cooler."* — this is a **guaranteed 7-marker**.

### 2.2.7 Ice-Bank Cooler
- **Construction:** A tank containing a **bundle of coils immersed in water**; refrigerant freezes the water into ice during off-peak hours. During milking, ice-water circulates around milk cans/through PHEs.
- **KTU Concept:** Uses *latent heat of fusion* of water (334 kJ/kg) — store cooling energy cheaply at night.
- **Advantage:** Saves electricity cost (operates compressor at night when tariff is low).

---

## 2.3 Cooling Systems — The Refrigeration Plant

### 2.3.1 Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC) — Foundation
Four processes form the cycle:

1. **Compression** (low-pressure vapour → high-pressure vapour) — *compressor*.
2. **Condensation** (high-pressure vapour → high-pressure liquid, rejects heat to ambient) — *condenser*.
3. **Expansion** (high-pressure liquid → low-pressure liquid+vapour mixture) — *expansion valve*.
4. **Evaporation** (low-pressure liquid → vapour, absorbs heat from milk side) — *evaporator*.

### 2.3.2 Dairy-Plant Cooling System Variants

| System | Description | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Expansion (DX)** | Refrigerant expands directly inside cooler coil | BMCs, small dairies |
| **Ice-Bank System** | Ice is built around coils at night; ice-water circulates | Village collection centres |
| **Chilled Water System** | Refrigerant chills a water/glycol loop; loop serves PHEs | Large dairy plants |
| **Brine System (CaCl₂ / NaCl)** | Refrigerant chills a salt-brine loop to sub-zero (–10 °C) | Ice-cream hardening tunnels |
| **Glycol System** | Propylene-glycol/water mix; food-grade; used in plate coolers | Pre-cooling raw milk to 4 °C |

---

## 2.4 Cleaning — Basic Principles (Engineering Depth)

### 2.4.1 The TACT Principle (Re-stated)
$$E_{cleaning} = T \times A \times C \times T$$
- **T (Time):** Typically 10–30 min per phase; longer for stubborn burnt-on soil.
- **A (Action):** Turbulent flow at 1.5–2.0 m/s in CIP; mechanical brushing in COP (Clean-Out-of-Place).
- **C (Concentration):** 1–3% caustic (NaOH) for general cleaning; 0.5–2% nitric/phosphoric acid for mineral scale.
- **T (Temperature):** 75–85 °C for caustic; 50–70 °C for acid; 80–85 °C for sanitizers.

### 2.4.2 Two-Stage CIP Sequence (Standard for HTST Lines)
1. **Pre-rinse** with warm water (40–45 °C) — removes loosely held milk solids.
2. **Caustic wash** (1.5% NaOH at 75–80 °C for 20 min) — emulsifies fat, hydrolyses protein.
3. **Intermediate rinse** with plain water — flushes alkali.
4. **Acid wash** (1% HNO₃ at 60–65 °C for 15 min) — dissolves mineral (calcium phosphate) scale.
5. **Final rinse** with cold water — flushes acid, cools line.
6. **Sanitization** (hot water 90 °C for 5 min, OR 200 ppm chlorine, OR peracetic acid) — kills surviving microbes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often state "use acid first." Wrong — caustic always **first** (to attack organic soil), acid **second** (to remove mineral scale that caustic leaves behind).

### 2.4.3 Types of Cleaning Systems

| System | Mode | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **CIP (Clean-in-Place)** | Automatic; closed loop; no disassembly | Pipework, PHEs, tanks, fillers |
| **COP (Clean-Out-of-Place)** | Manual disassembly & brush-washing | Fittings, valves, small parts |
| **SIP (Sterilize-in-Place)** | Steam at 121 °C for 15 min | Aseptic lines |

### 2.4.4 Cleaning Agents — Classification

| Class | Function | Example | Typical Concentration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Alkaline** | Saponify fat, hydrolyse protein | NaOH, KOH, sodium silicate | 1–3% |
| **Acidic** | Dissolve mineral scale | HNO₃, H₃PO₄, citric acid | 0.5–2% |
| **Surfactants/wetting** | Reduce surface tension | Non-ionic detergents | 0.1–0.5% |
| **Chelating/sequestering** | Bind Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ | EDTA, polyphosphates | 0.1–0.3% |
| **Sanitizers** | Kill microbes | NaOCl (chlorine), peracetic acid, quaternary ammonium | 100–200 ppm |

---

## 2.5 Can Washing — Engineering Detail

### 2.5.1 Why Cans Need Special Washing
Milk cans have **crevices, seams, handles, and rims** where biofilm forms. Their narrow mouth (typically 18–22 cm) restricts access. Hence, can washers are designed to combine **mechanical impact, hot water, detergent, and steam** in a sequence.

### 2.5.2 Types of Can Washers (Favourite KTU Diagram Question)

| Type | Construction | Flow |
|---|---|---|
| **Rotary (drum-type) Can Washer** | A rotating drum with internal jets; cans tumble | Continuous |
| **Straight-through (in-line) Can Washer** | Cans travel on a conveyor through zones | Continuous |
| **Soak-tank + Jet Washer** | Two-stage: long hot soak, then pressure jet | Semi-continuous |
| **Hand-fed Steam-Jet Washer** | Manual; steam jet + brush | Small-scale |

### 2.5.3 Sequence Inside a Modern Rotary Can Washer
1. **Inverted can entry** on drum.
2. **Pre-rinse** zone (warm water 45 °C) — loosens milk film.
3. **Detergent wash** (caustic solution 70–75 °C) with high-pressure jets.
4. **Hot water rinse** (≥ 82 °C) — rinses alkali.
5. **Steam sterilization** (100 °C steam injection for 30–60 s).
6. **Drying** (hot air blast or hot water draining — most rotary washers rely on residual heat and drainage).
7. **Discharge** can ready for refilling.

### 2.5.4 Critical Parameters (Numericals Appear in KTU)
- **Throughput:** 100–400 cans/hour (rotary); 200–600 cans/hour (in-line).
- **Water consumption:** 2–4 L/can.
- **Steam consumption:** 0.2–0.4 kg/can.
- **Drainage design:** Inverted discharge ensures self-draining — no puddles.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ⭐

> All quantities in **SI units** unless stated. ⚠️ In the table below, vertical bars are rendered as `\vert` to keep the markdown table intact.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat to be removed | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $m$ = kg, $c_p$ = 3.93 kJ/(kg·K) for milk, $\Delta T$ = K | First calculation in any cooler design problem |
| 2 | Log Mean Temperature Difference | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Counter-current cooler analysis | Always positive; use $\ln$ |
| 3 | Heat-exchanger size | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ | $U$ = W/(m²·K), $A$ = m² | Choose cooler area |
| 4 | Overall heat-transfer coefficient | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | $h_i,h_o$ = W/(m²·K); $x$ = m; $k$ = W/(m·K) | Includes fouling; tube wall typically 0.5–0.6 mm |
| 5 | Reynolds number (CIP flow) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v d}{\mu}$ | $\rho$ = kg/m³, $v$ = m/s, $d$ = m, $\mu$ = Pa·s | Must exceed **4000** for turbulent CIP |
| 6 | CIP minimum velocity | $v_{min} \ge 1.5$ m/s | Empirical | Ensures soil removal |
| 7 | Refrigeration effect | $Q_{evap} = \dot{m}_{ref} \cdot (h_g - h_f)$ | kW | Cooling capacity |
| 8 | COP of refrigeration | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{W_{comp}}$ | Dimensionless | Energy efficiency |
| 9 | Time to cool (Newton's law) | $T(t) = T_{env} + (T_0 - T_{env})e^{-t/\tau}$ | $\tau = m c_p / (U A)$ | Can-cooling time |
| 10 | TACT principle | $E = T \cdot A \cdot C \cdot T$ | Dimensionless | Cleaning engineering |
| 11 | Steam consumption per can | $m_{steam} = \dfrac{Q_{steam}}{h_{fg}}$ | $h_{fg} \approx 2257$ kJ/kg at 100 °C | Can-washer design |
| 12 | Ice-Bank latent storage | $Q_{ice} = m_{ice} \cdot L_f$ | $L_f = 334$ kJ/kg | Off-peak cooling storage |
| 13 | Cooling load estimate | $Q = \dot{V} \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$ | $\dot{V}$ = m³/s | Plant sizing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Numerical problems in KTU 2024 typically test **Q1** ($Q = mc_p\Delta T$) and **Q9** (cooling time). Memorize the milk specific heat as **3.93 kJ/(kg·K)** or equivalently **0.93 kcal/(kg·°C)** — *both forms appear in question papers*.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Energy economics:** Cooling accounts for **~40–50%** of a dairy plant's electrical energy — choosing a *counter-current PHE* with *ice-bank pre-cooling* is the most cost-effective configuration.
- **FSSAI compliance:** BIS IS:14653 (1999) and FSSAI mandate ≤4 °C storage — drives cooler specification.
- **Energy efficiency codes:** Indian Ministry of Power's **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme covers dairy refrigeration plants.
- **Sustainability:** Transition to **ammonia (R-717)** and **CO₂ (R-744)** refrigerants is industry-wide because of low Global Warming Potential (GWP ≈ 1 vs. R-22 GWP ≈ 1810).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations Frequently Tested in KTU

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Heat-Load Equation for a Cooler

**Problem (typical KTU Part B 7-marker):**
*A dairy plant receives 5000 L/h of milk at 30 °C from a bulk milk cooler outlet. The milk must be cooled to 4 °C using a counter-flow plate cooler. Calculate (a) the heat load to be removed per hour, and (b) the required cooling-water flow rate if cooling water enters at 1 °C and leaves at 15 °C. Take $c_p$ of milk = 3.93 kJ/(kg·K), density = 1030 kg/m³, and $c_p$ of water = 4.18 kJ/(kg·K).*

#### Step 1 — Convert volumetric flow to mass flow.
$$\dot{V} = 5000 \text{ L/h} = 5 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{milk} = \dot{V} \times \rho = 5 \times 1030 = 5150 \text{ kg/h}$$

*Explanation:* Density of milk is higher than water because of dissolved solids (lactose, protein, fat) and colloidal matter.

#### Step 2 — Compute temperature drop.
$$\Delta T_{milk} = 30 - 4 = 26 \text{ K}$$

#### Step 3 — Apply $Q = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$ to milk side.
$$Q = 5150 \text{ kg/h} \times 3.93 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} \times 26 \text{ K}$$

Let us evaluate the coefficient first:

$$5150 \times 3.93 = 20239.5 \text{ kJ/(h·K)}$$

$$20239.5 \times 26 = 526227 \text{ kJ/h}$$

Convert to kW (1 kW = 1 kJ/s, and 1 h = 3600 s):

$$Q = \frac{526227}{3600} \approx 146.2 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 146.2 \text{ kW} \quad \text{or} \quad 526{,}227 \text{ kJ/h}}$$

#### Step 4 — Compute required cooling-water flow.
Assume no heat loss to surroundings: $Q_{milk,released} = Q_{water,absorbed}$.

$$\Delta T_{water} = 15 - 1 = 14 \text{ K}$$

$$\dot{m}_{water} = \frac{Q}{c_{p,water} \times \Delta T_{water}}$$

$$\dot{m}_{water} = \frac{526227 \text{ kJ/h}}{4.18 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} \times 14 \text{ K}}$$

Compute denominator:

$$4.18 \times 14 = 58.52 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

$$\dot{m}_{water} = \frac{526227}{58.52} \approx 8992.6 \text{ kg/h}$$

Convert to volume:

$$\dot{V}_{water} = \frac{8992.6}{1000} = 8.99 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \approx 9 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{V}_{water} \approx 9 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical interpretation:** To cool 5000 L/h of milk from 30 °C to 4 °C we need ~9000 L/h of chilled water — a **1.8 : 1 water-to-milk ratio**. This is the standard design rule of thumb in dairy pre-coolers.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation of LMTD for Counter-Current Cooler

**Setup:** Milk enters at $T_{h,in} = 72$ °C, leaves at $T_{h,out} = 4$ °C. Chilled water enters at $T_{c,in} = 1$ °C, leaves at $T_{c,out} = 15$ °C.

#### Step 1 — Identify end temperature differences.
For counter-current flow:
$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 72 - 15 = 57 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 4 - 1 = 3 \text{ °C}$$

#### Step 2 — Apply the LMTD formula.
$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{57 - 3}{\ln(57 / 3)} = \frac{54}{\ln(19)}$$

Compute $\ln(19) = 2.944$ (using natural log, base $e$):

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{54}{2.944} \approx 18.34 \text{ °C}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{lm} \approx 18.3 \text{ °C}}$$

#### Step 3 — If asked, compute heat-exchanger area.
Assume $U = 2500$ W/(m²·K) for a sanitary plate cooler.

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{146{,}200 \text{ W}}{2500 \times 18.34}$$

$$A = \frac{146{,}200}{45{,}850} \approx 3.19 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 3.2 \text{ m}^2 \text{ of plate area}}$$

In practice, one would select a PHE with 13–15 plates (each ~0.2 m²) and apply a 1.3 safety factor for fouling — yielding **~4.2 m²** actual.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Cooling Time (Newton's Law of Cooling)

**Setup:** 40 L of milk at 30 °C is placed in a 40 L can; the can is in a BMC with surface heat-transfer coefficient $U A / m c_p = 0.0035$ s⁻¹ (i.e., $\tau = 1/0.0035 \approx 286$ s). Ambient milk-can wall temp = 1 °C. Find time to reach 10 °C.

#### Step 1 — Apply Newton's Law.
$$T(t) = T_{env} + (T_0 - T_{env}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

#### Step 2 — Substitute $T(t) = 10$, $T_0 = 30$, $T_{env} = 1$, $\tau = 286$ s.
$$10 = 1 + (30 - 1) \cdot e^{-t/286}$$

$$9 = 29 \cdot e^{-t/286}$$

$$e^{-t/286} = \frac{9}{29} = 0.3103$$

#### Step 3 — Take natural logarithm.
$$-t/286 = \ln(0.3103) = -1.170$$

$$t = 286 \times 1.170 \approx 334.6 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 335 \text{ s} \approx 5.6 \text{ minutes}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation:** This is *the* reason a BMC with 2-inch insulation and a DX coil can cool 40 L of milk to ≤10 °C in **under 6 minutes** — way faster than the *1-hour rule* mandated at farm level. The rule applies to *uncooled* milk sitting at ambient before chilling infrastructure is reached.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Cooler Selection Decision Tree

Use this when a question gives you a scenario and asks you to *recommend a cooler type*.

| If the scenario is… | Recommend… | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Large HTST plant, 5000 L/h, hygienic | **Counter-current Plate Cooler (PHE)** | Highest $U$, CIP-compatible |
| Viscous/fouling product (cream, ice-cream mix) | **Scraped-Surface Cooler** | Mechanical scraping prevents fouling |
| Village collection centre, 200 L/day, intermittent power | **Ice-Bank Cooler** | Stores cooling energy at night |
| Small on-farm, <500 L/day | **Direct-Expansion BMC** | Simple, no chilled-water loop |
| Batch pasteurizer, 200 L vat | **Jacketed Vessel** | Batch operation suits jacket |
| Tubular shape needed, high pressure | **Tubular Cooler** | Welded, no gaskets |
| Field can chilling (no electricity) | **Surface (can) cooler with well water** | Lowest cost |

---

## 3.3 Numerical Practice Box (Self-Test)

> Work these out by hand before checking Section 3.4.

1. **Q1:** A 10,000 L/h HTST line has milk entering a plate cooler at 72 °C and leaving at 4 °C. Chilled water enters at 1 °C and leaves at 18 °C. Find (a) heat load in kW, (b) chilled water flow in m³/h, (c) LMTD.
2. **Q2:** A BMC has $U A = 80$ W/K, milk mass 200 kg, $c_p = 3.93$ kJ/(kg·K), ambient wall 1 °C. If milk is initially at 30 °C, how long to reach 6 °C? (Hint: $\tau = mc_p/(UA)$.)
3. **Q3:** Steam at 100 °C ($h_{fg}$ = 2257 kJ/kg) is used to sterilize cans. Each can needs 200 kJ of heat. How many cans can be sterilized per kg of steam?

---

## 3.4 Solutions to Numerical Practice Box

#### Q1 Solution
- (a) $\dot{m} = 10{,}000/1000 \times 1030 = 10{,}300$ kg/h; $Q = 10{,}300 \times 3.93 \times (72-4)/3600 = 10{,}300 \times 3.93 \times 68 / 3600 \approx 765.6$ kW.
- (b) $\dot{m}_w = 765.6 \times 3600 / (4.18 \times 17) = 2{,}756{,}160 / 71.06 \approx 38{,}786$ kg/h ≈ 38.8 m³/h.
- (c) $\Delta T_1 = 72 - 18 = 54$; $\Delta T_2 = 4 - 1 = 3$; $\Delta T_{lm} = 51 / \ln(18) = 51 / 2.890 \approx 17.65$ °C.

#### Q2 Solution
- $\tau = 200 \times 3930 / 80 = 9825$ s.
- $T(t) = 1 + 29 e^{-t/9825} = 6 \Rightarrow e^{-t/9825} = 5/29 = 0.1724 \Rightarrow -t/9825 = \ln 0.1724 = -1.758$
- $t = 9825 \times 1.758 \approx 17{,}272$ s ≈ **4.8 hours**.

#### Q3 Solution
- Cans per kg steam = $1 \times 2257 / 200 = 11.28$, i.e., **~11 cans/kg steam**.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Direct-Expansion Bulk Milk Cooler (BMC) Working Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AMBIENT[Ambient Surroundings]
        AIR[Warm ambient air]
    end

    subgraph PLANT[Dairy Plant Building]
        MILK[Hot raw milk 30 C]
        CAN[Insulated milk can or BMC tank]
        COIL[DX evaporator coil on tank wall]
        THERM[Thermostat sensor]
    end

    subgraph REF[Refrigeration Cycle]
        COMP[Compressor]
        COND[Condenser rejects heat to AIR]
        EXP[Expansion valve]
        COIL
    end

    MILK -->|poured in| CAN
    CAN -->|loses heat| COIL
    COIL -->|low pressure vapour| COMP
    COMP -->|high pressure vapour| COND
    COND -->|liquid refrigerant| EXP
    EXP -->|low pressure liquid| COIL
    THERM -->|switches compressor on off| COMP

    style CAN fill:#9467bd,color:#ffffff,stroke:#4a1f6b,stroke-width:2px
    style COIL fill:#17becf,color:#000000,stroke:#0a6e75,stroke-width:2px
    style COMP fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff,stroke:#8b0a0e,stroke-width:2px
    style COND fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#9c4a02,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Engineering story:** The hot milk in the insulated can loses heat to the cold DX coil welded on its wall. The refrigerant absorbs this heat, evaporates, is compressed, rejects heat at the condenser (back to ambient air), condenses, expands, and returns cold — completing the **Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle**.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — The TACT Principle in CIP Cleaning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E[Cleaning Effectiveness E] --> T1[Time T]
    E --> T2[Mechanical Action A]
    E --> T3[Chemical Concentration C]
    E --> T4[Temperature T]

    T1 --> N1[10 to 30 min per phase]
    T2 --> N2[Turbulent flow 1.5 to 2.0 m per second]
    T3 --> N3[1 to 3 percent caustic or 0.5 to 2 percent acid]
    T4 --> N4[75 to 85 C for caustic; 50 to 70 C for acid]

    E --> OUT[IF ANY ONE FACTOR = 0, CLEANING FAILS]
```

> **Conceptual takeaway:** Cleaning is a *product*, not a *sum*. A high concentration with zero time is as useless as zero concentration with high time.

## 4.6 Diagram 6 — Counter-Current vs Co-Current Flow Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CO[Co current Flow]
        direction LR
        CH1[Hot milk 72 C] --> CM1[Cold water enters 1 C] --> EX1[Mixer exit]
    end

    subgraph CC[Counter current Flow KTU recommended]
        direction LR
        CH2[Hot milk 72 C] --> EX2[Mixer exit 4 C milk and 15 C water] --> CW2[Cold water 1 C]
    end

    style CO fill:#ffe4e1,color:#000000,stroke:#cc5a5a,stroke-width:1px
    style CC fill:#e0f7e0,color:#000000,stroke:#2c8a2c,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Why counter-current wins:** In counter-current, the coldest water meets the already-cooled milk (maximising ΔT at the cold end), and the warmest water meets the hottest milk (maximising ΔT at the hot end). The *area-under-curve* in the temperature profile is larger ⇒ greater LMTD ⇒ smaller area needed for the same duty.

> All questions are aligned to **KTU 2024 Scheme — Bloom's Levels 1 to 4**, with simulated past-year tags. Use the model answers and the examiner warnings to sharpen your write-up style.

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any three types of coolers used in a dairy plant and state one specific application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Plate Cooler (PHE):** Used in HTST pasteurizer downstream to cool milk from 72 °C to 4 °C. *(1 mark)*
2. **Direct-Expansion (DX) Bulk Milk Cooler:** Used at village collection centres to chill raw milk from 30 °C to ≤4 °C in 2–3 hours. *(1 mark)*
3. **Ice-Bank Cooler:** Used where power is intermittent; builds ice at night and uses it during the day to pre-cool milk. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *cooler* (heat exchanger) with the *cooling system* (refrigeration plant). Always specify which you are naming.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the TACT principle of cleaning. Why is the cleaning action considered a "product" rather than a "sum" of the four variables?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **TACT principle** states that cleaning effectiveness $E$ is the product of four variables:

$$E = T \times A \times C \times T$$

where **T** = Time, **A** = Mechanical Action, **C** = Chemical Concentration, **T** = Temperature. *(2 marks for stating the equation and expanding all four)*

Because the equation is **multiplicative**, if any one factor becomes zero (e.g., zero chemical, zero temperature, zero time, or no mechanical turbulence), the entire cleaning effectiveness collapses to zero — irrespective of how large the other three factors are. Hence cleaning is a *product*, not a *sum*. *(1 mark for the conceptual explanation)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Writing "sum of T, A, C, T" instead of "product" is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. Memorize the word *product*.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). You are given an *internal choice* — answer either **Question A** or **Question B** below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Describe the construction and working of a counter-current Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE) used as a milk cooler. With a neat diagram, explain why counter-current flow is preferred over co-current flow.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A dairy plant processes 8000 L/h of pasteurized milk at 72 °C in a PHE. The chilled water enters at 2 °C and leaves at 18 °C. Calculate (i) the heat load to be removed per hour in kJ/h and in kW, and (ii) the chilled water flow rate in m³/h. Take $c_p$ of milk = 3.93 kJ/(kg·K) and density of milk = 1030 kg/m³.*

#### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks**

- **Construction (3 marks):**
  - A pack of pressed SS-304/316 stainless-steel plates (each 0.5–0.6 mm thick), suspended on a frame, clamped between a fixed end-plate and a movable pressure plate.
  - Each plate has **four corner ports**; alternating plates are sealed with food-grade EPDM/NBR gaskets that direct milk and water through alternate channels.
  - A typical PHE has 10–100 plates, giving heat-transfer areas of 0.5 m² to 50 m². Plates are corrugated to induce turbulence even at low flow rates.

- **Working (2 marks):**
  - Hot milk enters the **bottom-left port** of the first plate, flows upward through the channel, exits from the top-left port into the next plate (and so on in series).
  - Chilled water enters the **top-right port**, flows **counter to milk** through alternate channels, and exits at the bottom-right.
  - Heat is transferred across the thin plate wall; flow velocities of 0.3–0.6 m/s give high $U$ values (≈ 2500 W/(m²·K)).

- **Counter-current vs Co-current — why counter-current is better (2 marks):**
  - **Co-current** (parallel flow): the cold stream quickly warms up while the hot stream cools down — the temperature difference *vanishes* at the outlet, limiting LMTD.
  - **Counter-current**: the coldest water meets the coolest milk (cold end), and the warmest water meets the hottest milk (hot end) — the temperature difference remains substantial throughout the exchanger, giving a **higher LMTD for the same inlet/outlet temperatures**, hence a smaller area $A$ for the same duty $Q$.
  - In KTU answer scripts, accompany this with a quick **T–Q diagram** showing two rectangles — counter-current fills more area.

#### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Mass flow of milk.** *(1 mark)*
$$\dot{m}_{milk} = \dot{V} \times \rho = 8 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \times 1030 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 8240 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 2 — Temperature drop of milk.** *(0.5 mark)*
$$\Delta T_{milk} = 72 - T_{out, milk}$$

For a *PHE pre-cooler*, the milk is cooled to a value close to the chilled-water outlet; assume milk leaves at ~4 °C (typical for downstream storage).

$$\Delta T_{milk} = 72 - 4 = 68 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Heat load.** *(1.5 marks)*
$$Q = \dot{m}_{milk} \times c_p \times \Delta T = 8240 \times 3.93 \times 68$$
$$Q = 8240 \times 3.93 = 32{,}383.2 \text{ kJ/(h·K)}$$
$$Q = 32{,}383.2 \times 68 = 2{,}202{,}058 \text{ kJ/h}$$

In kW:
$$Q = \frac{2{,}202{,}058}{3600} \approx 611.7 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 2.20 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ/h} \quad \text{or} \quad 611.7 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 4 — Chilled-water flow.** *(2 marks)*
$$\Delta T_{water} = 18 - 2 = 16 \text{ K}$$

By energy balance $Q_{water} = Q_{milk}$:
$$\dot{m}_{water} = \frac{Q}{c_{p,water} \times \Delta T_{water}} = \frac{2{,}202{,}058}{4.18 \times 16} = \frac{2{,}202{,}058}{66.88} \approx 32{,}925 \text{ kg/h}$$

Convert to volume:
$$\dot{V}_{water} = \frac{32{,}925}{1000} = 32.9 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{V}_{water} \approx 32.9 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}}$$

**Step 5 — Brief sanity check and interpretation.** *(2 marks)*
- Water-to-milk volumetric ratio: $32.9 / 8 \approx 4.1$ — a bit high, indicating chilled water at 2 °C is barely adequate; for lower ratio, the chilled water must be colder (e.g., 0 °C using glycol).
- The plant must size the refrigeration plant to deliver **at least 612 kW of cooling** to handle peak summer loads.
- Answer is dimensionally consistent and realistic for a mid-size dairy plant.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the basic principles of cleaning in a dairy plant. Describe the TACT principle and list the standard two-stage CIP sequence with the temperature and chemical used in each step.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Describe, with a labelled diagram, the construction and working of a rotary-type milk-can washer. State any four operating parameters of the can washer.*

#### **Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks**

- **Principles of cleaning (3 marks):** Cleaning must remove three classes of soil from equipment surfaces:
  1. **Organic soil (fat + protein):** removed by **alkaline detergents** (NaOH/KOH) at 75–85 °C. NaOH saponifies fat into soap, hydrolyses protein into soluble peptides.
  2. **Mineral soil (calcium phosphate, milkstone):** removed by **acid detergents** (HNO₃/H₃PO₄) at 50–70 °C. Acid dissolves the mineral scale that alkaline cannot touch.
  3. **Microbial film (biofilm):** removed by **sanitizers** (NaOCl, peracetic acid, quaternary ammonium compounds) OR by hot water at ≥82 °C for ≥5 min.

  Cleaning follows a *logical order*: **organic → mineral → microbial**. The CIP system must also use **food-grade water** (potable, hardness <100 ppm as CaCO₃) and ensure **turbulent flow** ($Re > 4000$).

- **TACT principle (2 marks):** $E = T \times A \times C \times T$ — explained as in Part A Q2. Expanded: Time, Action (mechanical/turbulence), Chemical concentration, Temperature — *all four are multiplicative*.

- **Two-stage CIP sequence (2 marks):** Already tabulated in Section 2.4.2. Write it as a *numbered list* in the answer script:
  1. Pre-rinse (40–45 °C, 5 min)
  2. Caustic wash (1.5% NaOH, 75–80 °C, 20 min)
  3. Intermediate rinse (water, 3 min)
  4. Acid wash (1% HNO₃, 60–65 °C, 15 min)
  5. Final rinse (cold water, 3 min)
  6. Sanitize (90 °C water for 5 min OR 200 ppm chlorine for 10 min)

#### **Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks**

- **Construction (3 marks):** A rotary can washer consists of a long, slightly inclined, slowly rotating **drum (cage)** with internal guide rails. Cans are loaded inverted (mouth down) at one end. Inside the drum are **multiple zones** with high-pressure spray nozzles fed by separate tanks. A steam line enters near the discharge end.
- **Working (2 marks):** As the drum rotates, the cans are gently tumbled through:
  1. Pre-rinse zone,
  2. Caustic-detergent wash zone (70–75 °C),
  3. Hot water rinse zone (≥82 °C),
  4. Steam sterilisation zone (100 °C, 30–60 s),
  5. Drying/holding zone (residual heat drains and dries the can).
  Cans are discharged at the opposite end, ready for refilling.
- **Operating parameters (2 marks):** (any four)
  1. Throughput: 100–400 cans/hour.
  2. Water consumption: 2–4 L/can.
  3. Steam consumption: 0.2–0.4 kg/can.
  4. Drum rotation: 4–8 rpm.
  5. Caustic concentration: 1.5–2.0% NaOH at 70–75 °C.
  6. Hot water temperature: ≥82 °C for ≥2 min contact.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Warnings — Where Students Typically Lose Marks**
> 1. **Skipping the units** in numerical answers → lose 0.5–1 mark per sub-question.
> 2. **Confusing Plate Cooler (PHE) with Plate Pasteurizer** → they are different equipment. PHE is for *cooling*; plate pasteurizer is for *heating*.
> 3. **Writing "first acid, then alkali"** in CIP sequence → reverse order is wrong; lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Omitting the diagram** in a "describe with diagram" 7-marker → lose 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting counter-current flow** in cooler diagrams → lose 1 mark; always label the arrows.
> 6. **Confusing BMC (Bulk Milk Cooler) with BMT (Bulk Milk Tank)** → BMC actively chills; BMT only stores at 4 °C.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> Use this as your **last-night revision sheet** before the KTU exam.

### 🧊 Coolers
- **Plate (PHE) cooler** is the most common in HTST plants → SS-304/316 plates, EPDM gaskets, **counter-current**, $U$ ≈ 2500 W/(m²·K).
- **Bulk Milk Cooler (BMC)** uses direct expansion of refrigerant in a coil welded to the tank wall; capacity 100–5000 L.
- **Ice-Bank Cooler** uses **latent heat of fusion** of water (334 kJ/kg) to store off-peak cooling.
- **Counter-current flow** gives the **highest LMTD** → preferred in *all* cooler designs.
- Key formula: $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$; $c_p$ of milk = **3.93 kJ/(kg·K)**.
- Standard cooler outlet: **≤ 4 °C**; first-hour rule at farm: **≤ 10 °C in 1 h**.

### ❄ Cooling Systems
- **DX system:** refrigerant expands directly in the cooler coil.
- **Chilled-water system:** refrigerant chills water, water cools milk via PHE.
- **Brine system:** CaCl₂/NaCl brine cooled to sub-zero for ice-cream hardening.
- **VCRC** = Compression → Condensation → Expansion → Evaporation.
- $\text{COP} = Q_{evap} / W_{comp}$ — higher COP means more energy-efficient.

### 🧽 Cleaning
- **TACT** = Time × Action × Concentration × Temperature (**product**, not sum).
- **CIP** sequence: **Pre-rinse → Caustic → Rinse → Acid → Rinse → Sanitize.**
- **Caustic** first to attack organic soil (fat/protein) at 75–80 °C; **acid** second to dissolve mineral scale at 60–65 °C; **sanitize** at the end to kill microbes.
- CIP flow velocity **≥ 1.5 m/s** (turbulent, $Re > 4000$).
- Sanitization options: hot water ≥82 °C for 5 min, OR 200 ppm chlorine, OR peracetic acid.

### 🛢 Can Washing
- **Rotary (drum-type) can washer** is the most common continuous washer.
- Zones: pre-rinse → detergent wash → hot rinse → steam sterilize → drain → discharge.
- **Hot water ≥ 82 °C** for 2 min; **steam at 100 °C for 30–60 s** ensures sterilization.
- Throughput: 100–600 cans/h; water 2–4 L/can; steam 0.2–0.4 kg/can.
- **Inverted can entry** ensures self-draining — no puddles harbour bacteria.

### 🧠 Examiner Mnemonics
- **"PHE — Plates, Heat-exchange, Engineered"** for remembering Plate Heat Exchanger.
- **"OAMS"** for CIP chemical order: **O**rganic (caustic) → **A**cid (mineral) → **M**icrobial (sanitize) → **S**tart-up.
- **"CC > PC"** for cooler flow: **C**ounter**C**urrent > Parallel/Co-current.
- **"Cool Cooler, Hot Cooler"** mnemonic for cooler outlet ≤ 4 °C (the "cold" number).

### 🧪 Quick-Fire Formula Recap
$$Q = m c_p \Delta T, \quad \Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}, \quad Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$$
$$E_{CIP} = T \cdot A \cdot C \cdot T, \quad Re = \frac{\rho v d}{\mu} > 4000, \quad \text{COP} = \frac{Q_{evap}}{W_{comp}}$$

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Cool the milk fast, clean the plant harder."* — Cooling preserves quality; cleaning preserves *all* quality. Master both to ace Module 1. 🎯

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Can Washers and Cleaning in Centralised and Decentralized CIP Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT615 - DAIRY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Can Washers and Cleaning in Centralised and Decentralized CIP Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Can Washers — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Can Washer:** A specialized mechanical cleaning device used in dairy plants to wash, rinse, and sanitize empty milk cans, containers, and churns after the contents have been emptied. It is designed to deliver consistent turbulent flow of water, detergent solution, and live steam onto the inner and outer surfaces of the can to achieve a defined level of hygiene before reuse.

According to KTU 2024 Dairy Technology syllabus (Module 1 — Cleaning & Sanitation in Dairy Industry), a **can washer** is a piece of equipment in the *receiving dock / can cleaning area* of a dairy plant whose primary objective is to convert a *soiled empty can* into a *microbiologically clean and dry vessel* ready for refilling or storage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key terms for KTU exams:**
> - **Turbulent flow** in pipe cleaning is defined by **Reynolds number (Re) > 4000**.
> - A "clean surface" in dairy context means: visually clean, free of milk residues, and free from detectable coliforms.
> - The accepted industrial benchmark is **< 10 CFU per 100 cm²** of can interior surface after cleaning.

### 1.2 Types of Can Washers (Syllabus-relevant)

1. **Soak-type Can Washer** — Cans are immersed in a hot detergent bath for a fixed duration.
2. **Spray / Jet-type Can Washer** — High-pressure hot water and detergent jets impinge on the can surfaces.
3. **Rotary (Rotating Brush) Can Washer** — Mechanical brushes rotate inside the can while hot detergent is sprayed.
4. **Combination (Soak + Spray) Can Washer** — Hybrid design used in modern large dairies.
5. **Steam-Jet Can Washer** — Uses **live steam** for sterilization after the wash cycle.

## 1.3 CIP — Cleaned-In-Place Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CIP (Cleaned-In-Place):** A method of cleaning the interior surfaces of pipelines, processing equipment, heat exchangers, tanks, fillers, and other closed processing systems **without dismantling them**, by circulating cleaning solutions through the equipment under controlled conditions of temperature, concentration, time, and flow velocity.

The four pillars of CIP (the **"Sinner’s Circle"** adapted to dairy CIP) are:

| Pillar | Symbol | Typical CIP Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | T | **70 – 80 °C** for caustic, **50 – 60 °C** for acid |
| **Chemical Concentration** | C | **1 – 3 %** NaOH, **0.5 – 1.5 %** HNO₃ |
| **Contact Time** | t | **10 – 30 min** per stage |
| **Mechanical Action (Velocity)** | v | **1.5 – 3.0 m/s** (turbulent) |

## 1.4 Centralized vs Decentralized CIP

> [!NOTE]
> **Centralized CIP** = *One central CIP unit supplies cleaning solutions to multiple processing lines through a ring-main loop.*
>
> **Decentralized CIP** = *Each processing line (or zone) has its own independent CIP skid dedicated only to that line.*

### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Understanding)

> [!TIP]
> **Think of CIP like washing dishes at home:**
> - A **Decentralized CIP** is like having a *small washbasin in every room* — you wash your dishes right where you used them. Quick, but you need many washbasins.
> - A **Centralized CIP** is like having *one large kitchen sink that serves the whole house* — water, soap, and rinse come through pipes to wherever you need them. Economical for large operations, but pipes must be designed carefully.
> - A **Can Washer** is like a *dishwasher machine* — a dedicated appliance specifically designed to clean one type of vessel (the milk can) efficiently and repeatedly.

## 1.6 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Specific heat of water: **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**
- Density of water at 20 °C: **998.2 kg/m³**
- Steam latent heat at 100 °C: **2257 kJ/kg**
- Caustic soda (NaOH) commercial strength: typically **48 – 50 % w/w** liquid.
- Conversion: **1 % solution = 10 g NaOH per litre of water.**
- Recommended hot water usage in can washing: **2 – 3 litres per 40-litre can.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sinner’s Circle Applied to Dairy Cleaning

The **Sinner’s Circle** states that cleaning effectiveness is the product of four interacting factors. If one factor is reduced, another must be increased to maintain the same cleaning result.

$$\text{Cleaning Effect} = f(T, C, t, M)$$

where **M = Mechanical action** (flow velocity, pressure, turbulence).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When answering "What is Sinner’s Circle?" — always mention all four parameters and add the line *"Reducing one variable must be compensated by increasing the others."*

## 2.2 Stages of a Standard CIP Cycle (Memorize for KTU)

| Stage | Solution | Temp (°C) | Time (min) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. **Pre-rinse** | Plain water | 40 – 50 | 5 – 10 | Remove loose milk solids |
| 2. **Caustic Wash** | 1 – 3 % NaOH | **75 ± 5** | 15 – 30 | Remove fat & protein |
| 3. **Intermediate Rinse** | Plain water | 40 – 50 | 5 – 10 | Flush out alkali |
| 4. **Acid Wash** | 0.5 – 1.5 % HNO₃ | **55 ± 5** | 10 – 20 | Remove mineral (milk stone) |
| 5. **Final Rinse** | Plain water (or hot) | 80 – 90 | 5 – 10 | Remove acid traces |
| 6. **Sanitize** | Hot water / chemical | 85 – 90 | 5 – 10 | Reduce microbial load |

## 2.3 CIP Configuration — Centralized System

A **centralized CIP** is built around a **ring main** that loops through the dairy.

**Key components:**

1. **CIP Supply Tank** — Concentrate of cleaning chemicals.
2. **Dilution Tank** — Where concentrate is mixed with water to working strength.
3. **Heater** — Plate or tubular heat exchanger (steam-heated).
4. **CIP Supply Pump** — Centrifugal or positive-displacement, **316 L stainless steel**.
5. **Return Line** — Returns spent solution to the recovery tank.
6. **Plate Heat Exchanger** — Heats CIP solution using steam.
7. **Conductivity Sensor** — Monitors chemical concentration.
8. **Flow Meter** — Monitors velocity (to ensure turbulence).
9. **Temperature Sensor (PT-100)** — Monitors each stage.
10. **Air-Operated Valves** — Three-way valves for routing.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point:** Centralized CIP is preferred for plants with **> 3 processing lines** because of economy of chemicals and labor.

## 2.4 CIP Configuration — Decentralized System

Each processing line has its own **CIP skid** mounted close to the equipment. Solutions are **not** shared between lines.

**Advantages over Centralized:**
- No cross-contamination between product lines.
- No long return loops, hence **less solution wastage**.
- Quick commissioning — no need to fill long return lines.

**Disadvantages:**
- Higher capital cost (multiple skids).
- Higher chemical inventory at multiple points.
- More floor space occupied.

## 2.5 Can Washer — Operating Principles

A can washer works on the principle of **hydraulic and thermal mechanical action** combined with chemical detergency.

$$\text{Mechanical Impact} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v^2$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of water (≈ 1000 kg/m³)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of jet (m²)
- $v$ = jet velocity (m/s)

Higher jet velocity → higher impact force → better soil removal.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum set of formulas** that a KTU student must be able to write from memory for Module 1.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (turbulence check) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{v \cdot D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number (using kinematic viscosity) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Volumetric flow rate for turbulent CIP | m³/s |
| 4 | $v_{\min} = \dfrac{4000 \cdot \nu}{D}$ | Minimum velocity for turbulent flow (Re = 4000) | m/s |
| 5 | $m_{\text{NaOH}} = V \cdot C \cdot 10$ | Mass of NaOH for %w/v solution | g (if V in L) |
| 6 | $M_{\text{stock}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{working}} \cdot V_{\text{tank}}}{C_{\text{stock}}}$ | Volume of stock liquid required | L |
