2. **Raw milk pump** — positive-displacement (timing-screw) type to ensure constant flow.
3. **Plate heat exchanger (regeneration section)** — preheats incoming cold milk using the outgoing hot pasteurized milk (heat recovery of 80–90%).
4. **Plate heat exchanger (heating section)** — final heating to 72 °C using hot water.
5. **Filter/strainer** — removes any coagulated protein particles.
6. **Holding tube** — provides the 15-s residence at 72 °C.
7. **Flow diversion valve (FDV)** — three-way valve that sends inadequately heated milk back to the raw tank.
8. **Plate heat exchanger (regeneration cooling)** — pre-cools pasteurized milk while pre-heating raw milk.
9. **Plate heat exchanger (chilled water cooling)** — final cooling to ≤ 4 °C.
10. **Pasteurized milk storage tank** — for downstream packaging.

**Function of FDV (3 marks):**
The **Flow Diversion Valve (FDV)** is a **three-way, pneumatically actuated valve** located at the **end of the holding tube**. Its functions are:

- **Position 1 (Forward):** When the milk temperature (and integrated $F_o$) is **≥ the setpoint** (72 °C or $F_o \geq 1$), the FDV routes milk to the cooling section and onward to packaging.
- **Position 2 (Divert):** When the temperature falls **below the setpoint** (e.g., during plant start-up, a hot-water failure, or a fouled plate), the FDV **diverts the milk back to the raw balance tank** for re-pasteurization.

The FDV is the **single most critical safety device** in an HTST plant, ensuring that **no sub-pasteurized milk ever reaches the packaging line**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting units of D-value and Z-value** — $D_T$ is in **seconds or minutes** (time), $Z$ is in **°C** (temperature). Do not mix them up.
> 2. **Reporting $F_o$ without interpretation** — A bare number is insufficient. Always conclude with: *"$F_o \geq 1$, hence pasteurization is adequate."*
> 3. **Confusing the reference temperature** — The reference for HTST is **72 °C** (not 63 °C). LTLT uses 63 °C.
> 4. **Using $N/N_0$ instead of $N_0/N$ in the log-reduction equation** — This inverts the sign. Always state: *"log kill = $\log_{10}(N_0/N)$"*.
> 5. **Forgetting the safety margin** — In real plants, $F_o$ is required to be $\geq 1$ **continuously**, not just at one instant. Mention the **integrated** nature of $F_o$ in descriptive answers.
> 6. **Misidentifying *Coxiella burnetii*** — It is the **most heat-resistant** vegetative pathogen and the **benchmark** for HTST design, *not* *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* (which is the classical but less resistant organism).
> 7. **Skipping the phosphatase test explanation** — A KTU favorite. Always mention that phosphatase is **slightly more heat-resistant** than *Coxiella burnetii*, hence its inactivation is a **safety indicator**.
> 8. **Diagrams without arrows or labels** — Mermaid/hand-drawn diagrams must include **flow direction arrows** and **section labels** (regeneration, heating, holding, cooling) for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pasteurization — Principles: 1-Page Rapid Revision**

### Core Definitions
- ✅ **Pasteurization** = killing pathogens while preserving quality.
- ✅ **LTLT** = 63 °C / 30 min (vat, batch).
- ✅ **HTST** = 72 °C / 15 s (continuous, plate).
- ✅ **HHST** = 89 °C / 1 s; **UHT** = 135–150 °C / 2–4 s.
- ✅ **Benchmark organism** = *Coxiella burnetii* (most heat-resistant vegetative pathogen).
- ✅ **Adequate condition** = $F_o \geq 1$.

### Key Formulas
- ✅ $N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-t/D_T}$ (survivor curve).
- ✅ $t = n \cdot D_T$ (time for n-log kill).
- ✅ $\log_{10}(D_{T_1}/D_{T_2}) = (T_2 - T_1)/Z$ (Z-value relation).
- ✅ $L(t) = 10^{(T(t) - T_{ref})/Z}$ (instantaneous lethality).
- ✅ $F_o = \int 10^{(T(t) - T_{ref})/Z} \, dt$ (integrated lethality).
- ✅ $F_o \geq 1 \Rightarrow$ adequate pasteurization.

### Critical Numbers (Memorize)
- ✅ $D_{72}$ of *Coxiella burnetii* ≈ 3 s.
- ✅ Z-value for *Coxiella burnetii* ≈ 6.94 °C (or 7 °C rounded).
- ✅ 5-log reduction = 99.999% kill.
- ✅ A 1 °C rise above 72 °C reduces required time by ~26% (factor $10^{-1/7} = 0.74$).
- ✅ 3 °C rise (72 → 75 °C) reduces required time by ~63% (factor $10^{-3/7} = 0.373$).

### Biochemical Indicators
- ✅ **Alkaline phosphatase test** = negative ⇒ pasteurization adequate.
- ✅ **Lactoperoxidase test** = negative ⇒ over-pasteurization.
- ✅ Phosphatase is **more heat-resistant** than *Coxiella burnetii*.

### Plant Components (HTST)
- ✅ Balance tank → Pump → Regenerator → Heater → Filter → Holding tube → **FDV** → Regenerator cooler → Chilled water cooler → Storage.
- ✅ **FDV** is the most critical safety device.

### Common Exam Traps
- ✅ Reference temperature for HTST is **72 °C**, not 63 °C.
- ✅ $F_o$ is **integrated**, not instantaneous.
- ✅ Time-temperature combinations are **equivalent** if $F_o$ is the same.
- ✅ UHT is **sterilization**, not pasteurization (kills spores too).

### One-Line Exam Statements (Use Verbatim)
- ✅ *"Pasteurization is a science of balancing microbial lethality with nutrient preservation."*
- ✅ *"The Z-value of *Coxiella burnetii* is approximately 7 °C."*
- ✅ *"The phosphatase test serves as the biological safety indicator of adequate pasteurization."*
- ✅ *"Adequate pasteurization is achieved when $F_o \geq 1$."*

---

**End of Notes — Pasteurization: Principles | OEFTT615 Dairy Technology | Module 2**

topics.title: Objectives and Methods – LTLT/Holding Pasteurization – Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT615 - DAIRY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Objectives and Methods – LTLT/Holding Pasteurization – Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pasteurization** is defined as the process of heating every particle of milk or milk product to a specific temperature for a definite period of time, followed by immediate cooling, with the primary objective of destroying pathogenic (disease-causing) microorganisms and significantly reducing the total viable microbial load, while causing minimal chemical, physical, nutritional, or sensory changes in the product.

In the context of **LTLT (Low Temperature, Long Time)** or **Holding Pasteurization**, the standard regulatory time-temperature combination mandated by the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)** and aligned with the **Pasteurized Milk Ordinance (PMO)** is:

$$\boxed{T = 63\,^\circ\text{C} \quad \text{for} \quad t = 30\,\text{minutes}}$$

The term *"Holding"* arises because the milk is held/retarded in a vat or chamber for a precisely controlled residence time to ensure uniform heat penetration throughout the bulk fluid.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Thermos Flask Principle":**
> Imagine boiling water in a kettle to kill germs — you need it bubbling hot (high temperature) for only seconds (short time). Alternatively, you could keep warm water in a **thermos flask at 63°C** for 30 minutes, and the germs would still die, just more gently. LTLT is exactly this "thermos flask" idea: lower heat, but held longer so that heat has time to penetrate every drop uniformly without cooking the milk's delicate proteins or destroying its vitamins.

The dairy industry calls this a **"batch" or "vat" process** because milk is treated in discrete batches rather than flowing continuously — much like how a chef prepares one pot of soup at a time rather than running a continuous pipeline.

---

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Standard Time–Temperature Combination** | **63°C × 30 min** |
| **Minimum Log Reduction (Pathogens)** | **≥ 5 log cycles** of *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* and *Coxiella burnetii* |
| **Residence Time Tolerance** | **± 1 second** in HTST; ± 30 s in LTLT |
| **Phosphatase Test Result** | **Negative** (proves proper pasteurization) |
| **Peroxidase Test Result** | **Positive** (proves under-pasteurization avoided) |
| **Microbial Count After Process** | **< 10,000 CFU/mL** (Grade A standard) |

---

### 1.4 Primary Objectives of Pasteurization

1. **Destruction of Pathogens** — Eliminates *M. tuberculosis*, *Brucella abortus*, *Salmonella spp.*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, and *E. coli*.
2. **Reduction of Spoilage Microbes** — Extends shelf-life from **2 days (raw milk)** to **7–14 days (pasteurized, refrigerated)**.
3. **Inactivation of Native Enzymes** — Particularly **alkaline phosphatase** (used as an indicator of successful pasteurization).
4. **Preservation of Nutritional Quality** — Vitamins (A, D, B₂, B₁₂) and proteins remain largely intact.
5. **Standardization of Microbial Quality** — Provides a uniformly safe product for downstream processing (cheese, yogurt).
6. **Compliance with Public Health Regulations** — Mandatory under **FSSAI (2011) & PMO** for liquid milk intended for direct consumption.

---

### 1.5 Classification of Pasteurization Methods

The dairy industry classifies pasteurization based on the **time–temperature equivalence** of the thermal process. All combinations are designed to deliver the same bactericidal effect (F₀ value equivalent).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fundamental principle is **thermal equivalence** — high heat for short time vs. low heat for long time achieves identical microbial destruction. This is governed by the **$D$-value** and **$z$-value** of target microorganisms.

| Method | Temperature | Time | Acronym | Mode |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Vat Pasteurization** | **63°C** | **30 min** | LTLT | Batch |
| **High-Temp Short-Time** | **72°C** | **15 sec** | HTST | Continuous |
| **Higher-Heat Shorter-Time** | **89°C** | **1.0 sec** | HHST | Continuous |
| **Ultra-High Temperature** | **135–150°C** | **2–6 sec** | UHT | Continuous/Aseptic |
| **Thermization** | **57–68°C** | **15–20 sec** | — | Sub-pasteurization |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why 63°C × 30 min?

The time-temperature combination was established empirically to achieve a **5-log reduction** of the most heat-resistant non-sporing pathogen relevant to milk: ***Mycobacterium tuberculosis*** and ***Coxiella burnetii***.

The underlying microbiological physics is captured by two parameters:

- **$D_T$ (Decimal Reduction Time):** Time required at temperature $T$ to destroy **90%** (1-log) of a microbial population.
- **$z$ (z-value):** Temperature increase (°C) required to reduce $D_T$ by a factor of 10.

For ***Coxiella burnetii*** (the reference pathogen):
- $D_{63} \approx 6.0$ minutes
- $z \approx 4.5$ °C

To achieve a **5-log reduction** at 63°C:

$$ t = 5 \times D_{63} = 5 \times 6.0 = 30\,\text{minutes} $$

This precisely justifies the **30-minute holding time** at 63°C.

---

### 2.2 Operational Sequence of LTLT Pasteurization

The classical LTLT process follows a **5-stage thermal cycle**:

1. **Pre-Heating (Optional, 40–45°C):** For efficient vat filling and to avoid thermal shock.
2. **Heating Phase:** Vat heating jacket raises bulk milk temperature from 4–5°C to 63°C.
3. **Holding Phase:** Milk maintained at 63°C ± 0.5°C for **exactly 30 minutes** (with continuous gentle agitation).
4. **Cooling Phase:** Hot water in jacket replaced by chilled water → milk cooled to **≤ 5°C**.
5. **Storage Phase:** Pasteurized milk held at **4–5°C** until packaging.

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Foaming" Rule** (FDA PMO §15p): If the milk surface foams above the heating coil, the foam must not be considered effectively pasteurized — foam must be **< 5 cm** and submerged below the heating surface.

---

### 2.3 Enzyme Indicators of Proper Pasteurization

| Enzyme | Source | Behaviour | Indicator Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Alkaline Phosphatase** | Native milk enzyme | Inactivated at **63°C × 30 min** | **Positive test = UNDER-pasteurized** |
| **Peroxidase** | Native milk enzyme | Destroyed at **> 75°C** | **Negative test = OVER-pasteurized** |
| **Lactoperoxidase** | Native milk enzyme | Destroyed at **> 78°C** | Indicator of severe overheating |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A correctly pasteurized milk must show **Phosphatase-NEGATIVE** and **Peroxidase-POSITIVE** simultaneously. This is a favourite 3-mark question in KTU ESE exams.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Log microbial reduction | $n = \log_{10}(N_0 / N_f)$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Time for *n*-log kill | $t = n \times D_T$ | minutes or seconds |
| 3 | z-value relation | $\log(D_2/D_1) = (T_1 - T_2)/z$ | temperature in °C |
| 4 | Decimal reduction time | $D_T = D_{ref} \cdot 10^{(T_{ref}-T)/z}$ | minutes |
| 5 | Pasteurization equivalent | $F_0 = \int 10^{(T-T_{ref})/z}\,dt$ | minutes at 63°C |
| 6 | LTLT target lethality | $F_0 \geq 30$ min | equivalent at 63°C |
| 7 | Heat transfer (vat jacket) | $Q = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kJ |
| 8 | Residence time (LTLT) | $\tau = V / \dot{V}$ | seconds |
| 9 | Phosphatase negative | $T \cdot t \geq$ lethal threshold | confirms process |
| 10 | Cream layer rise (gravity) | $v = \frac{2}{9} \cdot \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_f)}{\mu} \cdot g \cdot r^2$ | Stokes' law, m/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are foundational for designing pasteurizer capacity, validating thermal processes during HACCP audits, and troubleshooting under-processed batches in dairy plants.

---

### 2.5 The Three Pillars of LTLT Design

A correctly engineered LTLT system must satisfy:

1. **Thermal Equivalence:** Every particle must reach ≥ 63°C for ≥ 30 min.
2. **Mechanical Uniformity:** Agitation prevents stratification of fat globules and ensures bulk homogeneity.
3. **Sanitary Design (3-A Standards):** All contact surfaces are **304/316 stainless steel**, polished to **Ra ≤ 0.8 μm**, with **≥ 3% slope** for complete drainage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why 30 minutes? From $D$-value to Process Time

We want to demonstrate mathematically why **63°C × 30 min** was chosen as the LTLT standard, using the kinetic framework of microbial inactivation.

---

#### Step 1 — Define the microbial inactivation kinetics

Microbial destruction under isothermal conditions follows **first-order kinetics**:

$$ \frac{dN}{dt} = -k \cdot N $$

where $N$ = number of viable cells at time $t$, and $k$ = specific death rate constant (min⁻¹).

Integrating from $N_0$ at $t=0$ to $N_f$ at $t=t$:

$$ N_f = N_0 \cdot e^{-k t} $$

---

#### Step 2 — Relate $k$ to $D_T$

By definition of $D_T$, a 1-log (90%) reduction occurs in time $D_T$:

$$ \frac{N_0}{10} = N_0 \cdot e^{-k D_T} $$

$$ \Rightarrow \ln(10) = k \cdot D_T \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{2.303}{D_T} $$

---

#### Step 3 — Express time for *n*-log reduction

Setting $N_f / N_0 = 10^{-n}$:

$$ 10^{-n} = e^{-k t} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{2.303 \cdot n}{k} = n \cdot D_T $$

Thus, for $n = 5$ log reduction (pasteurization standard):

$$ t = 5 \times D_{63} $$

For *Coxiella burnetii*, $D_{63} = 6$ min:

$$ t = 5 \times 6 = 30 \text{ minutes} \;\;\checkmark $$

---

#### Step 4 — Verify equivalence with HTST (72°C × 15 s)

Using the z-value relation:

$$ \frac{D_{72}}{D_{63}} = 10^{(63-72)/z} = 10^{-9/4.5} \approx 0.0063 $$

$$ D_{72} = 6.0 \times 0.0063 = 0.038\,\text{min} \approx 2.3\,\text{seconds} $$

For 5-log kill: $t = 5 \times 2.3 = 11.5 \text{ s} \approx 15 \text{ s (safety margin)}$ ✓

---

#### Step 5 — Energy Balance for LTLT Vat Heating

To heat 1000 L of milk from 5°C to 63°C:

$$ Q = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T $$

$$ m = \rho \cdot V = 1030\,\text{kg/m}^3 \times 1.0\,\text{m}^3 = 1030\,\text{kg} $$

$$ C_p (\text{milk}) = 3.93\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)} $$

$$ \Delta T = 63 - 5 = 58\,\text{K} $$

$$ Q = 1030 \times 3.93 \times 58 = 234{,}838\,\text{kJ} \approx 65.2\,\text{kWh} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This energy requirement (≈65 kWh per 1000 L) explains why HTST is more energy-efficient at scale, but LTLT remains preferred for **artisanal cheeses** and **craft dairy** due to gentler thermal impact on casein micelles.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Process Variables & Their Effects

| Variable | Increase Effect on Pathogen Kill | Increase Effect on Milk Quality | Engineering Control |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Temperature** | ↑ Log reduction (exponential) | ↑ Whey protein denaturation | PID controller ± 0.5°C |
| **Hold Time** | ↑ Log reduction (linear) | ↑ Lysine loss, cooked flavour | Timer relay ± 30 s |
| **Agitation Speed** | ↑ Uniformity | ↑ Fat globule disruption | 30–60 rpm paddle |
| **Foam Height** | ↓ Effective kill | ↓ Cream line | Anti-foam sensor |
| **Pre-heat** | None | Mild lactulose formation | Plate pre-heater |
| **Cooling Rate** | None | ↓ Psychrotrophic growth | Chilled water ≤ 4°C |

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A dairy plant pasteurizes 500 kg of milk in an LTLT vat. Initial microbial load is $5 \times 10^5$ CFU/mL. $D_{63}$ for the target pathogen is 6 minutes. Calculate:
(a) Holding time needed for 5-log reduction.
(b) Number of CFU/mL after pasteurization.
(c) Heat energy required to raise milk from 6°C to 63°C.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Holding time:

$$ t = n \times D_{63} = 5 \times 6 = 30\,\text{min} $$

**(b)** Survivors:

$$ N_f = N_0 \times 10^{-n} = 5 \times 10^5 \times 10^{-5} = 50\,\text{CFU/mL} $$

**(c)** Energy:

$$ Q = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = 500 \times 3.93 \times 57 = 112{,}005\,\text{kJ} $$

$$ Q = \frac{112{,}005}{3600} = 31.1\,\text{kWh} $$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define pasteurization. List any four objectives of pasteurization.**

**Model Answer:**
Pasteurization is the process of heating every particle of milk to a specific temperature for a definite period of time, followed by immediate cooling, to destroy pathogenic microorganisms while minimizing chemical/nutritional changes.

**Four objectives:**
1. Destruction of pathogenic bacteria (e.g., *M. tuberculosis*, *Brucella*).
2. Reduction of total viable microbial count → extended shelf-life.
3. Inactivation of native enzymes (e.g., alkaline phosphatase).
4. Preservation of nutritional and sensory quality of milk.

---

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between the Phosphatase Test and the Peroxidase Test with respect to their use in pasteurization.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Phosphatase Test | Peroxidase Test |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Enzyme | Alkaline phosphatase | Lactoperoxidase |
| Inactivation temperature | **63°C × 30 min** | **> 75°C** |
| Positive test indicates | **Under-pasteurization** | **Correct pasteurization** |
| Negative test indicates | **Correct pasteurization** | **Over-pasteurization** |
| Use | Safety verification | Quality verification |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **LTLT (Holding) Pasteurization** process in detail, including the standard time-temperature combination, equipment design, and operational sequence. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the various **types of LTLT/Holding pasteurizers** with their features and applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution A(a):

LTLT or **Low Temperature Long Time** pasteurization is the classical batch process standardized at **63°C for 30 minutes**, also called **"Vat Pasteurization"** because of the equipment used.

**Equipment Design (Vat Pasteurizer):**
- Constructed of **stainless steel (SS 304)**, double-walled with a steam/water jacket.
- Inner surface polished to **Ra ≤ 0.8 μm** (3-A sanitary standard).
- Equipped with **paddle agitator** (30–60 rpm), **precision thermometer**, **timing device**, and **outlet valve**.
- Holds typically **500–2000 L** per batch.
- Sloped bottom (≥ 3%) for complete drainage.
- Foaming restriction — milk surface must remain below heating coils.

**Operational Sequence (5 steps):**

1. **Filling:** Cold raw milk (4–5°C) is pumped into the vat up to a preset level.
2. **Heating:** Steam in jacket raises milk temperature to **63°C ± 0.5°C** over 25–35 minutes.
3. **Holding:** Milk held at **63°C for exactly 30 minutes** with continuous gentle agitation to prevent fat stratification and ensure temperature uniformity.
4. **Cooling:** Steam replaced by chilled water in jacket; milk cooled to **≤ 5°C** within 30 min.
5. **Discharge & Storage:** Milk discharged to insulated storage tank, awaiting packaging at 4–5°C.

**Validation Tests:**
- **Phosphatase Test:** Must be **negative** (correct pasteurization achieved).
- **Peroxidase Test:** Must be **positive** (no over-processing).

**Advantages:** Simple, low capital cost, ideal for craft/artisanal dairy, preserves casein structure for cheesemaking.

**Disadvantages:** Batch operation, high energy consumption, labour-intensive, not scalable beyond ~5000 L/hr.

---

#### Model Solution A(b):

The main **types of LTLT/Holding pasteurizers** are:

**1. Open Vat Pasteurizer:**
- Oldest design; milk surface exposed to atmosphere.
- Slow heating using steam or hot water jacket.
- Used in small farms; **not FSSAI-compliant** for commercial sale in India.

**2. Enclosed Vat Pasteurizer:**
- Fully sealed, sanitary, FDA/FSSAI-compliant.
- Closed-circuit with CIP (clean-in-place) capability.
- Standard for modern small dairies.

**3. Double-Jacket Vat with Agitator:**
- Jacketed both on sides and bottom for uniform heating.
- Motor-driven slow-speed paddle prevents scorching.
- Most common industrial LTLT unit.

**4. Continuous Holding Tube Type (Pseudo-LTLT):**
- Milk heated in a plate heat exchanger to 63°C, then passed through a **long holding tube** sized for 30-minute residence.
- Combines batch lethality with continuous flow advantages.
- Examples: **Stork Sterideal** (75°C × 15 s) and **Stork Thermodare**.

**5. High-Heat Short Hold (HHSH) — 73–75°C × 5 min:**
- Intermediate between LTLT and HTST.
- Used specifically for **cheese milk** to inactivate unwanted lactic cultures.

**6. Regenerative Vat Pasteurizer:**
- Uses hot outgoing milk to pre-warm incoming cold milk.
- Reduces energy consumption by **40–50%**.

**Comparison Summary:**

| Type | Capacity | Cost | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Open Vat | Very Low | Lowest | Farmhouse |
| Enclosed Vat | Low–Medium | Medium | Small Dairy |
| Regenerative Vat | Medium | High | Mid-scale Plant |
| Holding Tube (Pseudo-LTLT) | High | Highest | Large Plant |

---

#### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **objectives and methods of pasteurization** of milk with reference to microbial destruction kinetics. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the holding time for LTLT pasteurization using the $D$-value concept and explain its equivalence to HTST using the $z$-value. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution B(a):

**Objectives of Pasteurization:**
1. Pathogen destruction (5-log reduction of *M. tuberculosis*, *Coxiella burnetii*).
2. Shelf-life extension (raw 2 days → pasteurized 14 days).
3. Enzyme inactivation (phosphatase).
4. Nutritional & sensory preservation.
5. Regulatory compliance (FSSAI 2011).
6. Standardization for downstream processing.

**Methods of Pasteurization:**

| Method | T (°C) | Time | Mode |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **LTLT (Vat)** | **63** | **30 min** | Batch |
| **HTST** | **72** | **15 s** | Continuous |
| **HHST** | **89** | **1 s** | Continuous |
| **UHT** | **135–150** | **2–6 s** | Aseptic |

**Microbial Destruction Kinetics:**
First-order kinetics: $N_f = N_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$, with $k = \ln(10)/D_T$. The $D$-value depends on temperature via the $z$-value: $D_T = D_{ref} \cdot 10^{(T_{ref} - T)/z}$.

For reference pathogen *C. burnetii*: $D_{63} = 6$ min, $z = 4.5°C$.

---

#### Model Solution B(b):

**Derivation:**

The first-order destruction equation:

$$ \frac{dN}{dt} = -k N \;\Rightarrow\; N_f = N_0 \cdot e^{-k t} $$

For 5-log reduction ($N_f / N_0 = 10^{-5}$):

$$ 10^{-5} = e^{-k t} \;\Rightarrow\; t = \frac{5 \ln 10}{k} $$

With $k = \ln 10 / D_{63} = \ln 10 / 6$:

$$ t = 5 \times 6 = 30\,\text{minutes} $$

**Equivalence to HTST:**

$$ D_{72} = D_{63} \cdot 10^{(63-72)/z} = 6 \times 10^{-9/4.5} \approx 0.038\,\text{min} $$

$$ t_{HTST} = 5 \times 0.038 \times 60 = 11.4\,\text{s} \approx 15\,\text{s (with safety margin)} $$

Therefore both treatments deliver the **same 5-log pathogen reduction** — confirming **thermal equivalence** of LTLT and HTST processes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing LTLT with UHT** — UHT is 135°C/2–6 s, NOT LTLT. Marks lost for this.
> 2. **Stating time–temperature incorrectly** — Always write **"63°C for 30 minutes"**, not "63 seconds".
> 3. **Forgetting foam rule** — In drawings, foam must be below heating coils (FDA PMO requirement).
> 4. **Confusing phosphatase vs peroxidase meanings** — Positive phosphatase = FAIL; Positive peroxidase = PASS.
> 5. **Missing units** in numerical answers — Always specify **min vs s**, °C vs K, CFU/mL.
> 6. **Skipping derivation steps** — Examiner allocates 2–3 marks purely for the derivation steps.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — LTLT/Holding Pasteurization**

- ✅ Pasteurization = **heat every particle** to destroy pathogens + reduce microbes.
- ✅ LTLT standard = **63°C × 30 minutes**.
- ✅ Reference pathogen for design = ***Coxiella burnetii*** (most heat-resistant non-sporing).
- ✅ Required log reduction = **5-log** (10⁵ reduction).
- ✅ **Phosphatase negative** = correct pasteurization.
- ✅ **Peroxidase positive** = no over-processing.
- ✅ $D_{63}$ for *C. burnetii* = **6 minutes** → $5 \times 6 = 30$ min.
- ✅ $z$-value for *C. burnetii* ≈ **4.5°C**.
- ✅ LTLT is **batch (vat)**, HTST is **continuous (plate)**.
- ✅ LTLT advantages: simple, gentle on casein, ideal for cheese.
- ✅ LTLT disadvantages: high energy, labour-intensive, low throughput.
- ✅ **3-A sanitary standards** mandatory for contact surfaces.
- ✅ Foam height restriction: **< 5 cm** below heating coils.
- ✅ Cooling endpoint: **≤ 5°C** within 30 minutes post-hold.
- ✅ FSSAI mandates pasteurization for all market milk in India.
- ✅ Thermal equivalence: HTST (72°C × 15s) ≡ LTLT (63°C × 30 min) in lethality.
- ✅ First-order kinetics governs microbial inactivation: $N_f = N_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$.
- ✅ Time for *n*-log kill: $t = n \times D_T$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Disadvantages – HTST Pasteurization – Components and Function of HTST Pasteurizer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT615 - DAIRY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advantages and Disadvantages – HTST Pasteurization – Components and Function of HTST Pasteurizer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is HTST Pasteurization?

**HTST (High Temperature — Short Time) Pasteurization** is a continuous thermal processing technique defined under the **Pasteurized Milk Ordinance (PMO)** standards. The codified legal condition is heating every particle of milk to **not less than 72 °C (161 °F)** and holding it continuously at that temperature for **not less than 15 seconds**, followed by immediate cooling to **4 °C or below**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legal Definition (PMO / FSSAI Aligned):**
> "Every particle of milk shall be heated to one of the specified temperatures and held continuously at or above that temperature for the specified time."
> — *Grade A Pasteurized Milk Ordinance, USFDA / FSSAI Dairy Regulation equivalent*

It is also commonly called **"Flash Pasteurization"** or **"Continuous Pasteurization"** because the milk moves as a continuous stream through the heat exchange sections at high velocity.

### Classification of Pasteurization Methods

| Method | Temperature | Time | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LTLT** (Low Temp — Long Time / Batch) | 63 °C | 30 min | Batch |
| **HTST** (High Temp — Short Time) | 72 °C | 15 sec | Continuous |
| **HHST** (Higher Heat — Shorter Time) | 89 °C | 1.0 sec | Continuous |
| **UHT** (Ultra High Temperature) | 138 °C | 2 sec | Aseptic |

> [!NOTE]
> HTST is the **most widely adopted industrial pasteurization method** in India and globally for fluid market milk. Kerala Co-operative Milk Marketing Federation (KCMMF / Milma) plants predominantly run HTST lines.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea Strain Pre-Warming" Intuition

Imagine making tea for 100 guests at a wedding:

- **Batch pasteurization** = Boil water in a big vessel on a stove, keep it boiling for 30 minutes, then pour. Energy is wasted, the milk at the bottom scalds, and waiting time is huge.
- **HTST pasteurization** = Hot water flows continuously inside a long insulated pipe (like a *chappa* / *kuzhi*) with milk on the other side of the wall. Milk enters cold, picks up heat from the outgoing hot milk, gets a final top-up from steam, passes through a fixed length pipe in exactly 15 seconds, then is instantly cooled by the very same incoming cold milk. **Efficiency sky-rockets.**

This is the genius of HTST — milk serves as its own "pre-heater" and "pre-cooler" through a **regenerative heat exchanger**, achieving energy savings of up to **85–90 %** versus batch systems.

## 1.3 Why HTST Exists — The Engineering Motivation

The concept rests on the principle of **bacterial death kinetics**:

$$\text{Total Lethality} \;=\; \int_{0}^{t} 10^{(T(t) - T_{\text{ref}})/z} \, dt$$

where microbial death follows **logarithmic destruction** with respect to time. By raising temperature sharply, the destruction rate constant (D-value) plummets — so we can destroy *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* and *Coxiella burnetii* in **15 seconds** instead of 30 minutes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** HTST is designed to achieve **>99.999 % destruction (5-log reduction)** of pathogenic vegetative microorganisms while preserving **>95 % of heat-sensitive vitamins (Vitamin B1, B12, C)** and **native whey proteins**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Advantages of HTST Pasteurization (Detailed Engineering Analysis)

### A. Process & Quality Advantages
1. **Continuous Operation** — Higher throughput (typically **5,000–50,000 L/hr**) compared to batch (200–2,000 L/batch).
2. **Superior Nutrient Retention** — Short exposure time preserves heat-labile vitamins: B1 (thiamine) loss drops from 25 % (batch) to **<10 %** in HTST.
3. **Better Organoleptic Quality** — Minimal cooked or "caramelized" flavor since surface heat damage is avoided.
4. **Better Cream Line Formation** — Lower denaturation of native whey proteins (β-lactoglobulin) → milk retains natural fat globule clustering.
5. **Higher Microbiological Safety** — Properly designed holding tube guarantees uniform "every particle" lethality; validated via *Dean Index* > 1.0.
6. **Energy Efficiency** — Regenerator recovers up to **85–90 %** of thermal energy.
7. **Automation Friendly** — Built-in PID-controlled FDV (Flow Diversion Valve) ensures automatic rejection of under-processed milk.

### B. Economic & Operational Advantages
8. **Lower Labour Intensity** — Once set, operator mainly supervises — no manual handling of hot vats.
9. **Compact Footprint** — Plate HTST unit for 10,000 L/hr occupies only **~6 m²** floor space vs. ~25 m² for batch.
10. **Consistent Product Quality** — Uniform heating eliminates batch-to-batch variability.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Tip:** For exam, always write the **"3Q Advantages"** — *Quality, Quantity, Quirk-free*.

## 2.2 Disadvantages of HTST Pasteurization

1. **High Capital Investment** — SS-316 stainless steel plates, pumps, FDV, instrumentation → ₹25 Lakh–₹1.5 Crore for mid-capacity plants.
2. **Technical Skill Required** — Operators need training in CIP (Cleaning-In-Place), plate pack integrity, and instrumentation calibration.
3. **Not Viable for Tiny Dairy Units** — Economical only above **~2,000 L/day throughput**.
4. **Risk of Micro-Cracks in Plates** — Gasket failure causes mixing of raw and pasteurized milk streams → catastrophic food safety hazard.
5. **Post-Pasteurization Contamination (PPC)** — Since product is cooled and exposed, it is vulnerable to recontamination if downstream hygiene fails.
6. **CIP Mandatory After Every Shift** — Downtime 90–120 min daily.
7. **Strict pH & Hardness Control Needed** — Limescale from hard water fouls heater plates.
8. **Requires Stable Electricity / Steam Supply** — Voltage drop can disrupt booster pump → flow mismatch → FDV activation.
9. **Pressure Differential Maintenance** — Pasteurized side must always be at **>7 kPa higher** than raw side to prevent cross-contamination via leaks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students write "HTST is expensive to operate" — it is **expensive to install** but **cheap to operate** (low steam consumption per litre due to regeneration). Be precise in valuation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Typical KTU Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Holding Tube Length** | $L = \dfrac{Q \cdot t}{60 \cdot A}$ | m | Calculated for *Q* L/min, *t* sec |
| 2 | **Holding Time (theoretical)** | $t_h = \dfrac{L \cdot A}{Q_{\text{vol}}}$ | sec | ≥ 15 s |
| 3 | **Dean Index (Flow Criterion)** | $De = \dfrac{d}{\sqrt{D_h \cdot R_c}}$ | dimensionless | ≥ 1.0 (turbulent) |
| 4 | **Reynolds Number (Plate)** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D_h}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | ≥ 10,000 |
| 5 | **Lethality (F-value)** | $F_T^{z} = \sum 10^{(T_i - T_{\text{ref}})/z} \Delta t$ | min | F₇₂¹⁰ ≥ 1 |
| 6 | **Regeneration Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{reg}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{out,raw}} - T_{\text{in,raw}}}{T_{\text{pasteur}} - T_{\text{in,raw}}} \times 100$ | % | 80–90 % |
| 7 | **Mass Balance (Milk)** | $Q_{\text{in}} = Q_{\text{out}} + Q_{\text{diverted}}$ | L/h | Continuous |
| 8 | **Heat Balance (Regenerator)** | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kW | Steam economy factor |
| 9 | **PU Calculation (PMO)** | $PU = 100 + \sum (1.393)^{(T_i - 72)}$ | PU | ≥ 100 = legal pass |
| 10 | **Throughput Time** | $t_{\text{residence}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{system}}}{Q}$ | sec | ~30–60 s total |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Don't confuse:**
> - $\rho$ — density of milk ≈ **1032 kg/m³**
> - $c_p$ — specific heat of milk ≈ **3.93 kJ/kg·K**
> - $D_h$ — hydraulic diameter of plate channel ≈ **6–8 mm**
> - $z$ — thermal resistance constant for *M. tuberculosis* ≈ **5.6 °C**

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Dairy Engineering

This system is the **backbone of fluid milk industry** — every drop of "Toned / Double-Toned / Standardized Pasteurized Milk" sold by Mother Dairy, Amul, Nandini, Milma, and Heritage undergoes HTST. It is also used for:
- Pasteurization of cream (76 °C / 15 s for butter making),
- Pasteurization of whey (cream cheese and ricotta),
- Pre-treatment before **cheese milk** (to control starter culture competition).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Length of Holding Tube

The holding tube must be sized so that the **slowest-moving particle** (boundary layer) stays at least 15 s inside it. KTU examiners frequently ask for this.

**Given Data:**
- Plant throughput, $Q$ = **2,000 L/h** = 2 m³/h = **2000/3600 m³/s**
- Holding tube internal diameter, $D$ = **25 mm = 0.025 m**
- Required holding time, $t_h$ = **15 s**

**Step 1 — Write velocity equation from volumetric flow.**

$$Q = A \cdot v \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute cross-sectional area.**

$$A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

$$A = \dfrac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{4}$$

$$A = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute velocity.**

$$v = \dfrac{2000/3600}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{0.5556}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$v \approx 1.132 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the residence time definition.**

$$\text{Holding Tube Length} = v \times t_h$$

$$L = 1.132 \times 15$$

$$\boxed{L = 16.98 \approx 17 \; \text{m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For safety, plant designers actually use a **+25 % margin**, giving a final holding tube length of ~**21 m**. This is why commercial holding tubes are looped into a serpentine or vertical zigzag inside the cabinet.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Regenerator Heat Recovery Calculation

**Problem:** Milk enters HTST at **4 °C** and leaves the heater at **72 °C**. After the regenerator, the raw milk is pre-heated to **62 °C**. Compute **regeneration efficiency**.

**Step 1 — Cold stream temperature rise.**

$$\Delta T_{\text{cold}} = T_{\text{out,raw}} - T_{\text{in,raw}} = 62 - 4 = 58 \;°\text{C}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum possible temperature rise (if 100 % recovery).**

$$\Delta T_{\text{max}} = T_{\text{pasteur}} - T_{\text{in,raw}} = 72 - 4 = 68 \;°\text{C}$$

**Step 3 — Apply efficiency formula.**

$$\eta_{\text{reg}} = \dfrac{58}{68} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{reg}} = 85.3 \;\%}$$

**Step 4 — Steam energy savings.**

For 1 L of milk, enthalpy gain needed = $m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 1 \cdot 3.93 \cdot 68 = 267.24 \; \text{kJ}$

Without regenerator steam would supply **267.24 kJ**, but regenerator supplies **$1 \cdot 3.93 \cdot 58 = 227.94 \; \text{kJ}$**. Steam only needs to deliver the **balance: $39.3 \; \text{kJ}$** — a saving of **~85 %**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pasteurization Unit (PU) Numerical

The **Pasteurization Unit (PU)** is defined by the PMO where 1 PU = 1 minute of holding at 72 °C.

$$PU = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (1.393)^{(T_i - 72)} \cdot \Delta t_i$$

**Example:** A processor runs a slightly higher regime — hold milk at **74 °C for 18 s**.

$$PU = (1.393)^{(74 - 72)} \times \dfrac{18}{60}$$

$$PU = (1.393)^2 \times 0.3$$

$$PU = 1.9405 \times 0.3$$

$$\boxed{PU = 0.582 \; \text{min}}$$

**Interpretation:** Operating at 74 °C / 18 s provides **0.582 PU**, which is **below the legal minimum of 1 PU**. The line must be raised in temperature OR duration. Increasing to **75 °C / 15 s**:

$$PU = (1.393)^{(75-72)} \times \dfrac{15}{60} = (1.393)^3 \times 0.25 = 2.703 \times 0.25 = 0.676 \; \text{PU}$$

Increasing to **76 °C / 15 s**:

$$PU = (1.393)^4 \times 0.25 = 3.766 \times 0.25 = 0.941 \; \text{PU}$$

Increasing to **77 °C / 15 s**:

$$PU = (1.393)^5 \times 0.25 = 5.246 \times 0.25 = \boxed{1.312 \; \text{PU}} \; \checkmark \; \text{Legal}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Don't use the *F*-value equation if the question says "PU". These are different bases ($z = 5.6$°C for F, but PU formula uses fixed base 1.393 which corresponds to $z = 7$°C). Mixing them = **3-mark penalty**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — HTST vs. Other Methods

| Parameter | LTLT (Batch) | HTST | HHST | UHT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **T (°C)** | 63 | 72 | 89–94 | 138 |
| **t** | 30 min | 15 s | 1.0–0.1 s | 2–4 s |
| **Equipment** | Vat + agitator | Plate HTST | Plate/Scraped | Tubular/Indirect |
| **Whey Protein Denaturation (%)** | ~30 | ~6 | ~12 | ~75 |
| **Vitamin B1 Loss (%)** | ~25 | ~10 | ~15 | ~25 |
| **Shelf Life (refrigerated)** | 10–14 d | 14–21 d | 21–28 d | 6–9 months (aseptic) |
| **Capital Cost** | Low | Medium-High | High | Very High |
| **Energy / Litre** | High | Low (regenerated) | Medium | Medium |
| **Best Suited For** | Tiny dairies | Market milk | Cheese, cream | Long-life packs |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — HTST Pasteurizer

This schematic shows the **process flow** including all critical components and the **two possible paths** (forward vs. diverted) the milk can take.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RawIn["A1: Raw Milk Inlet"] --> BalanceTank["B1: Balance Tank with Float"]
    BalanceTank --> Pump1["C1: Raw Milk Pump / Booster Pump"]
    Pump1 --> Reg1["D1: Regenerative Heat Exchanger Section 1 (Plate Pack)"]
    Reg1 --> Heater["E1: Final Heater (Hot Water 78-82 C)"]
    Heater --> HoldingTube["F1: Holding Tube Loop 72 C for 15 seconds"]
    HoldingTube --> FDV["G1: Flow Diversion Valve FDV"]
    FDV --> Decision{"G2: Is Temperature at least 72 C?"}
    Decision -- Yes --> Reg2["D2: Regenerator Section 2 Pasteur side cools raw"]
    Decision -- No --> DivertBack["H1: Divert back to Balance Tank"]
    DivertBack --> BalanceTank
    Reg2 --> Cooler["I1: Cooling Section Plate Chilled Water"]
    Cooler --> Cream["I2: Optional Cream Standardization Loop"]
    Cream --> Storage["J1: Pasteurized Milk Storage Tank at 4 C"]
    Storage --> Pack["K1: Packaging and Distribution"]

    Heater --> BoilerLoop["E2: Hot Water Boiler / Steam Loop"]
    BoilerLoop --> Heater
    Cooler --> Chiller["I3: Chilled Water Brine System"]
    Chiller --> Cooler

    style BalanceTank fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e
    style Heater fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c
    style HoldingTube fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d
    style FDV fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a
    style Decision fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e
    style Storage fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46
```

## 4.2 Component-Level Architecture — Plate Heat Exchanger Pack

The **plate pack** is the heart of HTST. The diagram below shows how plates are arranged to form alternating raw/pasteurized channels:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PlatePack["HTST Plate Pack - Plate Heat Exchanger SS-316"]
        P1["P1 Plate: Raw Channel"] --- P2["P2 Plate: Pasteur Channel"] --- P3["P3 Plate: Raw"] --- P4["P4 Plate: Pasteur"] --- P5["P5 Plate: Raw"] --- P6["P6 Plate: Pasteur"]
    end

    RawIn2["Raw Milk 4 C"] --> P1
    P6 --> RawHot["Raw Milk Out 65-70 C Preheated"]
    PasteurIn["Pasteur Milk 75 C from Heater"] --> P2
    P2 --> PasteurOut["Pasteur Out 35-40 C Precooled"]
    P3 --> RegMid["Inter-stage Heat Exchange"]

    style P1 fill:#fde68a
    style P2 fill:#fca5a5
    style P3 fill:#fde68a
    style P4 fill:#fca5a5
    style P5 fill:#fde68a
    style P6 fill:#fca5a5
```

> [!NOTE]
> Plates are stamped with herringbone grooves to induce turbulence at low Reynolds numbers and enhance heat transfer. Typical plate thickness: **0.6–0.8 mm**; gasket material: EPDM or NBR food grade.

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Operational Sequence with FDV Logic

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant R as Raw Milk
    participant BT as Balance Tank
    participant P as Pump
    participant RG as Regenerator
    participant H as Heater
    participant HT as Holding Tube
    participant FDV as Flow Diversion Valve
    participant CL as Cooler
    participant PK as Packaging

    R->>BT: Incoming cold milk 4 C
    BT->>P: Float maintains constant level
    P->>RG: Pump to regenerator section 1
    RG->>H: Pre-heated to 62 C
    H->>HT: Final heated to 72 C
    HT->>FDV: After 15 sec retention
    FDV->>FDV: Thermometer probe reads temperature
    alt Temperature greater than or equal 72 C
        FDV->>CL: Forward flow to regenerator section 2
        CL->>PK: Cooled to 4 C and stored
    else Temperature less than 72 C
        FDV->>BT: Divert back to balance tank
        FDV->>FDV: Audible alarm + red lamp
    end
```

## 4.4 Detailed Component Listing Table

| Component | Function | Typical Material |
|---|---|---|
| **A — Balance Tank** | Buffers flow fluctuations; float valve | SS-304 |
| **B — Raw Milk Pump** | Transfers milk through the line | SS-316 centrifugal |
| **C — Regenerator (Plate)** | Recovers heat from outgoing → incoming milk | SS-316 plates, EPDM gaskets |
| **D — Final Heater** | Brings milk to **72 °C** using hot water | Plate / tubular, SS-316 |
| **E — Holding Tube** | Maintains **72 °C for 15 s** | SS-316, often 1–1.5" |
| **F — Flow Diversion Valve (FDV)** | 3-way valve, redirects under-processed milk | Pneumatic, SS-body |
| **G — Timing Pump** | Variable-speed positive displacement | SS-316 lobe |
| **H — Indicating Thermometer** | Visual temperature readout | Mercury-in-glass / digital |
| **I — Recording Thermometer** | Permanent chart record for compliance | Circular chart 24 h |
| **J — Pressure Gauges** | Differential pressure for leak detection | Bourdon, sanitary |
| **K — Cooling Section** | Cools pasteurized milk to **≤ 4 °C** | Plate heat exchanger |
| **L — CIP System** | Clean-In-Place loop | Spray balls, chemical tanks |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any six advantages of HTST pasteurization over batch (LTLT) pasteurization.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Continuous operation** enables higher throughput (5,000–50,000 L/hr) versus batch.
2. **Regenerative heat recovery** of 80–90 % reduces steam consumption.
3. **Better retention of heat-labile nutrients** — vitamin B1 loss drops from ~25 % (LTLT) to <10 % (HTST).
4. **Improved organoleptic quality** — minimal cooked flavour.
5. **Compact footprint** — plate HTST needs only ~6 m² for 10,000 L/hr capacity.
6. **Automated safety** via Flow Diversion Valve (FDV) guarantees regulatory compliance.
7. *(Any six valid points, full marks)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners accept "continuous process" + "energy efficient" as minimum 2 distinct points.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Enumerate four disadvantages of HTST pasteurization.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **High capital cost** — plate heat exchangers, FDV, and CIP system require substantial initial investment (~₹25 Lakh+).
2. **Technical complexity** — skilled operators needed for plate pack maintenance, gasket replacement, and instrumentation.
3. **Post-pasteurization contamination (PPC) risk** — leakage in regenerator plates can mix raw and pasteurized streams.
4. **Mandatory CIP** — daily cleaning-in-place adds 90–120 min downtime.
5. **Not economical for small dairy units** below 2,000 L/day throughput.

---

## PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **components and function of an HTST plate pasteurizer**. Discuss the role of the regenerative heat exchanger in detail.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A 10,000 L/h HTST plant holds milk at 72 °C in a 30 mm internal diameter tube. Calculate the **required holding tube length**. If the diameter is reduced to 25 mm, what is the new holding tube length? Comment on the implications.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

The HTST pasteurizer consists of the following components in sequence:

1. **Balance Tank (BT)** — receives raw milk from upstream storage; a float maintains constant head; prevents pump cavitation.
2. **Raw Milk Pump** — centrifugal pump moves milk forward; downstream booster pump stabilizes flow.
3. **Regenerative Heat Exchanger (Plate Pack)** — multi-stream plate exchanger that:
   - Section A: incoming raw milk (4 °C) absorbs heat from outgoing pasteurized milk (72 °C) → exits at ~62 °C.
   - Section B: outgoing pasteurized milk loses heat → cools from 72 °C → ~35 °C.
   - **Efficiency = 80–90 %**; this single component is responsible for the bulk of the economic advantage of HTST.
4. **Heater** — hot water (78–82 °C) on utility side raises milk from 62 °C → 72 °C.
5. **Holding Tube** — serpentine SS-316 tube; design ensures minimum 15 s residence for the slowest particle.
6. **Flow Diversion Valve (FDV)** — pneumatic 3-way valve actuated by a temperature probe at the holding tube exit. If milk <72 °C, FDV **diverts milk back to balance tank** and sounds alarm.
7. **Cooling Section** — chilled water (≤1 °C) brings milk to ≤4 °C.
8. **Instruments** — indicating thermometer (visible) + recording thermometer (chart for legal compliance) + pressure gauges for regenerator leak detection.

**Role of Regenerator:**

The regenerator is the **energy-efficient heart** of HTST. It reduces steam consumption by 85 %. Since raw and pasteurized sides are separated by **two thin SS-316 plates with NBR/EPDM gasket** between, the system maintains pasteurized-side pressure **> raw-side pressure** to ensure that **in case of gasket leak, pasteurized milk leaks into raw side, not vice versa** (defense-in-depth design).

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Given:**
- Throughput $Q$ = 10,000 L/h = 10 m³/h = 10/3600 m³/s = 2.778 × 10⁻³ m³/s
- Holding tube $D_1$ = 30 mm = 0.030 m
- Required $t_h$ = 15 s

**Step 1 — Cross-section area for $D_1 = 0.030$ m:**

$$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi D_1^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.030)^2}{4} = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Velocity:**

$$v_1 = \dfrac{Q}{A_1} = \dfrac{2.778 \times 10^{-3}}{7.069 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.929 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Holding tube length:**

$$L_1 = v_1 \times t_h = 3.929 \times 15 = \boxed{58.94 \approx 59 \; \text{m}}$$

**Step 4 — For $D_2 = 0.025$ m:**

$$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2$$

$$v_2 = \dfrac{2.778 \times 10^{-3}}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.659 \; \text{m/s}$$

$$L_2 = 5.659 \times 15 = \boxed{84.88 \approx 85 \; \text{m}}$$

**Implications:**
- Smaller diameter → higher velocity → longer holding tube → more pressure drop → higher pumping cost.
- Smaller diameter → better thermal uniformity (closer to plug flow) → easier to achieve legal "every particle" condition.
- Larger diameter → less pressure drop but increased risk of laminar boundary layer at walls → potential for under-processing (Dean Index check needed).

---

### Question B — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages of HTST pasteurization**. Why is HTST preferred over LTLT in modern commercial dairy plants?* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the working of the **Flow Diversion Valve (FDV)** with a sketch. What happens if the FDV fails during operation?* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

| **Advantages** | **Disadvantages** |
|---|---|
| Continuous high throughput | High capital cost |
| Better nutrient retention | Requires skilled operators |
| Energy efficient (85 % regen) | Risk of PPC if plates leak |
| Better flavour profile | Daily CIP required |
| Automated FDV safety | Not viable for very small units |
| Compact footprint | Electricity/steam dependency |

**Why HTST over LTLT?**
- HTST achieves **5-log microbial reduction** at 72 °C / 15 s — equivalent in lethality to 63 °C / 30 min but with **dramatically lower thermal damage**.
- HTST's **regenerative design** makes it 70–80 % cheaper to operate per litre.
- HTST's **continuous FDV** provides **automatic regulatory assurance** that LTLT cannot — a manual operator may forget to time correctly, but FDV responds in <1 s.
- For plants above 2,000 L/day, **amortized cost per litre** of HTST is much lower.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**FDV Working Principle:**

The FDV is a **3-way pneumatically-actuated sanitary valve** located at the exit of the holding tube. Its position is governed by a **temperature-sensing probe** immersed in milk right after the holding tube.

**Normal Operation (≥ 72 °C):**
- Probe sends signal to FDV controller.
- Valve opens the **forward line** → milk flows to regenerator and cooler.
- A small sample is recorded on the chart recorder.

**Divert Operation (< 72 °C):**
- Probe detects sub-standard temperature.
- Within **<1 second**, pneumatic actuator repositions the valve to **divert line** → milk flows back to balance tank.
- Audible alarm and red warning lamp activate.
- Recorder marks the diversion event on the chart.

**Sketch Description (text-based):**

```
                    +---> TO COOLER (Forward)
                    |
[Holding Tube] ---> [FDV] -- (Probe T)
                    |
                    +---> TO BALANCE TANK (Divert)
```

**If FDV Fails:**
- Stuck in **forward position** → under-processed milk reaches packaging → **public health hazard + product recall**.
- Stuck in **divert position** → entire line stops → plant shutdown → revenue loss.
- Therefore, **FDV must be tested daily** by injecting hot water at <72 °C and observing whether it diverts.
- Most modern FDVs are **dual-redundant** (two independent probes + dual seals) for fail-safe operation.

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Writing **"FDV controls flow rate"** — WRONG. It controls **direction**, not rate. The timing pump controls rate.
> 2. Confusing **holding tube** with **holding time** — holding tube is the *physical pipe*, holding time is the *residence duration*.
> 3. Stating **"UHT is a type of pasteurization"** in the same breath — UHT is sterilization, not pasteurization. UHT achieves *complete* destruction; pasteurization does not.
> 4. Missing the **pressure differential** concept in regenerator — pressure on pasteurized side **must be ≥7 kPa higher** than raw side.
> 5. Forgetting that the **recording thermometer** chart is a **legal document** under PMO.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> ✅ **HTST stands for High Temperature — Short Time**, defined by PMO as **72 °C / 15 s**.

> ✅ **FDV (Flow Diversion Valve)** is the *automatic safety interlock* — it is **not** a flow regulator.

> ✅ **Regenerator efficiency = 80–90 %** — this is the *energy engine* of HTST.

> ✅ **Holding tube** must be sized for **slowest particle** at the wall — minimum residence = 15 s.

> ✅ **Pressure rule:** Pasteurized side pressure > Raw side pressure (≥7 kPa) to prevent cross-contamination.

> ✅ **PU (Pasteurization Unit):** legally required to be **≥ 1**, with z-value assumption of 7 °C.

> ✅ **Critical instruments:** Indicating + Recording thermometer, Pressure gauges, Timing pump, FDV probe — all mandatory under PMO.

> ✅ **CIP mandatory daily** because milk solids and mineral scale foul the plates.

> ✅ **HTST advantages** → continuous, energy-efficient, better nutrients, automated safety.
> ✅ **HTST disadvantages** → high capital cost, skilled labour, PPC risk, not for tiny dairies.

> ✅ **Holding tube length formula:** $L = \dfrac{Q \cdot t_h}{A}$, **Add +25 % safety margin.**

> ✅ **Always mention PMO / FSSAI** in legal/regulatory questions — examiners love it.

> ✅ **Diagrams carry weight** — always draw balance tank → pump → regenerator → heater → holding tube → FDV → cooler → storage.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Disadvantages of HTST; Sterilization – In-Bottle Sterilization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT615 - DAIRY TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Advantages and Disadvantages of HTST; Sterilization – In-Bottle Sterilization # 📘 Module 2 — Heat Processing of Milk: HTST & In-Bottle Sterilization --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 HTST Pasteurization — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **HTST (High Temperature – Short Time) Pasteurization** is the continuous-flow thermal process in which milk is heated to a minimum of **72 °C (161 °F)** and held at that temperature for **at least 15 seconds**, followed by immediate cooling to ≤ 4 °C. It is the legal benchmark for pasteurized market milk in most countries (FSSAI, FDA, EU Codex). The process is typically carried out in a **plate heat exchanger (PHE)** consisting of regeneration, heating, and cooling sections, and is mathematically governed by the **equivalent pasteurization effect** concept: $$F_{72}^{7.5} = t \cdot 10^{\frac{T - 72}{7.5}}$$ where $T$ is the temperature (°C) and $t$ is the holding time (seconds). The reference $z$-value is **7.5 °C**. --- ### 1.2 In-Bottle Sterilization — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **In-Bottle Sterilization** is a *commercial sterilization* technique in which the milk is first filled into hermetically sealed glass bottles (or metal cans) and then heat-treated in a batch retort or autoclave at **108 °C – 115 °C for 20 – 40 minutes**, depending on container geometry. The objective is to render the product **commercially sterile** — i.e., free from pathogenic and spoilage organisms capable of growing under normal storage conditions — providing a **shelf-stable** product for 3–6 months at ambient temperature. --- ### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy | Concept | Analogy (Real-World Picture) | | :--- | :--- | | **HTST Pasteurization** | Imagine a *fast toll-booth tunnel* 🚗 — cars (milk) zoom through, get a 15-second "flash bath," and exit cleaned. Quick, continuous, gentle — but you must still refrigerate them. | | **In-Bottle Sterilization** | Imagine *pressure-cooking your lunch in a sealed tiffin box* 🍱 — once sealed and heat-processed, it can sit on the counter for weeks without spoiling. Heavy treatment, but gives total independence from the cold chain. | --- ### 1.4 Standard Process Metrics (Bold Highlights) > **HTST Reference Conditions:** **72 °C / 15 s** — codified under FSSAI (Part II, Chapter 2). > > **HTST Plate Outlet Temperature:** **4 °C (≤ 7 °C tolerance)** > > **HTST Logarithmic Microbial Reduction (Coxiella burnetii):** **≥ 5-log₁₀** > > **In-Bottle Sterilization Temperature:** **108 – 115 °C** > > **In-Bottle Sterilization Time:** **20 – 40 minutes** > > **Target Shelf Life of Sterilized Milk:** **≥ 6 months at 25 °C** --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet --- ## 2.1 Advantages and Disadvantages of HTST — Structured Logic ### 2.1.1 Why HTST Dominates the Dairy Industry The dominance of HTST over LTLT (Low Temperature Long Time, 63 °C/30 min) in modern dairies is driven by **six engineering and biochemical advantages**: 1. **Continuous Operation** — Plate Heat Exchangers (PHEs) handle **5,000 – 50,000 L/h**, vastly higher than batch LTLT vats (~2,000 L max). This makes HTST **economically superior at industrial scale**. 2. **Superior Organoleptic Quality** — The brief 15-second exposure preserves: - **Whey proteins** (β-lactoglobulin, α-lactalbumin) which retain native state - **Vitamins B-complex** and **Vitamin C** retention improves by 10–15 % - **Fresh milk flavor** is retained (minimal Maillard browning) 3. **Energy Economy via Regeneration** — The *regeneration section* of the PHE transfers heat from outgoing hot milk to incoming cold milk, achieving **up to 90 % heat recovery**, dramatically reducing steam and cooling-water costs. 4. **Automation Compatibility** — HTST integrates with **PLC-based control**, **CIP (Clean-In-Place)** systems, **flow diversion valves (FDV)**, and **chart recorders** required by food-safety regulators. 5. **Effective Microbial Kill** — Achieves **> 99.999 % (5-log)** destruction of *Coxiella burnetii* (the most heat-resistant vegetative pathogen in milk), satisfying FSSAI/FDA standards. 6. **Combination Operations** — Easily combined with **homogenization** (200 – 250 bar) and **cream separation** in the same PHE line. --- ### 2.1.2 Disadvantages of HTST — Engineering Limitations 1. **High Capital Investment** — A 10,000 L/h HTST plant costs approximately **₹ 50 – 80 lakhs**, compared to **₹ 5 – 10 lakhs** for an LTLT vat. 2. **Requires Skilled Operation** — Precision control of **± 0.5 °C** deviation, flow rate, and FDV logic is essential; human error can lead to **public health failure**. 3. **Fouling of Plates** — Mineral deposition (calcium phosphate) and protein denaturation on PHE plates reduce heat transfer efficiency; CIP cycles become more frequent. 4. **Thermophilic Growth** — Between processing runs, thermophilic bacteria (*Bacillus stearothermophilus*) can colonize the regeneration section, generating **psychrotrophic survival risk** if temperature drops below 65 °C. 5. **Limited Shelf Life** — Pasteurized milk has only **7 – 14 days** at 4 °C; post-pasteurization contamination (PPC) is a perpetual threat. 6. **Not Sterilizing** — HTST does **not destroy bacterial spores** (*Bacillus*, *Clostridium*); the product must remain refrigerated. --- ### 2.1.3 Logic Flow — HTST Decision Matrix > **Why Choose HTST?** → Large volume + refrigeration available + retail in 7 days + regulatory compliance. > > **Why Avoid HTST?** → Tiny farm dairy + unreliable power + remote distribution without cold chain. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations > [!NOTE] > The following table compiles every equation you may need for HTST / In-Bottle Sterilization numerical problems. | # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Boundary | Engineering Use | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Pasteurization Effect (Equivalent Time)** | $F = t \cdot 10^{\frac{T - T_{ref}}{z}}$ | $F$ in s, $T$ in °C | Compare lethality of any HTST profile to standard 72 °C/15 s | | 2 | **Reference Conditions for HTST** | $T_{ref} = 72$ °C, $z = 7.5$ °C, $t_{ref} = 15$ s | $F_{72}^{7.5} \geq 15$ s | FSSAI / FDA regulatory benchmark | | 3 | **D-Value (Decimal Reduction Time)** | $D_T$ = time at $T$ °C to kill 90 % of population | minutes | Time to achieve 1-log reduction at $T$ | | 4 | **Z-Value** | $z = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{\log D_{T_1} - \log D_{T_2}}$ | °C | °C rise needed to reduce D by 90 % | | 5 | **Sterilization Value F₀** | $F_0 = \Delta t \cdot \sum 10^{\frac{T - 121.1}{10}}$ | minutes | Reference: 121.1 °C, $z = 10$ °C for *C. botulinum* | | 6 | **Heat Recovery (Regeneration)** | $\eta = \frac{T_{out, reg} - T_{in, reg}}{T_{pasteurize} - T_{in, reg}} \times 100$ | % | Efficiency of PHE regeneration | | 7 | **First-Order Microbial Death** | $\frac{N}{N_0} = e^{-kt}$ or $= 10^{-t/D}$ | dimensionless | Determines log reduction | | 8 | **Stokes' Law (In-Bottle Convection)** | $v = \frac{g \rho (\beta) \Delta T L^2}{18 \mu}$ | m/s | Models natural convection inside sealed bottle during autoclave cycle | | 9 | **Arrhenius Reaction Rate** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | s⁻¹ | Used for Maillard browning & nutrient destruction during sterilization | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | HTST Utility | In-Bottle Sterilization Utility | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Urban Dairies (Mother Dairy, Aavin, Nandini)** | Daily fluid milk supply chain | Flavored shelf-stable milk for hotels, defence, railways | | **Defence (Indian Army)** | Camp fresh milk in cold zones | Combat ration milk in forward posts without refrigeration | | **Space / Aerospace (NASA analogue)** | — | Long-shelf-life beverages for ISS missions | | **Export Markets** | Pasteurized cream, cheese milk | UHT (conceptually related) and bottled sterilized milk for tropical export | | **Soft Drink Industry** | Carbonated beverage pasteurization | In-can hot-fill sterilization | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equivalent Pasteurization Effect **Goal:** Show that a process running at 75 °C for 10 s is equivalent (or not) to the standard 72 °C/15 s. ### Step 1 — Recall the Pasteurization Equivalent-Time Formula The general formulation of thermal process lethality for a constant-temperature holding section is: $$F = t \cdot 10^{\frac{T - T_{ref}}{z}}$$ **Interpretation:** - $t$ = actual holding time (seconds) - $T$ = actual holding temperature (°C) - $T_{ref}$ = reference temperature (72 °C for HTST) - $z$ = temperature change needed for a 10-fold change in death rate (7.5 °C for vegetative pathogens in milk) ### Step 2 — Compute F for Standard HTST Substituting $T = 72$ °C, $t = 15$ s, $z = 7.5$ °C: $$ F_{72}^{7.5} = 15 \cdot 10^{\frac{72 - 72}{7.5}} $$ $$ F_{72}^{7.5} = 15 \cdot 10^{0} = 15 \cdot 1 $$ $$ \boxed{F_{72}^{7.5} = 15 \text{ seconds}} $$ **Logic:** At the reference temperature itself, the equivalent time equals the actual time — a sanity-check identity. ### Step 3 — Compute F for the Proposed Profile (75 °C / 10 s) $$ F_{75}^{7.5} = 10 \cdot 10^{\frac{75 - 72}{7.5}} $$ $$ F_{75}^{7.5} = 10 \cdot 10^{0.4} $$ $$ 10^{0.4} = 2.5119 \quad \text{(from log tables or calculator)} $$ $$ F_{75}^{7.5} = 10 \times 2.5119 = 25.119 \text{ s} $$ ### Step 4 — Compare $$ F_{75}^{7.5} = 25.12 \text{ s} > F_{72}^{7.5} = 15 \text{ s} $$ **Conclusion:** The 75 °C / 10 s profile provides **~1.67× the lethality** of the standard. It is *acceptable* as a legal pasteurization process provided the flow diversion device (FDV) triggers correctly. ✔️ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Logarithmic Reduction Achieved by HTST **Goal:** Find log reduction when initial count is $N_0 = 1 \times 10^5$ cfu/mL and HTST kills 99.999 % (5-log). ### Step 1 — Define Logarithmic Reduction $$\log_{10} \text{ Reduction} = \log_{10} N_0 - \log_{10} N_t$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute the Given 99.999 % Kill 99.999 % killed means survivors $N_t = 0.001 \% = 10^{-5}$ fraction. $$ N_t = N_0 \times 10^{-5} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply the Logarithm $$ \log_{10} \text{ Reduction} = \log_{10} N_0 - \log_{10}(N_0 \times 10^{-5}) $$ $$ \log_{10} \text{ Reduction} = \log_{10} N_0 - \log_{10} N_0 - \log_{10}(10^{-5}) $$ $$ \log_{10} \text{ Reduction} = -(-5) $$ $$ \boxed{\log_{10} \text{ Reduction} = 5} $$ **Logic:** Each "9" in a percentage kill corresponds to one log₁₀ unit. Hence "99.999" = 5 nines = 5-log reduction. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Time Required at 110 °C for Commercial Sterility **Given:** - Target $F_0$ for *C. botulinum* in milk = 3.0 minutes (commercial sterility benchmark) - $z = 10$ °C for *C. botulinum* - Reference temperature $T_{ref} = 121.1$ °C ### Step 1 — Use the $F_0$ Formula $$ F_0 = t \cdot 10^{\frac{T - 121.1}{10}} $$ ### Step 2 — Solve for $t$ at $T = 110$ °C $$ 3.0 = t \cdot 10^{\frac{110 - 121.1}{10}} $$ $$ 3.0 = t \cdot 10^{-1.11} $$ $$ 10^{-1.11} = 0.0776 $$ $$ t = \frac{3.0}{0.0776} $$ $$ t = 38.66 \text{ minutes} $$ ### Step 3 — Round Up for Safety Margin $$ \boxed{t \approx 39 \text{ minutes at 110 °C}} $$ **Logic:** Because 110 °C is **11.1 °C below the reference 121.1 °C**, lethality drops by a factor of $10^{1.11} \approx 12.9$, requiring nearly 13× the reference time. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — HTST vs In-Bottle Sterilization > [!NOTE] > Use this matrix as a single-pane revision before the examination. | Parameter | HTST Pasteurization | In-Bottle Sterilization | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Reference Temperature** | **72 °C** | **108 – 115 °C** | | **Reference Time** | **15 s** | **20 – 40 min** | | **Process Type** | Continuous (PHE) | Batch (Autoclave / Retort) | | **Target Organism** | Vegetative pathogens (*M. tuberculosis*, *Coxiella*) | Spores (*C. botulinum*, *B. stearothermophilus*) | | **Log Reduction Achieved** | **5-log** | **≥ 12-log** (commercial sterility) | | **Shelf Life** | **7 – 14 days at ≤ 4 °C** | **3 – 6 months at 25 °C** | | **Refrigeration Required** | **Yes (mandatory)** | **No** | | **Flavor Impact** | Fresh, mild | Caramelized, cooked | | **Nutrient Retention (Vitamin B₁)** | **~90 %** | **~50 – 60 %** | | **Protein Denaturation** | **~10 – 15 %** | **~50 – 70 %** | | **Maillard Browning** | Negligible | Pronounced | | **Capital Cost (per 1000 L/h)** | ₹ 8 – 10 lakh | ₹ 12 – 15 lakh (with autoclave) | | **Operational Skill** | High (PLC) | Moderate (batch timing) | | **FSSAI Status** | Standardized under FSSAI | Falls under "Sterilized Milk" provisions | | **Major Risk** | Post-pasteurization contamination | Bottle breakage, explosion if sealing fails | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics --- ## 4.1 HTST Plate Heat Exchanger — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Raw Milk Tank
4 to 6 °C] --> A2[Balance Tank
with Float Switch] A2 --> A3[Raw Milk Regenerator Section
PHE Cold Side] A3 --> A4[Heating Section
Hot Water 78 to 80 °C] A4 --> A5[Holding Tube
72 °C for 15 seconds] A5 --> A6{Flow Diversion Valve
FDV Decision} A6 -- Temp OK 72 °C --> A7[Forward Flow
Pasteurized Milk] A6 -- Temp Low --> A2 A7 --> A8[Regenerator Section
PHE Hot Side
Heats incoming raw milk] A8 --> A9[Cooling Section
Chilled Water 4 °C] A9 --> A10[Final Milk Outlet
≤ 4 °C] A10 --> A11[Packaging
Pouch or Bottle] style A1 fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#0066cc style A5 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600 style A6 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000 style A10 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009900 ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - The **regenerator** is the heat-recovery heart of the PHE. - The **holding tube** is sized by the formula $L = v \times t$ where $v$ is turbulent velocity (~1 m/s). - The **FDV** is the regulatory safety gate — failure to divert = legal violation. --- ## 4.2 In-Bottle Sterilization — Batch Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD B1[Standardized Homogenized Milk
3.5 percent fat 8.5 percent SNF] --> B2[Pre-heating to 50 to 60 °C
Indirect tubular heater] B2 --> B3[Glass Bottle Filling
Glass Bottles Pre-sterilized] B3 --> B4[Crown Sealing
Hermetic Crimp] B4 --> B5[Loading into Autoclave Crates
Stainless Steel Racks] B5 --> B6[Steam-Heated Autoclave
108 to 115 °C, 20 to 40 min] B6 --> B7[Pressure Cooling
Counter-pressure with sterile water] B7 --> B8[Storage at 25 °C Ambient
for 7 days Quarantine] B8 --> B9{Incubation Test
Passes Spoilage Check} B9 -- Pass --> B10[Labeling and Dispatch
Shelf Life 3 to 6 Months] B9 -- Fail --> B11[Reject Batch
Incinerate or Reprocess] style B1 fill:#e0f0ff style B6 fill:#ffcccc style B9 fill:#ffe0b3 style B10 fill:#e6ffe6 style B11 fill:#333333,color:#ffffff ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - Note the **counter-pressure cooling** step — sudden pressure release would explode the glass bottle. - The **7-day quarantine incubation test** is critical for confirming commercial sterility before release. --- ## 4.3 Lethality vs Time–Temperature — Conceptual Plot ```mermaid flowchart LR C1[HTST Zone
72 °C and 15 s
5-log kill] -.->|Below line| C3[Safe Pasteurized Milk] C2[In-Bottle Zone
110 °C and 30 min
12-log kill] -->|Above line| C4[Commercially Sterile Milk] C3 --> C5[Refrigerated Distribution
7 to 14 days] C4 --> C6[Ambient Distribution
3 to 6 months] style C1 fill:#fff5cc style C2 fill:#ffcccc style C5 fill:#e6ffe6 style C6 fill:#ccf0ff ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short-Answer) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **List any four advantages of HTST pasteurization over LTLT.** **Model Answer (4 points):** 1. **Continuous operation** — higher throughput (5,000–50,000 L/h) compared to batch LTLT. 2. **Heat recovery up to 90 %** through the regenerator section, reducing steam/energy cost. 3. **Better nutrient retention** — short 15-s hold preserves heat-sensitive vitamins and whey proteins. 4. **Automation-friendly** — easily integrated with PLC, CIP, and FDV-based safety systems. *(Any 4 points × 0.75 = 3 marks)* --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **State two disadvantages of in-bottle sterilization of milk.** **Model Answer (4 points):** 1. **Cooked / caramelized flavor** — prolonged high heat (110–115 °C) triggers Maillard browning and produces a pronounced "sterilized" off-flavor disliked by many consumers. 2. **High nutrient loss** — Vitamin B₁, B₆, B₁₂, and Vitamin C degrade by 30–50 %; lysine becomes biologically unavailable due to Maillard reaction with reducing sugars. *(2 points × 1.5 = 3 marks)* --- ## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Explain the working of an HTST plate heat exchanger with a neatly labelled flow diagram. List any four advantages and three disadvantages of HTST pasteurization.** #### **Model Solution — Part (a):** **1. Working of HTST PHE (4 marks):** The HTST system consists of the following sequential sections: - **Balance Tank** with float switch ensures constant feed and prevents pump starvation. - **Regeneration Section** (cold side): incoming raw milk at 4 °C is pre-heated by outgoing pasteurized milk via stainless-steel plates, achieving heat recovery up to **90 %**. - **Heating Section**: milk is brought to **72 °C ± 0.5 °C** by hot water (78–80 °C) flowing counter-currently on the service side. - **Holding Tube**: a precisely sized tube where milk resides for **exactly 15 seconds**; tube length is calculated by $L = v \times t$, with turbulent velocity of ~1 m/s. - **Flow Diversion Valve (FDV)**: if milk temperature drops below 72 °C, the FDV automatically diverts flow back to the balance tank. - **Regeneration Section** (hot side): pasteurized milk gives up its heat to raw milk and drops to ~15 °C. - **Cooling Section**: milk is further cooled by chilled water to ≤ **4 °C**. **2. Advantages of HTST (2 marks — any 4):** 1. Continuous, high-throughput operation (5,000–50,000 L/h). 2. Excellent heat recovery (~90 %) via regenerator → lower steam cost. 3. Superior retention of vitamins, whey proteins, fresh flavor. 4. Easy integration with CIP, PLC, homogenization. 5. Legally compliant worldwide (FSSAI, FDA, Codex). 6. 5-log destruction of *Coxiella burnetii*. **3. Disadvantages of HTST (1 mark — any 3):** 1. High capital investment (₹ 50–80 lakh for 10,000 L/h). 2. Requires skilled operators and constant monitoring. 3. Plate fouling by calcium phosphate needs frequent CIP. 4. Does not kill spores → must be refrigerated. 5. Thermophilic growth in regeneration section between cycles. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Calculate the equivalent pasteurization time $F_{72}^{7.5}$ for a process running at 78 °C for 8 seconds. State whether this process satisfies FSSAI regulations.** #### **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Step 1 — Recall the Equivalent Pasteurization Formula** $$ F_{72}^{7.5} = t \cdot 10^{\frac{T - 72}{7.5}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute $T = 78$ °C, $t = 8$ s** $$ F_{72}^{7.5} = 8 \cdot 10^{\frac{78 - 72}{7.5}} $$ $$ F_{72}^{7.5} = 8 \cdot 10^{\frac{6}{7.5}} $$ $$ F_{72}^{7.5} = 8 \cdot 10^{0.8} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the exponential term** $$ 10^{0.8} = 6.3096 $$ **Step 4 — Final Computation** $$ F_{72}^{7.5} = 8 \times 6.3096 = 50.48 \text{ seconds} $$ **Step 5 — Compliance Check** FSSAI requires **minimum $F_{72}^{7.5} = 15$ s**. Since $50.48$ s $\geq$ 15 s, the process is **COMPLIANT** and provides a **safety margin of 35.48 s** (3.37× the legal minimum). ✔️ **Conclusion:** The 78 °C / 8 s profile is a safe and over-engineered HTST alternative, commonly used in **European-style ESL (Extended Shelf Life) plants**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Describe in detail the In-Bottle Sterilization process for milk. List four advantages and three disadvantages.** #### **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Process Description (4 marks):** 1. **Standardization & Homogenization** — Milk is standardized to 3.5 % fat and 8.5 % SNF, then homogenized at 200–250 bar to prevent fat separation during autoclaving. 2. **Pre-heating** — Milk is warmed to 50–60 °C in a tubular heater to reduce thermal shock on glass. 3. **Bottle Filling** — Cleaned, sterilized glass bottles (typically 200 mL or 500 mL) are filled leaving ~2 cm headspace for expansion. 4. **Crown Sealing** — Hermetic crown caps are crimped to ensure airtight closure. 5. **Autoclaving** — Loaded crates enter a batch steam autoclave operating at **108–115 °C for 20–40 minutes**, depending on bottle geometry. Steam pressure is held at 0.5–1.0 bar above water pressure to prevent bottle deformation. 6. **Counter-Pressure Cooling** — Sterile cooled water (under pressure) is sprayed to bring bottle temperature to ~40 °C without rupturing the seal. 7. **Quarantine & Incubation Test** — Bottles are held for **7 days at 25 °C and 55 °C** in parallel. Batches showing swelling are rejected. 8. **Labeling & Dispatch** — Approved batches are labelled with "Sterilized Milk" and a best-before date. **Advantages (2 marks — any 4):** 1. **Hermetic seal** prevents any post-sterilization contamination. 2. **Shelf-stable** for 3–6 months at ambient — no refrigeration. 3. **Drastic reduction in vegetative pathogens and spores** (≥ 12-log). 4. Suitable for **remote distribution** without cold chain. 5. **Strong cultural preference** in South India for "toned sterilized milk" taste. 6. Bottle itself is recyclable and reusable for home purposes. **Disadvantages (1 mark — any 3):** 1. **Cooked flavor & brown color** due to Maillard reaction. 2. **Nutrient loss** — Vitamins B₁, C, B₁₂ degrade significantly; lysine becomes unavailable. 3. **Risk of bottle breakage / explosion** if counter-pressure fails. 4. **Slow batch process** — lower throughput than continuous UHT. 5. **Higher energy consumption** than UHT/HTST for equivalent volume. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Determine the holding time required at 113 °C in an autoclave to achieve $F_0 = 3.0$ minutes, given $z = 10$ °C and reference temperature $T_{ref} = 121.1$ °C.** #### **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Step 1 — Apply the F₀ Formula** $$ F_0 = t \cdot 10^{\frac{T - 121.1}{10}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute Known Values** $$ 3.0 = t \cdot 10^{\frac{113 - 121.1}{10}} $$ $$ 3.0 = t \cdot 10^{-0.81} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the Exponential** $$ 10^{-0.81} = 0.1549 $$ **Step 4 — Solve for t** $$ t = \frac{3.0}{0.1549} $$ $$ t = 19.36 \text{ minutes} $$ **Step 5 — Apply Practical Safety Margin (Typically +20 %)** $$ t_{recommended} = 19.36 \times 1.20 \approx 23.2 \text{ minutes} $$ **Conclusion:** To achieve commercial sterility ($F_0 = 3.0$ min) at 113 °C, the milk must be held for **~23 minutes** in the autoclave, accounting for industrial safety margin. ✔️ --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing "sterilization" with "pasteurization"** — they are NOT synonyms. Sterilization destroys spores; pasteurization does not. > 2. **Wrong $z$-value** — Use $z = 7.5$ °C for pasteurization (HTST) but $z = 10$ °C for sterilization ($F_0$). > 3. **Sign error in exponent** — when $T < T_{ref}$, exponent is *negative*; students often invert it. > 4. **Forgetting units** — $F_0$ is in *minutes*; $F_{72}^{7.5}$ is in *seconds*. > 5. **Skipping safety margin** in sterilization problems — always add 15–20 % in industrial context. > 6. **Missing FDV logic** — HTST without flow diversion is regulatory non-compliance, even if temperature is correct. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > ✅ **HTST = 72 °C / 15 s** in a continuous PHE. > ✅ **FDV (Flow Diversion Valve) is mandatory** — failure to divert = legal violation. > ✅ **Regenerator section** recovers up to **90 %** of process heat. > ✅ HTST gives **5-log reduction** of vegetative pathogens; it does **NOT** kill spores. > ✅ Pasteurized milk **must be refrigerated** at ≤ 4 °C. > ✅ **In-Bottle Sterilization = 108–115 °C / 20–40 min** in a sealed glass bottle inside an autoclave. > ✅ **Counter-pressure cooling** is mandatory to prevent glass-bottle explosion. > ✅ **7-day quarantine incubation test** at 25 °C and 55 °C validates commercial sterility. > ✅ Sterilized milk is **shelf-stable for 3–6 months** at ambient — no cold chain needed. > ✅ **Maillard browning + cooked flavor** are unavoidable sensory penalties. > ✅ Use $z = 7.5$ °C for HTST and $z = 10$ °C for sterilization — DO NOT swap. > ✅ **Three crucial equations to memorize:** $F = t \cdot 10^{(T-T_{ref})/z}$, $\log \text{ Reduction} = \log N_0 - \log N_t$, and $\eta_{regeneration} = \Delta T / \Delta T_{max} \times 100$.
