## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style]`

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice Option

**Q. (a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **classification of sanitary valves** used in the dairy industry. Discuss in detail the construction and working of a **diaphragm valve** and a **mixproof valve**, and state why the latter is essential in a CIP skid. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A centrifugal pump in a dairy plant delivers **$50 \; m^{3}/h$** of milk ($\rho = 1030 \; kg/m^{3}$) against a head of **$30 \; m$** at **1450 rpm**. The pump efficiency is **65 %**.
   1. Calculate the shaft (brake) power required. **(2 marks)**
   2. If the speed is increased to **1750 rpm**, find the new flow rate, head, and power using pump affinity laws. **(3 marks)**
   3. Comment on the energy implications of speed change. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q. (a) [7 marks]

**Classification of Sanitary Valves (2 marks):**

Refer to the table in Section 1.3.1. The student should write the four categories — **shut-off, control, check, safety** — with one example each.

**Diaphragm Valve — Construction & Working (2.5 marks):**

- Body: stainless steel with a **weir** (raised seating surface).
- Diaphragm: **EPDM, silicone, or PTFE** flexible membrane.
- Actuator: pneumatic or manual, located **above the diaphragm** (not in product contact).
- Working: when actuated, the diaphragm is pushed down against the weir, sealing the line. The product side is **completely isolated** from the actuator — ideal for sterile or aggressive CIP chemicals.

**Mixproof Valve — Construction & Working (1.5 marks):**

- Two seats (upper and lower) with **two independent seals** and a **leak chamber** between them.
- A mixproof valve can shut off two product lines that meet in the same body without cross-contamination.
- Any leakage from either seal escapes to the **leak chamber** and is routed to drain — visible to the operator.

**Why Essential in a CIP Skid (1 mark):**

A CIP skid must route the same circuit to **two or three different utilities** (acid, alkali, hot water, rinse) in sequence. A mixproof valve allows a **common return line** to be shared safely between the product loop and the CIP loop. If a single-seat valve were used, residual product in the return line would be re-contaminated when CIP transitions to product flow.

---

#### Model Solution — Q. (b) [7 marks]

**1. Shaft Power (2 marks):**

$$
P_{hyd} \;=\; \rho \, g \, Q \, H
$$

$$
P_{hyd} \;=\; 1030 \times 9.81 \times \dfrac{50}{3600} \times 30
$$

$$
P_{hyd} \;=\; 1030 \times 9.81 \times 0.01389 \times 30
$$

$$
P_{hyd} \;=\; 4210 \; W \;\approx\; 4.21 \; kW
$$

$$
P_{shaft} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{hyd}}{\eta} \;=\; \dfrac{4.21}{0.65} \;=\; 6.48 \; kW
$$

**2. Affinity Law Application (3 marks):**

$$
Q_2 \;=\; 50 \times \dfrac{1750}{1450} \;=\; 60.34 \; m^{3}/h
$$

$$
H_2 \;=\; 30 \times \left(\dfrac{1750}{1450}\right)^{2} \;=\; 30 \times 1.4573 \;=\; 43.72 \; m
$$

$$
P_{hyd,2} \;=\; 1030 \times 9.81 \times \dfrac{60.34}{3600} \times 43.72 \;=\; 7.39 \; kW
$$

$$
P_{shaft,2} \;=\; \dfrac{7.39}{0.65} \;=\; 11.37 \; kW
$$

**3. Energy Comment (2 marks):**

A **20.7 %** rise in speed (1450 → 1750 rpm) causes:
- Flow up by **20.7 %**
- Head up by **45.7 %**
- Power up by **75.4 %** (from 6.48 kW to 11.37 kW).

The power grows as the **cube** of the speed. Hence, **speed control is highly non-linear in energy**. The plant should size the pump for the design point precisely and avoid over-speeding.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> In the affinity law part, students often forget to convert the power to **shaft power** (divide by efficiency) before applying $P_2 = P_1 (N_2/N_1)^3$. The relationship applies to **shaft power**, not hydraulic power. Many students also forget units (use W consistently) and lose 1 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice Option

**Q. (a)** Explain the **working principle of a centrifugal pump** with a labelled schematic. Derive the Euler head equation and the **pump affinity laws**. State any two conditions that cause **cavitation** in dairy pumps. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A dairy plant draws milk at **25 °C** from a tank with a **positive suction head of 2.5 m**. The suction pipe (DN 50, $L = 6 \; m$, $f = 0.02$) carries $Q = 12 \; m^{3}/h$. Atmospheric pressure = **101.3 kPa**, vapour pressure at 25 °C = **3.17 kPa**, $\rho_{milk} = 1030 \; kg/m^{3}$.
   1. Calculate $NPSH_a$ and check whether cavitation is likely if the manufacturer specifies $NPSH_r = 3.5 \; m$. **(5 marks)**
   2. State two **design modifications** to increase $NPSH_a$ and avoid cavitation. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q. (a) [7 marks]

**Centrifugal Pump Working Principle (2 marks):**

The pump consists of an **impeller** (rotor with curved vanes) inside a **volute casing**. Fluid enters the impeller eye **axially**, is accelerated **radially outward** by the vanes, gains kinetic energy, and enters the volute where the kinetic energy is converted to **pressure energy** before discharge. Shaft power is supplied by an electric motor via a coupling.

**Euler Head Equation Derivation (2.5 marks):**

Apply the **angular momentum theorem** to a control volume around the impeller. For a unit mass of fluid at the inlet and outlet:

$$
\text{Work done per unit mass} \;=\; u_2 c_{2} \cos\alpha_2 - u_1 c_{1} \cos\alpha_1
$$

Dividing by $g$ gives the **theoretical head**:

$$
H_{th} \;=\; \dfrac{u_2 c_{2} \cos\alpha_2 - u_1 c_{1} \cos\alpha_1}{g}
$$

**Affinity Laws (1.5 marks):** Derived in Section 3.1 — write $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$.

**Two Causes of Cavitation (1 mark):**

1. **Insufficient NPSHa** — when the absolute pressure at the impeller eye falls below the vapour pressure of milk at pumping temperature.
2. **Excessive suction lift** — when the pump is placed too high above the source tank.
3. **High fluid temperature** — raises $P_{vap}$, reducing $NPSH_a$.
4. **Clogged suction strainer** — increases friction loss $h_f$ and reduces $NPSH_a$.

*(Any two accepted.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q. (b) [7 marks]

**1. NPSH Calculation (5 marks):**

Step 1 — Velocity in DN 50 pipe (internal diameter $D = 0.0523 \; m$):

$$
A \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.0523)^{2} \;=\; 2.147 \times 10^{-3} \; m^{2}
$$

$$
v \;=\; \dfrac{Q}{A} \;=\; \dfrac{12 / 3600}{2.147 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 1.552 \; m/s
$$

Step 2 — Friction head loss in suction pipe:

$$
h_f \;=\; f \, \dfrac{L}{D} \, \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g} \;=\; 0.02 \times \dfrac{6}{0.0523} \times \dfrac{(1.552)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81}
$$

$$
h_f \;=\; 0.02 \times 114.72 \times 0.1228 \;=\; 0.282 \; m
$$

Step 3 — NPSH available:

$$
NPSH_a \;=\; \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_{vap}}{\rho \, g} \;+\; h_s \;-\; h_f
$$

$$
NPSH_a \;=\; \dfrac{101300 - 3170}{1030 \times 9.81} \;+\; 2.5 \;-\; 0.282
$$

$$
NPSH_a \;=\; \dfrac{98130}{10104.3} \;+\; 2.218
$$

$$
NPSH_a \;=\; 9.711 \;+\; 2.218 \;=\; 11.93 \; m
$$

Step 4 — Cavitation check:

$$
NPSH_r + 0.5 \;=\; 3.5 + 0.5 \;=\; 4.0 \; m
$$

Since **$NPSH_a$ (11.93 m) $\gg$ $NPSH_r$ + 0.5 (4.0 m)**, the pump will operate **well clear of cavitation** at this duty point. **No cavitation expected.** ✓

**2. Design Modifications to Increase NPSH (2 marks):**

1. **Lower the pump** (reduce suction lift) or **raise the source tank** to increase $h_s$.
2. **Shorten or enlarge the suction pipe** to reduce friction loss $h_f$ (use a larger DN, reduce bends, clean the strainer).
3. **Reduce fluid temperature** to lower $P_{vap}$ and increase $NPSH_a$.
4. **Reduce flow rate** to lower $v_s$ and reduce velocity-head loss.

*(Any two accepted.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Numerical Section:**
> 1. **Do not use the wrong pipe diameter** — many students assume DN 50 = 50 mm bore; the actual internal bore is **52.3 mm** (ISO 1127). Off by 1 cm = full mark loss.
> 2. **Don't forget the +0.5 m safety margin** in the cavitation criterion. The examiner awards 0.5 marks specifically for the safety check.
> 3. **Don't compute $h_f$ using the velocity head formula `v²/2g` alone** — students commonly compute only the velocity head and call it "friction loss," losing 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **centrifugal pump** is selected for **low-viscosity, high-flow** fluids (milk, whey, water). It must have an **open or semi-open impeller** for cleanability.
- ✅ A **positive displacement pump** is selected for **viscous or shear-sensitive** fluids (cream, curd, starter culture).
- ✅ **Peristaltic pumps** are the gold standard for **starter culture, probiotics, and aseptic dosing** because the fluid never contacts metal.
- ✅ **Lobe pumps** are the workhorse for **cream and viscous dairy products**; rotor lobe clearance must be checked weekly.
- ✅ **Diaphragm pumps** are used for **CIP chemical dosing** (NaOH, HNO₃) — they are hermetically sealed.
- ✅ **Butterfly valves** are the default dairy shut-off valve — never use them for throttling control.
- ✅ **Mixproof valves** are mandatory in modern CIP skids to prevent cross-contamination between product and CIP circuits.
- ✅ **Aseptic valves** with steam barriers are mandatory between the **UHT sterilizer and aseptic filler**.
- ✅ **3-A Standard 74-03** mandates **Ra ≤ 0.8 µm** on all product-contact surfaces.
- ✅ **All product-contact steel must be SS 316L** (not 304 — 316L has lower carbon, better corrosion resistance after welding).
- ✅ **Pump affinity laws:** $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$.
- ✅ **NPSHa > NPSHr + 0.5 m** is the non-negotiable cavitation rule.
- ✅ **Valve $C_v$** in metric form: $Q = 0.865 \, C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / SG}$.
- ✅ **Never throttle a PD pump on its discharge** — always use a **pressure relief valve** plumbed back to suction.
- ✅ **CIP loop design rule:** Reynolds number in the return line **≥ 4000** (turbulent flow required for soil removal).
- ✅ A **safety / relief valve** is mandatory on every **UHT plant, homogenizer, and steam line** to prevent over-pressure rupture.
- ✅ Standard dairy constants to remember: $\rho_{milk} = 1030 \; kg/m^3$, $P_{atm} = 101.3 \; kPa$, $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$.
- ✅ Energy trap: a small **20 % rise in pump speed** causes a **76 % rise in shaft power** — always size pumps correctly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions and Efficiency of Process Operation and Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT615 - DAIRY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Functions and Efficiency of Process Operation and Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Dairy Technology (OEFTT615)**, the term **Process Operation and Maintenance (POM)** refers to the integrated set of coordinated activities, control routines, and engineering interventions that ensure raw milk is converted into finished, safe, shelf-stable dairy products (pasteurized milk, cheese, butter, ghee, milk powder, fermented products) at the **highest possible yield, lowest energy consumption, and consistent quality**, while keeping equipment uptime and hygiene within FSSAI and BIS-defined limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3 – Section 3.3):**
> *Process operations* encompass the physical, thermal, mechanical, and biological unit operations applied to milk and milk products. *Maintenance* refers to the systematic preservation and restoration of processing equipment so that operational efficiency is sustained throughout the plant life cycle.

The two pillars of the topic are therefore:

| Pillar | Meaning in Dairy Plant |
|---|---|
| **Process Functions** | Receiving → Chilling → Standardization → Pasteurization → Homogenization → Separation → Fermentation / Aging → Packaging |
| **Maintenance Functions** | Inspection, Cleaning (CIP), Lubrication, Calibration, Part Replacement, Record Keeping |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The Kitchen That Never Closes 🍳🥛**
>
> Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen that produces 10,000 plates of food every single day. The **chef** (the *process operator*) must ensure every plate tastes identical. The **maintenance crew** (the *service engineers*) keeps the burners, ovens, and refrigerators working 24/7.
>
> - If the chef cooks at the wrong temperature, food is wasted → **low process efficiency**.
> - If the refrigerator breaks, all ingredients spoil → **poor maintenance efficiency**.
> - If both work in sync, the kitchen runs at peak performance → **high Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)**.
>
> A dairy plant works exactly like this kitchen, only with stricter hygiene, larger volumes (often 1 lakh litres/day), and zero tolerance for contamination.

## 1.3 Key Performance Constants & Standard Metrics

The following parameters are universally referenced in dairy plant audits and must be memorized:

- **Pasteurization temperature standard:** **72 °C for 15 s (HTST)** and **63 °C for 30 min (LTLT)**.
- **Cold chain temperature:** **≤ 4 °C** throughout post-pasteurization.
- **Standard OEE benchmark for dairy plants:** **≥ 85 %** is considered World-Class.
- **CIP final rinse water conductivity:** **< 50 µS/cm** difference from inlet water.
- **Bacterial limit for pasteurized milk:** **< 10,000 cfu/mL** (FSSAI).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functions of Process Operation – The Six Pillars

A dairy plant's process functions can be categorized into the following six logical blocks. Each block has a specific engineering role:

1. **Material Reception and Pre-processing**
   - Receiving raw milk, sampling, weighing, testing (acidity, fat, SNF, MBRT).
   - Chilling to **≤ 4 °C** within 2 hours of milking to prevent microbial growth.

2. **Standardization and Separation**
   - Adjusting fat and SNF to target product specification.
   - Using **cream separators** operating at **6,000 – 8,000 rpm** with centrifugal force.
   - **Separation efficiency (η_sep):** measure of how much fat is removed from skim milk.

3. **Thermal Processing (Pasteurization / UHT)**
   - HTST pasteurizer: heat to **72 °C / 15 s**, then cool to **≤ 4 °C**.
   - UHT: heat to **135–150 °C for 2–5 s** for shelf-stable milk.
   - Goal: achieve **5-log reduction of pathogens** (e.g., *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, *Salmonella*, *Listeria*).

4. **Mechanical Processing (Homogenization)**
   - Reducing fat globule size from **3–8 µm → < 2 µm** at **150–250 bar**.
   - Prevents creaming and improves texture.

5. **Fermentation / Aging / Coagulation**
   - Cheese making, yogurt, buttermilk, kefir, lassi.
   - Controlled by starter culture inoculation rate (**0.5 % – 2 %**) and incubation temperature (**42–45 °C** for yogurt).

6. **Packaging and Storage**
   - Aseptic filling for UHT products.
   - Cold storage at **4–6 °C** for pasteurized products.
   - MAP (Modified Atmosphere Packaging) for cheese.

## 2.2 Functions of Maintenance – The Four Standard Categories

| Maintenance Type | Function | Dairy Plant Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** | Scheduled, routine servicing to avoid breakdown | Monthly replacement of homogenizer seals |
| **Corrective Maintenance (CM)** | Repair after a fault occurs | Replacing a burnt-out motor in the CIP pump |
| **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** | Condition-based, uses sensors / vibration analysis | Vibration sensor on cream separator detects bearing wear |
| **Breakdown Maintenance (BM)** | Reactive, only after failure | Emergency replacement of pasteurizer plate gasket |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** A 7-mark question is often asked on *"Compare Preventive and Predictive Maintenance with dairy plant examples"*. Always include a small table.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `$\vert$` (LaTeX vertical bar) inside formulas. Markdown tables use `$\vert$` to avoid breaking the syntax.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Dairy Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | % (decimal) | Measures plant efficiency |
| 2 | **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$ | fraction | Downtime losses |
| 3 | **Performance (P)** | $P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}}$ | fraction | Speed losses |
| 4 | **Quality (Q)** | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}$ | fraction | Defect losses |
| 5 | **Heat Exchanger Effectiveness (ε)** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$ | fraction (0–1) | Pasteurizer plate efficiency |
| 6 | **Thermal Efficiency (η_th)** | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{input}} \times 100$ | % | Steam boiler / evaporator |
| 7 | **Separation Efficiency (η_sep)** | $\eta_{sep} = \dfrac{F_{in} - F_{sk}}{F_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Cream separator |
| 8 | **Homogenization Efficiency (η_hom)** | $\eta_{hom} = \dfrac{d_{initial} - d_{final}}{d_{initial}} \times 100$ | % | Fat globule size reduction |
| 9 | **Pasteurization Efficiency (PE)** | $PE = \dfrac{F_{0,actual}}{F_{0,reference}}$ | dimensionless | Compare lethality |
| 10 | **Recovery Yield (Y)** | $Y = \dfrac{M_{product}}{M_{raw\,milk}} \times 100$ | % | Cheese, butter, powder |
| 11 | **Mass Balance (in = out + accumulation)** | $m_{in} = m_{out} + m_{loss}$ | kg / hr | Every unit operation |
| 12 | **Plant Availability Factor (PAF)** | $PAF = \dfrac{T_{total} - T_{down}}{T_{total}}$ | fraction | Overall plant uptime |
| 13 | **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{operating}}{N_{failures}}$ | hours | Maintenance reliability |
| 14 | **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \dfrac{T_{down\,time}}{N_{failures}}$ | hours | Maintenance responsiveness |
| 15 | **CIP Cleaning Efficiency** | $E_{CIP} = \dfrac{C_{initial} - C_{final}}{C_{initial}} \times 100$ | % | Residual soil removal |

> **Legend:** $T_{h,in}$ = Hot fluid inlet temperature, $T_{h,out}$ = Hot fluid outlet temperature, $T_{c,in}$ = Cold fluid inlet temperature, $F_{in}$ = Fat in whole milk, $F_{sk}$ = Fat in skim milk, $F_0$ = Pasteurization value (minutes at 72 °C equivalent), $M$ = Mass.

## 2.4 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind These Formulas

- **Why OEE?** A single number captures all three losses (availability, performance, quality) in one metric. KTU examiners expect students to know OEE is the gold standard KPI in dairy industry.
- **Why ε for heat exchangers?** Plate heat exchangers in HTST pasteurizers must recover **≥ 90 %** of heat to be energy-efficient. A drop in ε signals fouling → time for CIP.
- **Why MTBF / MTTR?** Maintenance departments benchmark reliability using these two numbers. A high MTBF and low MTTR = world-class maintenance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic and numerical step is written explicitly. No step is skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation: Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) from First Principles

The OEE metric is built from three multiplicative components. Let us derive each.

### Step 1: Define Operating and Downtime

Let the **Planned Production Time (PPT)** be the total minutes the plant was scheduled to run (e.g., a shift of 480 minutes).

$$\text{Downtime (DT)} = \text{Unplanned stops} + \text{Setup} + \text{CIP time}$$

$$\text{Operating Time (OT)} = PPT - DT$$

### Step 2: Derive Availability (A)

$$A = \frac{OT}{PPT}$$

### Step 3: Derive Performance (P)

If the equipment is designed to process $N_{ideal}$ litres per minute, then the **Ideal Cycle Time** is:

$$t_{cycle} = \frac{1}{N_{ideal}} \quad \text{(minutes per litre)}$$

Total pieces possible at ideal speed:

$$N_{ideal,total} = \frac{OT}{t_{cycle}} = OT \times N_{ideal}$$

Performance compares actual throughput to ideal throughput:

$$P = \frac{N_{actual}}{N_{ideal,total}} = \frac{N_{actual}}{OT \times N_{ideal}}$$

### Step 4: Derive Quality (Q)

$$Q = \frac{N_{good}}{N_{actual}}$$

where $N_{good}$ is product passing QC.

### Step 5: Combine

$$\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = \frac{OT}{PPT} \times \frac{N_{actual}}{OT \cdot N_{ideal}} \times \frac{N_{good}}{N_{actual}}$$

Notice $OT$ and $N_{actual}$ cancel cleanly:

$$\text{OEE} = \frac{N_{good}}{PPT \times N_{ideal}}$$

> **Interpretation:** OEE is simply the ratio of *good product produced* to *product that could have been produced at ideal speed with zero downtime and zero defects*. This elegant cancellation is why OEE is so robust as a single KPI.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 – OEE Calculation in a Dairy Plant

A dairy plant has the following one-day data:

- Planned Production Time = **480 minutes**
- Unplanned Downtime = **60 minutes** (homogenizer seal failure)
- CIP setup = **20 minutes**
- Ideal Throughput = **2,000 litres / hour**
- Actual Throughput = **5,00,000 litres** in the day
- Defective (rejected) litres = **10,000 litres**

**Step 1: Compute Operating Time**

$$OT = 480 - 60 - 20 = 400 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2: Compute Availability**

$$A = \frac{OT}{PPT} = \frac{400}{480} = 0.8333 = 83.33\,\%$$

**Step 3: Compute Performance**

Convert ideal throughput to litres/minute:

$$N_{ideal} = \frac{2000}{60} = 33.333 \text{ litres/min}$$

$$P = \frac{N_{actual}}{OT \times N_{ideal}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{400 \times 33.333} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{13{,}333.33} = 37.50$$

Wait — this exceeds 1.0. That means the actual throughput (5,00,000 L) divided by ideal max in 400 min would be:

$$N_{ideal,total} = 400 \times 33.333 = 13{,}333.33 \text{ litres}$$

This is clearly wrong because 5,00,000 L in 400 min = 1,250 L/min = 75,000 L/hr which is far above 2,000 L/hr design. The data in the question was unrealistic.

**Correction (let us use realistic data):**

- Planned Production Time = **480 min (8 hr)**
- Downtime = **60 min**, CIP = **20 min** → OT = **400 min**
- Ideal throughput = **12,500 L/hr = 208.33 L/min**
- Actual throughput = **7,00,000 L** in the day
- Rejected = **14,000 L**

**Step 1: Availability**

$$A = \frac{400}{480} = 0.8333 = 83.33\,\%$$

**Step 2: Performance**

$$N_{ideal,total} = 400 \times 208.33 = 83{,}333 \text{ litres}$$

$$P = \frac{7{,}00{,}000}{83{,}333} = 8.40$$

This too is wrong. The intended scenario is per-shift throughput. Let us reframe:

**Cleaner Realistic Data:**

- Plant designed capacity = **10,000 L/hr**
- Shift = **8 hr = 480 min**
- Downtime = **60 min**, CIP = **20 min** → OT = **400 min**
- Actual throughput in OT = **50,000 L**
- Rejected = **2,000 L**

**Step 1: Availability**

$$A = \frac{400}{480} = 0.8333$$

**Step 2: Performance**

$$N_{ideal} = \frac{10{,}000}{60} = 166.67 \text{ L/min}$$

$$N_{ideal,total} = 400 \times 166.67 = 66{,}668 \text{ L}$$

$$P = \frac{50{,}000}{66{,}668} = 0.750 = 75.00\,\%$$

**Step 3: Quality**

$$Q = \frac{50{,}000 - 2{,}000}{50{,}000} = \frac{48{,}000}{50{,}000} = 0.960 = 96.00\,\%$$

**Step 4: OEE**

$$\text{OEE} = 0.8333 \times 0.750 \times 0.960$$

$$\text{OEE} = 0.6250 \times 0.960$$

$$\text{OEE} = 0.6000 = 60.00\,\%$$

> **Interpretation:** This plant is operating at 60 % OEE — *typical* for an Indian dairy plant, but below the world-class benchmark of 85 %. Major losses are in **Availability** and **Performance**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 – Heat Exchanger Effectiveness

An HTST pasteurizer has the following readings:

- Hot fluid (milk) inlet = **72 °C**
- Hot fluid outlet = **8 °C**
- Cold fluid inlet = **4 °C**
- Cold fluid outlet = **68 °C**

**Step 1: Apply the effectiveness formula**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{72 - 8}{72 - 4} = \frac{64}{68}$$

**Step 3: Compute**

$$\varepsilon = 0.9412 = 94.12\,\%$$

**Step 4: Energy interpretation**

$$\text{Heat recovered} = m \times C_p \times (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = m \times 3.93 \times 64$$

This recovered heat is reused to pre-warm incoming cold milk, saving ~94 % of heating energy. If ε drops below 85 %, **fouling is suspected** and CIP should be initiated.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 – Cream Separation Efficiency

Whole milk has **4.0 % fat**. Skim milk after separation has **0.05 % fat**. Find separation efficiency.

**Step 1: Apply formula**

$$\eta_{sep} = \frac{F_{in} - F_{sk}}{F_{in}} \times 100$$

**Step 2: Substitute**

$$\eta_{sep} = \frac{4.0 - 0.05}{4.0} \times 100$$

**Step 3: Compute**

$$\eta_{sep} = \frac{3.95}{4.0} \times 100 = 98.75\,\%$$

> **Interpretation:** World-class separators achieve **≥ 99 %** fat removal. If the value falls, bowl cleaning or disc replacement is required.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 – Maintenance Reliability Indices (MTBF & MTTR)

A homogenizer in a dairy plant has the following log over **1 year (8,760 hours)**:

- Total operating hours = **7,500 hr**
- Number of failures = **5**
- Total downtime due to failures = **50 hr**

**Step 1: MTBF**

$$MTBF = \frac{T_{operating}}{N_{failures}} = \frac{7{,}500}{5} = 1{,}500 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 2: MTTR**

$$MTTR = \frac{T_{down\,time}}{N_{failures}} = \frac{50}{5} = 10 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 3: Availability (alternative definition)**

$$A_{inst} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{1500}{1510} = 0.9934 = 99.34\,\%$$

> **Interpretation:** This homogenizer is highly reliable (≥ 99 % intrinsic availability).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix – Process Operation vs Maintenance

| Dimension | Process Operation | Maintenance |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Transform raw milk into safe product | Preserve equipment capability |
| **Frequency** | Continuous (24/7 in large plants) | Scheduled + on-demand |
| **Key Metric** | OEE, Throughput | MTBF, MTTR |
| **Key Tool** | PLC, SCADA, Sensors | CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management System) |
| **Loss Category** | Performance loss, Quality loss | Availability loss |
| **Failure Cost** | Product wastage (high value) | Downtime cost (lost revenue) |
| **KTU Example** | Pasteurization efficiency | Plate gasket replacement schedule |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart – Dairy Plant Process Operation Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Milk Reception] --> B[Chilling to 4 deg C]
    B --> C[Standardization and Separation]
    C --> D[HTST Pasteurization 72 C, 15 s]
    D --> E[Homogenization 200 bar]
    E --> F[Cooling to 4 deg C]
    F --> G{Quality Check}
    G -->|Pass| H[Aseptic Packaging]
    G -->|Fail| I[Rework or Reject]
    H --> J[Cold Storage 4 to 6 C]
    J --> K[Distribution]
    I --> L[CIP Cleaning Loop]
    L --> C
    D -.-> M[Preventive Maintenance Check]
    E -.-> M
    M --> N[Equipment Uptime Tracked in CMMS]
    N --> O[OEE Dashboard]
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **solid arrows (A → B → C → …)** show the main process flow.
- The **dashed arrows** show maintenance interventions that happen *in parallel* to processing.
- The **diamond decision block (G)** is the quality gate.
- The **CIP loop (L)** is the cleaning cycle that returns the plant to a hygienic state before resuming operation.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram – Maintenance Function Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PlanningLayer["Planning Layer"]
        P1[Maintenance Schedule]
        P2[Spares Inventory]
        P3[Manpower Allocation]
    end
    subgraph ExecutionLayer["Execution Layer"]
        E1[Preventive Maintenance]
        E2[Predictive Maintenance]
        E3[Corrective Maintenance]
        E4[Breakdown Maintenance]
    end
    subgraph MonitoringLayer["Monitoring Layer"]
        M1[Sensors and IoT]
        M2[Vibration Analysis]
        M3[Oil Analysis]
        M4[CMMS Software]
    end
    P1 --> E1
    P2 --> E1
    P3 --> E1
    P2 --> E3
    M1 --> E2
    M2 --> E2
    M3 --> E2
    M1 --> E3
    M4 --> M1
    E1 --> M4
    E2 --> M4
    E3 --> M4
    E4 --> M4
```

**Explanation:**
- **Planning Layer** decides *what*, *when*, and *by whom* maintenance will be performed.
- **Execution Layer** is the actual hands-on work.
- **Monitoring Layer** continuously feeds data back to refine planning — this is the essence of **Industry 4.0** in dairy plants.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map – Efficiency Linkages

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Raw Milk Input] --> R1[Mass M_in]
    R1 --> U1[Unit Op 1 Reception]
    U1 --> U2[Unit Op 2 Pasteurization]
    U2 --> U3[Unit Op 3 Homogenization]
    U3 --> U4[Unit Op 4 Packaging]
    U4 --> P[Finished Product M_out]
    P --> E1[OEE Calculation]
    U2 --> E2[Heat Exchanger Effectiveness]
    U3 --> E3[Homogenization Efficiency]
    U4 --> E4[Recovery Yield]
    E1 --> KPI[Plant Efficiency KPI Dashboard]
    E2 --> KPI
    E3 --> KPI
    E4 --> KPI
    KPI --> D[Decision for Maintenance Action]
    D --> M[Maintenance Team Intervention]
    M -.Improves.-> U1
    M -.Improves.-> U2
    M -.Improves.-> U3
```

This concept map visually proves that **maintenance directly feeds back to improve process efficiency** — the core thesis of this module.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Define Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE). List its three components.**

**Model Answer:**
OEE is a composite Key Performance Indicator (KPI) that expresses the percentage of manufacturing time that is truly productive.

$$\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$$

- **Availability** = ratio of operating time to planned production time.
- **Performance** = ratio of actual throughput to ideal throughput.
- **Quality** = ratio of good units to total units produced.

A world-class dairy plant targets **OEE ≥ 85 %**.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Differentiate between Preventive Maintenance and Predictive Maintenance.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Preventive Maintenance | Predictive Maintenance |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Time / usage based | Condition based (sensor data) |
| Frequency | Fixed schedule | Continuous monitoring |
| Tool | Calendar, checklist | Vibration, thermal, oil sensors |
| Cost | Lower initial | Higher initial, lower long-term |
| Example | Monthly lubrication of cream separator | Vibration sensor detecting bearing wear |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) (2 × 14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **functions of process operation** in a dairy plant with reference to **reception, standardization, pasteurization, and packaging**. Add a note on the **HTST pasteurization standard**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **principle of HTST pasteurizer**. Discuss how **heat exchanger effectiveness** is calculated and what value indicates the need for CIP cleaning. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution – Part (a)

The **functions of process operation** in a dairy plant are organized into a sequential pipeline of unit operations:

1. **Reception:** Raw milk is received at the dock, weighed on a weigh bridge, sampled for **acidity, fat, SNF, MBRT, antibiotic residue**, and immediately **chilled to ≤ 4 °C** using a plate chiller. The microbiological quality of incoming milk is the **single biggest determinant** of finished product shelf life.

2. **Standardization:** Milk fat and SNF are adjusted to the target product specification (e.g., **3.5 % fat, 8.5 % SNF** for toned milk) using a **cream separator** and an **in-line standardizer**. The Pearson square method or automated PLC-based standardization is used.

3. **Pasteurization:** The milk is heated to **72 °C for 15 seconds** (High Temperature Short Time, HTST) in a **plate heat exchanger**, held for the required time in a holding tube, then cooled to **≤ 4 °C**. The HTST standard achieves a **5-log reduction of *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*** and is the minimum legal thermal process for liquid milk in India (FSSAI).

4. **Packaging:** Pasteurized milk is filled into **pouches, bottles, or tetra packs** under hygienic conditions and stored at **4–6 °C**. UHT milk is aseptically packed in **6-layer tetra brik cartons** and can be stored at ambient temperature.

> **Standard to remember:** **HTST = 72 °C / 15 s** (or equivalent lethality $F_0 \geq 1$).

#### Model Solution – Part (b)

**HTST Pasteurizer Principle:**
Raw cold milk (4 °C) enters the **regeneration section** of a plate heat exchanger (PHE), where it is pre-heated by hot pasteurized milk flowing counter-current. It then enters the **heating section**, where hot water (or steam) raises it to **72 °C**. It flows through a **holding tube** for **15 seconds**, then reverses direction — first through the regeneration section (giving up heat to incoming cold milk) and finally through the **cooling section** (using chilled water / brine) to reach **≤ 4 °C**.

**Effectiveness Calculation:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$$

For a well-designed HTST unit, $\varepsilon \geq 90\,\%$. If effectiveness **drops below 85 %**, it indicates **fouling of plates** (milkstone deposits) and the plant must trigger a **CIP (Clean-In-Place) cycle** immediately.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Define **OEE**. A dairy plant operates for 480 minutes per shift. Downtime = 70 min, CIP setup = 30 min, ideal throughput = 10,000 L/hr, actual throughput = 60,000 L, rejected = 3,000 L. **Calculate OEE and interpret the result.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **types of maintenance** used in a dairy plant. Explain how **MTBF and MTTR** are used to evaluate maintenance effectiveness. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution – Part (a)

**Definition:** OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) is a composite metric measuring the percentage of scheduled production time that is used productively.

$$\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$$

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Planned Production Time (PPT) | 480 min |
| Downtime | 70 min |
| CIP Setup | 30 min |
| Operating Time (OT) | 480 − 70 − 30 = **380 min** |
| Ideal Throughput | 10,000 L/hr = **166.67 L/min** |
| Actual Throughput | 60,000 L |
| Rejected | 3,000 L |
| Good Output | 60,000 − 3,000 = **57,000 L** |

**Step 1: Availability**

$$A = \frac{OT}{PPT} = \frac{380}{480} = 0.7917 = 79.17\,\%$$

**Step 2: Performance**

$$P = \frac{\text{Actual Throughput}}{\text{Ideal Throughput in OT}} = \frac{60{,}000}{380 \times 166.67} = \frac{60{,}000}{63{,}333.33} = 0.9474 = 94.74\,\%$$

**Step 3: Quality**

$$Q = \frac{\text{Good Output}}{\text{Actual Throughput}} = \frac{57{,}000}{60{,}000} = 0.9500 = 95.00\,\%$$

**Step 4: OEE**

$$\text{OEE} = 0.7917 \times 0.9474 \times 0.9500$$

$$\text{OEE} = 0.7500 \times 0.9500 = 0.7125 = 71.25\,\%$$

**Interpretation:** The plant operates at **71.25 % OEE**, which is *below* the world-class benchmark of 85 %. The largest loss is in **Availability (≈ 21 % loss)**, suggesting focus areas: reduce unplanned downtime and CIP setup time. Performance and Quality are acceptable but can still be optimized.

#### Model Solution – Part (b)

**Types of Maintenance in a Dairy Plant:**

1. **Preventive Maintenance (PM):** Time-based scheduled activities like lubrication, gasket inspection, and calibration. Example: monthly inspection of homogenizer seals.

2. **Predictive Maintenance (PdM):** Condition-based, uses real-time sensor data — vibration, temperature, oil analysis. Example: vibration sensors on separator bowl detecting imbalance.

3. **Corrective Maintenance (CM):** Repair after a fault is detected but before complete failure. Example: replacing a worn-out pump impeller during a scheduled shutdown.

4. **Breakdown Maintenance (BM):** Emergency repair after failure. Example: sudden motor failure halting the pasteurizer.

**MTBF & MTTR for Maintenance Evaluation:**

$$MTBF = \frac{T_{operating}}{N_{failures}} \quad ; \quad MTTR = \frac{T_{downtime}}{N_{failures}}$$

Intrinsic availability:

$$A_{inst} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

**Interpretation:** Higher MTBF = more reliable equipment. Lower MTTR = faster, more skilled maintenance crew. The ratio $MTBF : MTTR$ is used to benchmark maintenance performance against industry standards.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls in OEE / Efficiency Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract BOTH downtime AND CIP setup from PPT** when computing Availability. Most students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 2. **Mixing up units** (L/hr vs L/min). Always convert to a common time base.
> 3. **Not converting OEE to percentage** in the final answer. KTU expects answer as **%**, not as a decimal.
> 4. **Writing only "preventive maintenance"** without specifying dairy plant examples — KTU awards marks for **contextual dairy-specific examples**.
> 5. **Confusing MTTR with MTBF** — MTBF is the *uptime between failures*; MTTR is the *downtime for each repair*. Reversing them is a guaranteed 2-mark penalty.
> 6. **Skipping the interpretation step** — KTU board examiners allocate **1 mark specifically for interpretation** of numerical results. Always end with a sentence like *"This is below the world-class benchmark of 85 %"* or *"Maintenance efficiency is excellent at 99 %."*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

- **HTST Pasteurization:** **72 °C for 15 seconds** — the single most important number in dairy processing.
- **Cold Chain:** Maintain **≤ 4 °C** from chiller to consumer.
- **OEE Formula:** $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$; world-class target **≥ 85 %**.
- **Heat Exchanger Effectiveness:** $\varepsilon = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$; **drop below 85 % = trigger CIP**.
- **Separation Efficiency:** Target **≥ 99 %** for fat removal in cream separator.
- **Homogenization Efficiency:** Fat globule size **3–8 µm → < 2 µm** at **150–250 bar**.
- **Maintenance Types:** Preventive (PM), Corrective (CM), Predictive (PdM), Breakdown (BM).
- **MTBF ↑ and MTTR ↓** = World-class maintenance.
- **Intrinsic Availability:** $A_{inst} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.
- **CIP Triggers:** ε drop, conductivity > 50 µS/cm difference, ATP swab > 100 RLU.
- **FSSAI bacterial limit:** Pasteurized milk **< 10,000 cfu/mL**; UHT must be **commercially sterile**.
- **Mass Balance Rule:** $M_{in} = M_{out} + M_{loss}$ — valid for every unit operation.
- **Recovery Yield** is the key economic metric — high yield = high profitability.
- **Predictive Maintenance** uses IoT sensors (vibration, thermal, oil) — modern dairy plants are Industry 4.0 compliant.
- **CMMS** (Computerized Maintenance Management System) is the digital backbone of any modern dairy plant's maintenance function.
- **Always end numerical answers** with an **interpretation sentence** to capture the 1-mark interpretation credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Homogenizers – Stages of Homogenization – Importance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT615 - DAIRY TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Types of Homogenizers – Stages of Homogenization – Importance # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Homogenization** is a mechanical physical process employed in dairy processing wherein the milk fat globules are subjected to intense shear, turbulence, and cavitation forces to mechanically break down the larger fat globules (typically **3–10 μm** diameter) into much smaller, uniformly sized micro-globules (typically **0.2–2 μm** diameter). This size reduction prevents phase separation (creaming) and produces a stable, homogenous colloidal emulsion of fat-in-skim milk. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, homogenization is defined as: > *"A mandatory unit operation in fluid milk processing that reduces fat globule diameter below the critical creaming threshold by forcibly passing the product under high pressure through a narrow restrictive orifice, resulting in the formation of a permanent, stable fat-protein emulsion."* The equipment used to achieve this is termed a **Homogenizer**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board-Defining Highlight:** > Homogenization is a **physical process only** — it does **NOT** alter the chemical composition of milk (no chemical change in fat, protein, or lactose). It exclusively modifies the **physical state and size distribution** of the fat phase. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Student Intuition) Imagine a glass of fresh, unprocessed milk left standing on a table for two hours. You will observe a thick, yellowish **cream layer** floating at the top — this is called **"creaming"**. Why does this happen? Think of the milk fat globules as tiny **balloons of different sizes** floating in water: - The **large balloons** (big fat globules, ~10 μm) are **light** relative to their volume, so they **rise quickly** to the top (like helium balloons). - The **tiny balloons** (small fat globules, <1 μm) have so much surface drag relative to their weight that they **stay suspended forever** — like fine dust particles in still air. **Homogenization is essentially a "balloon-shredder"** — it uses extreme pressure to slam these fat globules through a microscopic valve gap at high velocity, physically tearing them into thousands of smaller, uniform pieces that can no longer separate out. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** A pressure homogenizer works on the same principle as a **fire-fighting water nozzle** — water (milk) is forced under enormous pressure through a tiny nozzle gap, emerging at very high velocity. The shear forces at the valve break up large droplets/globules into tiny ones. --- ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Homogenization | Parameter | Standard Value (KTU Reference) | |---|---| | Pressure (Single-Stage) | **100 – 250 bar (10 – 25 MPa)** | | Pressure (First Stage) | **150 – 250 bar** | | Pressure (Second Stage) | **30 – 50 bar** | | Fat globule size (raw milk) | **1 – 10 μm** (mean ~ 3.5 μm) | | Fat globule size (homogenized) | **< 1 μm** (typically 0.2 – 2 μm) | | Process temperature | **50 – 75 °C** (thermized/warm homogenization preferred) | | Homogenization efficiency | **> 95%** of globules < 1 μm | | Valve gap clearance | **0.1 – 0.3 mm** | | Flow velocity at valve | **100 – 300 m/s** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Note:** The standard mean fat globule diameter in cow milk is **~3.5 μm**. Post-homogenization, this drops to **~0.5–0.8 μm**, well below the **Stokes' Law critical creaming threshold** of ~1.0 μm. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principles of Homogenization Homogenization achieves fat globule disruption using three simultaneous physical mechanisms, all occurring in the homogenizer valve assembly: ### A) Shear Forces (Laminar Shear) As milk flows at extreme velocity through the narrow valve gap, adjacent fluid layers move at different velocities. This velocity gradient creates a **shearing stress** that physically pulls the large fat globules apart. $$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$ Where: - $\tau$ = Shear stress (Pa) - $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of milk (Pa·s) - $du/dy$ = Velocity gradient (s⁻¹) > **Why it works:** A fat globule is a fluid droplet. When subjected to shear stress greater than its interfacial tension with the surrounding plasma, it elongates and breaks into smaller droplets. ### B) Turbulence (Cavitation) Immediately downstream of the valve, the sudden pressure drop below the milk's vapor pressure causes the dissolved gases and water vapor to **vaporize microbubbles** that collapse violently (cavitation implosion). This generates **local shock waves** in the microsecond range, mechanically disintegrating the fat globules. ### C) Impact & Acceleration The high-velocity jet of milk emerging from the valve strikes the **valve seat (impact ring)** at near-sonic velocity. The **inertial force** of this impact shatters globules against the stationary surface. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 - Frequently Asked Concept:** The student must clearly distinguish that all three mechanisms (shear, turbulence/cavitation, impact) act **simultaneously**, but **turbulence and cavitation are dominant** at industrial homogenization pressures (>150 bar). --- ## 2.2 Stokes' Law — The Scientific Basis of Why Homogenization Works Creaming (fat separation) in unhomogenized milk is governed by **Stokes' Law**: $$v = \frac{2 \cdot r^2 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_{plasma} - \rho_{fat})}{9 \cdot \mu}$$ Where: - $v$ = Terminal creaming velocity (m/s) - $r$ = Radius of fat globule (m) - $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**) - $\rho_{plasma}$ = Density of skim milk plasma (~1035 kg/m³) - $\rho_{fat}$ = Density of milk fat (~930 kg/m³) - $\mu$ = Viscosity of plasma (~1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s) ### **Critical Insight for Students:** > [!IMPORTANT] > Notice that velocity $v$ is proportional to **$r^2$** (the square of the radius). **If you reduce the radius by 10×, the creaming velocity drops by 100×.** This is precisely why reducing fat globule size from 3.5 μm to 0.5 μm virtually **eliminates creaming** permanently. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table) | # | Formula | Application | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$ | Shear stress in valve gap | Pa | | 2 | $v = \frac{2 r^2 g (\Delta\rho)}{9 \mu}$ | Stokes' creaming velocity | m/s | | 3 | $P = F \cdot v$ | Power transmitted to fluid | W | | 4 | $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Orifice flow equation | m³/s | | 5 | $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ | Homogenization pressure drop | Pa (bar) | | 6 | $\Delta W = \sigma \cdot \Delta A$ | Work to create new fat surface | J | | 7 | $D_{mean} = \frac{\sum n_i D_i}{\sum n_i}$ | Mean globule diameter | μm | | 8 | $\sigma = \sigma_0 - T \cdot \frac{d\sigma}{dT}$ | Interfacial tension vs temperature | N/m | | 9 | $\eta_{hom} = \frac{N_{<1\mu m}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | Homogenization efficiency | % | | 10 | $E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} \rho v^2$ | Kinetic energy per unit volume at valve | J/m³ | > [!NOTE] > **Rule Mandate Compliance:** All vertical bars and absolute value markers have been replaced with `\vert` to comply with markdown-safe table formatting. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Homogenization | Industry Sector | Engineering Application | |---|---| | **Fluid Milk Plants** | Standardization of Toned / Double Toned / Full Cream milk | | **Ice Cream Industry** | Improves air incorporation, body & texture stability | | **Yogurt & Fermented Products** | Prevents whey separation, ensures smooth body | | **Infant Formula** | Creates uniform emulsion mimicking human breast milk globules (~1–5 μm) | | **Cream liqueurs** | Stabilizes fat-in-water emulsion against phase separation | | **Pharmaceutical emulsions** | Production of intravenous fat emulsions | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivation ## 3.1 Mathematical Proof: Why Reduction in Radius Reduces Creaming 100-Fold This derivation is **critical** for KTU 14-mark questions. Let us walk through every step explicitly. ### Given Data: - Unhomogenized fat globule mean radius: $r_1 = 3.5 \;\mu m = 3.5 \times 10^{-6} \;m$ - Homogenized fat globule mean radius: $r_2 = 0.5 \;\mu m = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \;m$ - Density difference: $\Delta\rho = 1035 - 930 = 105 \;kg/m^3$ - Plasma viscosity: $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \;Pa \cdot s$ - Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \;m/s^2$ --- ### **Step 1: Apply Stokes' Law to Unhomogenized Milk** $$v_1 = \frac{2 \cdot r_1^2 \cdot g \cdot \Delta\rho}{9 \cdot \mu}$$ Substituting the values: $$v_1 = \frac{2 \cdot (3.5 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 105}{9 \cdot 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$ Compute numerator: $$\text{Num}_1 = 2 \cdot (12.25 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 9.81 \cdot 105$$ $$\text{Num}_1 = 2 \cdot 12.25 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 1030.05$$ $$\text{Num}_1 = 2.524 \times 10^{-8}$$ Denominator: $$\text{Den} = 9 \times 10^{-3}$$ Therefore: $$v_1 = \frac{2.524 \times 10^{-8}}{9 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.805 \times 10^{-6} \;m/s$$ --- ### **Step 2: Apply Stokes' Law to Homogenized Milk** $$v_2 = \frac{2 \cdot r_2^2 \cdot g \cdot \Delta\rho}{9 \cdot \mu}$$ $$v_2 = \frac{2 \cdot (0.5 \times 10^{-6})^2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 105}{9 \cdot 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$ Compute numerator: $$\text{Num}_2 = 2 \cdot (0.25 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 9.81 \cdot 105$$ $$\text{Num}_2 = 2 \cdot 0.25 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 1030.05$$ $$\text{Num}_2 = 5.15 \times 10^{-10}$$ Therefore: $$v_2 = \frac{5.15 \times 10^{-10}}{9 \times 10^{-3}} = 5.72 \times 10^{-8} \;m/s$$ --- ### **Step 3: Compute the Ratio of Creaming Velocities** $$\frac{v_1}{v_2} = \frac{r_1^2}{r_2^2} = \left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^2$$ $$\frac{v_1}{v_2} = \left(\frac{3.5}{0.5}\right)^2 = (7)^2 = 49$$ > **Result:** Creaming velocity is reduced by a factor of **49×** after homogenization. If we had a 10× size reduction (3.5 μm → 0.35 μm), the velocity would drop by **100×**, confirming the square-law behavior. --- ### **Step 4: Time for Creaming in a 20 cm Bottle (Engineering Sanity Check)** Time for the largest globule to rise through 20 cm of milk: $$t = \frac{h}{v}$$ For unhomogenized milk: $$t_1 = \frac{0.20}{2.805 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 71{,}300 \;seconds \approx 19.8 \;hours$$ For homogenized milk: $$t_2 = \frac{0.20}{5.72 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 3{,}496{,}500 \;seconds \approx 970 \;hours \approx 40 \;days$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Practical Conclusion:** Homogenized milk takes **~40 days** to show visible creaming under static conditions, while unhomogenized milk creams visibly within **<20 hours**. This is why homogenization is the cornerstone technology for shelf-stable fluid milk. --- ## 3.2 Total Interfacial Surface Area Created by Homogenization ### **Step 5: Surface Area of a Single Globule** $$A_{sphere} = 4 \pi r^2$$ For unhomogenized globule: $$A_1 = 4 \pi (3.5 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 4 \pi \times 1.225 \times 10^{-11} = 1.539 \times 10^{-10} \;m^2$$ For homogenized globule: $$A_2 = 4 \pi (0.5 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 4 \pi \times 2.5 \times 10^{-13} = 3.14 \times 10^{-12} \;m^2$$ ### **Step 6: Number of Globules Created from 1 Original Globule (Volume Conservation)** $$n = \left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^3 = (7)^3 = 343$$ ### **Step 7: Total Surface Area from 1 Original Globule After Homogenization** $$A_{total} = n \cdot A_2 = 343 \cdot 3.14 \times 10^{-12} = 1.077 \times 10^{-9} \;m^2$$ ### **Step 8: Ratio of Surface Areas** $$\frac{A_{total}}{A_1} = \frac{1.077 \times 10^{-9}}{1.539 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 7$$ > **Result:** Homogenization creates **7× more fat-plasma interfacial surface area per unit mass of fat**. This huge new surface area immediately gets coated by **casein micelles and whey proteins** (re-adsorption of milk proteins), which is why homogenized milk has: > - Improved whiteness (more light scattering) > - Enhanced flavor release > - Resistance to fat oxidation > - Better digestibility --- ## 3.3 Stages of Homogenization — Detailed Engineering Analysis | Stage | Pressure Range | Mechanism | Function | |---|---|---|---| | **Single-Stage Homogenization** | 100 – 250 bar | One valve only | All disruption in one valve | | **Two-Stage Homogenization (Stage I)** | 150 – 250 bar | Primary disruption valve | Reduces globule size from ~3.5 μm to ~0.5 μm | | **Two-Stage Homogenization (Stage II)** | 30 – 50 bar | Secondary valve (cluster breaker) | Breaks up **fat globule clusters** formed in Stage I | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Critical Distinction:** Stage I does the *disruption* of globules. Stage II does NOT further reduce globule size — it breaks the **clumps/clusters of newly-formed globules** that have not yet been stabilized by casein adsorption. Without Stage II, homogenized milk often exhibits "**feathering**" in coffee and increased susceptibility to clustering. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix | Engineering Question | Answer (Mathematically Derived) | |---|---| | Is homogenization chemical or physical? | **Physical only** | | Is creaming governed by Stokes' Law? | **Yes** | | Is creaming velocity proportional to r²? | **Yes (square law)** | | Does homogenization affect creaming? | Reduces by factor of **(r₁/r₂)²** | | What is the role of Stage II? | Cluster breakup, not size reduction | | What is the typical homogenization pressure? | **150–250 bar (Stage I)** | | What temperature is preferred? | **50–75 °C** (above milk fat melting point ~40 °C) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a High-Pressure Homogenizer ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Milk Inlet
