Mention the typical pressure and temperature used in the process."* ### **Model Answer:** **Homogenization** is a purely **physical unit operation** in dairy processing in which milk is forced under very high pressure (typically **100–250 bar**) through a narrow valve gap, breaking the native fat globules (~3.5 μm) into much smaller, uniform micro-globules (~0.5 μm). This prevents creaming and produces a permanent, stable fat-in-plasma emulsion. - **Pressure used:** **150 – 250 bar** (in Stage I of a two-stage homogenizer) - **Temperature used:** **50 – 75 °C** (kept above the melting point of milk fat, ~40 °C, so that fat is in liquid state and can be sheared efficiently) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why temperature > 40 °C?** Because milk fat is partially crystalline at lower temperatures; crystalline fat globules resist deformation and break unevenly. Above ~40 °C, all milk fat is **liquid**, allowing clean shear-disruption. --- ### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** > *"Differentiate between single-stage and two-stage homogenization."* ### **Model Answer:** | Feature | Single-Stage | Two-Stage | |---|---|---| | Number of valves | One | Two (Stage I + Stage II) | | Stage I pressure | 100–250 bar | 150–250 bar | | Stage II pressure | None | 30–50 bar | | Globule size reduction | Yes | Yes (Stage I) | | Cluster breakup | No | Yes (Stage II) | | Product quality | Risk of fat cluster formation | Cluster-free, smoother body | | Susceptibility to feathering in coffee | Higher | Lower | | Application | Standardized pasteurized milk | Premium fluid milk, ice cream mix | --- ## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ### **Question B1 — Choice A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** > **(a)** Discuss the various **types of homogenizers** used in the dairy industry with their working principles. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** Explain the **stages of homogenization** in detail with a neat sketch. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for (a) — Types of Homogenizers:** The major types of homogenizers used in the dairy industry are: **1. High-Pressure Valve Homogenizer (Most Widely Used)** - **Working Principle:** A positive displacement pump (usually triplex piston pump) forces the milk under high pressure (150–250 bar) through a precisely machined **adjustable valve gap** (~0.1–0.3 mm). The combination of **shear, turbulence, and cavitation** in this gap disrupts the fat globules. The fluid then impacts the **valve seat (impact ring)**, completing the breakup. - **Pressure range:** **100 – 250 bar** - **Application:** All commercial fluid milk, cream, ice cream mix. **2. Ultrasonic / Sonic Homogenizer** - **Working Principle:** Milk is exposed to high-frequency **ultrasonic vibrations (20 kHz)** via a piezoelectric transducer. The acoustic waves generate intense **cavitation microbubbles** that collapse and mechanically disrupt fat globules. - **Application:** Laboratory-scale and research; some specialty dairy products. **3. Microfluidizer** - **Working Principle:** Milk is accelerated at very high pressure through **microchannels arranged in Z or Y configuration** (channels ~75–100 μm wide). The flow splits and recombines at high velocity, creating extreme shear and impact forces that produce sub-micron globules. - **Application:** **Infant formula**, pharmaceutical fat emulsions, sub-micron-grade dairy products. **4. Membrane Homogenizer** - **Working Principle:** Milk is forced through a **porous ceramic or polymeric membrane** with uniform pore size under low pressure. Fat globules larger than the pore are mechanically broken; smaller ones pass intact. - **Application:** Gentle processing for heat-sensitive products. **5. Colloid Mill** - **Working Principle:** A **rotating disc** spins at high RPM (~3000–6000) within a stationary stator, creating a narrow gap (0.05–0.5 mm). Centrifugal and shear forces disrupt globules. - **Limitation:** Limited pressure (~100 bar); less effective than valve homogenizers. --- ### **Model Solution for (b) — Stages of Homogenization:** **Single-Stage Homogenization:** - Milk passes through **only one homogenizing valve** at high pressure (100–250 bar). - Globule size reduction occurs, but the newly formed globules often **re-aggregate into clusters** before being stabilized by casein adsorption. - Risk of **feathering** in coffee. **Two-Stage Homogenization (The Industrial Standard):** | Stage | Pressure | Function | |---|---|---| | **Stage I (Primary Valve)** | 150–250 bar | **Disruption** — large globules are shattered into small ones via shear, turbulence, cavitation | | **Stage II (Secondary Valve)** | 30–50 bar | **Cluster breakup** — the clusters of newly formed globules are dispersed; gives smooth body | > **Process Logic:** Stage I does the **physical work of size reduction**. Stage II prevents the **secondary problem of clustering**, ensuring the small globules remain individually suspended and coated by casein micelles. This is why two-stage homogenization is the **industry default** for premium products. **Refer to Section 4.4 Diagram** for the schematic flow. --- ### **Question B2 — Choice B (Alternative) (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** > **(a)** Explain the **importance of homogenization** in dairy processing with reference to creaming, body, texture, and digestibility. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** With the help of **Stokes' Law**, mathematically justify why homogenization prevents creaming. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for (a) — Importance of Homogenization:** 1. **Prevention of Creaming (Phase Separation):** The primary importance. Fat globule reduction below the **Stokes' critical threshold (~1 μm)** ensures the milk remains a **homogeneous single phase** for the entire shelf life (~7–14 days for pasteurized milk). 2. **Improved Whiteness & Optical Properties:** The 7-fold increase in fat-plasma interfacial area (derived in Section 3.2) increases **light scattering**, giving homogenized milk its characteristic **opaque, bright white appearance**. 3. **Enhanced Flavor Release:** Smaller fat globules have a higher **surface-area-to-volume ratio**, allowing fat-soluble flavor compounds (like lactones, short-chain fatty acids) to be perceived more easily by the taste buds. 4. **Better Mouthfeel / Body:** Homogenized milk has a **smoother, creamier mouthfeel** because the smaller globules form a more uniform emulsion, distributing the fat uniformly on the tongue. 5. **Improved Digestibility:** Smaller globules have a **larger surface area for lipase enzyme action**, making the fat more accessible to digestive enzymes. This is particularly important in **infant formula** to mimic the digestibility profile of human breast milk. 6. **Stability of Derived Products:** In **ice cream**, homogenization improves air incorporation, prevents churning during freezing, and gives a smooth texture. In **yogurt**, it prevents whey separation (syneresis). --- ### **Model Solution for (b) — Mathematical Justification via Stokes' Law:** The creaming velocity of a fat globule in milk is given by: $$v = \frac{2 \cdot r^2 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_{plasma} - \rho_{fat})}{9 \cdot \mu}$$ Let the radius ratio after homogenization be: $$k = \frac{r_{homogenized}}{r_{raw}} = \frac{0.5}{3.5} = \frac{1}{7}$$ The ratio of creaming velocities is: $$\frac{v_{hom}}{v_{raw}} = \left(\frac{r_{hom}}{r_{raw}}\right)^2 = k^2 = \frac{1}{49}$$ > **Conclusion:** The creaming velocity is reduced by a factor of **49** — meaning a fat globule now takes **49 times longer** to rise to the surface. Since creaming in raw milk is visible within ~20 hours, in homogenized milk it would take **> 40 days** under identical conditions, effectively eliminating phase separation for the duration of normal shelf life. > [!NOTE] > **Mathematical Logic:** Because the velocity depends on the **square** of the radius, even a modest reduction in size produces a dramatic suppression of creaming. This is the **physical justification** that KTU examiners expect for the 7-mark part. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > - ❌ Stating homogenization is a **chemical** process — **WRONG**; it is purely physical. > - ❌ Confusing **Stage II pressure** with Stage I — Stage II is **LOW** pressure (30–50 bar), not high. > - ❌ Writing that Stage II **further reduces** globule size — **WRONG**; Stage II only breaks **clusters**. > - ❌ Using Stokes' Law with **wrong radius** (must use **mean** diameter, not maximum). > - ❌ Forgetting to square the radius when computing velocity ratio. > - ❌ Confusing homogenization with **pasteurization** (these are two entirely different unit operations). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ Homogenization is a **physical process**, not chemical. - ✅ Typical pressure: **150 – 250 bar (Stage I)**, **30 – 50 bar (Stage II)**. - ✅ Typical temperature: **50 – 75 °C** (above milk fat melting point ~40 °C). - ✅ Fat globule size reduced from **~3.5 μm → ~0.5 μm** (>95% below 1 μm). - ✅ Creaming velocity follows Stokes' Law: **v ∝ r²**. - ✅ A 7× reduction in radius gives a **49× reduction** in creaming velocity. - ✅ Surface area of fat phase increases by **~7×** per unit mass. - ✅ **Stage I = disruption** (size reduction); **Stage II = cluster breakup** (no size reduction). - ✅ Two-stage homogenization is the **industrial standard** for premium fluid milk. - ✅ Types: **High-pressure valve**, **Ultrasonic**, **Microfluidizer**, **Membrane**, **Colloid mill**. - ✅ High-pressure valve homogenizer is **most widely used** in dairy industry. - ✅ Homogenization improves: **whiteness, mouthfeel, flavor release, digestibility, stability**. - ✅ Critical in: **fluid milk, ice cream, yogurt, infant formula, cream liqueurs**. - ✅ Microfluidizers produce **sub-micron** globules for infant formula. - ✅ In unhomogenized milk, creaming is visible within **~20 hours**. - ✅ In homogenized milk, visible creaming would take **> 40 days** (theoretical). - ✅ Casein micelles **adsorb onto the new fat surface** immediately after disruption, stabilizing the emulsion. - ✅ Homogenized milk is **more susceptible to light-induced oxidation** (riboflavin activation) — must be protected from light. - ✅ **Feathering** in coffee is reduced by **two-stage homogenization**. - ✅ Always remember: **$\tau = \mu \cdot (du/dy)$** for shear stress in valve gap.

topics.title: Centrifugation and Membrane Separation: Centrifugation – Clarification – Clarifiers and Separators – Separation Efficiency – Factors Affecting Fat Percentage in Cream – Fat Loss in Skim Milk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT615 - DAIRY TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Centrifugation and Membrane Separation: Centrifugation – Clarification – Clarifiers and Separators – Separation Efficiency – Factors Affecting Fat Percentage in Cream – Fat Loss in Skim Milk # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Centrifugation — Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **CENTRIFUGATION (KTU 2024 Definition):** > Centrifugation is a **mechanical separation process** that exploits **density gradients** between immiscible or finely dispersed phases (e.g., fat globules vs. skim milk serum) by subjecting the fluid to a **radial acceleration field** generated inside a **rotating bowl** of a high-speed machine. The denser phase (skim milk/serum) is forced outward toward the bowl periphery, while the lighter phase (cream/fat) migrates toward the **axis of rotation**. In dairy technology, the standard operational regime is governed by an angular velocity $(\omega)$ that produces **centrifugal accelerations** typically between **3,000 × g and 10,000 × g** (g-force), as opposed to the Earth's gravitational acceleration $(g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2)$. ## 1.2 Clarification — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **CLARIFICATION (KTU Syllabus Highlight):** > Clarification is a **centrifugal purification operation** designed to remove **insoluble extraneous matter (IES)** — such as **somatic cells, dust, dirt particles, hair, bacteria clumps, and straw fragments** — from whole milk without significantly altering its **fat composition** (i.e., it is *not* primarily a fat-separation step). The two principal industrial devices are: - **Clarifier** — designed to remove **solid impurities (IES)** from milk. - **Separator** — designed to split milk into **cream (fat-rich phase)** and **skim milk (fat-poor phase)**. ## 1.3 Clarifier vs. Separator — The Core Distinction | Parameter | Clarifier | Separator | |---|---|---| | **Primary Goal** | Removal of solid impurities | Separation of fat from skim milk | | **Fat Percentage Change** | **Negligible** (~no change) | Deliberately increases cream fat % | | **Outlets** | Single clarified milk outlet | Two outlets: **Cream + Skim Milk** | | **Disc Stack Geometry** | Discs have **neutral (non-holed) inserts** | Discs have **alignment holes** to form separate streams | | **Throughput Direction** | Single-pass, axial | Cream exits via top, skim exits peripherally | | **Operating G-Force** | Lower (5,000–6,000 × g) | Higher (6,000–10,000 × g) | ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **REAL-WORLD ANALOGY (Salad Spinner Concept):** > Imagine you have a salad spinner filled with water and lettuce leaves. When you spin the handle vigorously, water droplets are flung outward to the bowl's walls and collected, while the drier lettuce collects in the centre. **Centrifugation works exactly the same way** — milk is spun rapidly inside a hermetically sealed bowl; heavier water-and-protein-phase (skim milk) is flung to the periphery and drained, while lighter butterfat globules migrate toward the central axis and are siphoned off as cream. **Why does fat rise instead of sink?** - Milk fat has density ≈ **0.93 g/cm³** at 20 °C - Skim milk serum has density ≈ **1.035 g/cm³** at 20 °C - Therefore, fat is **lighter** and cream naturally "rises" — just faster under centrifugation. ## 1.5 Key Engineering Constants Used in This Module | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Magnitude | |---|---|---| | $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | **9.81 m/s²** | | $\rho_{fat}$ | Density of milk fat | **0.91–0.93 g/cm³** | | $\rho_{serum}$ | Density of skim milk | **1.030–1.036 g/cm³** | | $\Delta\rho$ | Density difference | **≈ 0.105 g/cm³** | | $d$ | Fat globule diameter | **0.2–15 µm** (avg 3–4 µm) | | $\eta$ | Viscosity of milk at 50 °C | **≈ 1.0 cP (mPa·s)** | | $N$ | Bowl speed (separator) | **5,000–7,500 rpm** | | Bowl radius $r$ | Typical radius | **0.15–0.30 m** | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Principle — The Centrifugal Force Field When a particle of mass $m$ rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ in a circular path of radius $r$, it experiences a **centrifugal force** directed outward, given by Newton's second law applied in a rotating frame: $$F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$$ Since $m = V \cdot \rho$, where $V$ is the volume of the particle and $\rho$ its density, the **centrifugal force per unit volume** becomes: $$\frac{F_c}{V} = \rho \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$$ This quantity has units of $\text{N/m}^3$, equivalent to $\text{kg/(m·s}^2\text{)}$. For analytical convenience, we express this in terms of **g-force** by dividing by gravitational force density $\rho g$: $$G = \frac{\omega^2 \cdot r}{g}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is this dimensionless number G so important?** > Because **sedimentation velocity under centrifugation scales linearly with G**, a separator running at **6,500 × g** settles fat globules **6,500× faster** than a quiescent gravity settling tank. This is the *engineering justification* for industrial centrifugal separation. ## 2.2 Stokes' Law Adapted to Centrifugal Field For a spherical fat globule of diameter $d$ moving through a viscous medium (skim milk serum) under centrifugal acceleration, the **terminal radial migration velocity** is: $$v_r = \frac{(\rho_{serum} - \rho_{fat}) \cdot d^2 \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r}{18 \cdot \eta}$$ **Logical interpretation of every term:** - $(\rho_{serum} - \rho_{fat})$ — driving density contrast → larger contrast ⇒ faster separation. - $d^2$ — quadratic dependence on globule size ⇒ **a 2× larger globule migrates 4× faster**. - $\omega^2 \cdot r$ — centrifugal field intensity ⇒ higher bowl speed ⇒ dramatically faster migration. - $\eta$ — viscosity of continuous phase ⇒ **hotter milk (lower $\eta$) separates better**. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **EXAM MANDATE:** Memorise the dimensionless units and the algebraic structure of every equation below. KTU valuation allocates full marks (typically 5 of 7) to students who write correct **units and boundary conditions**. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Unit / Typical Range | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Centrifugal force | $F_c = m \omega^2 r$ | Newtons (N) | | 2 | G-force (separation factor) | $Z = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g}$ | **Dimensionless**; 5,000–10,000 | | 3 | Stokes migration velocity | $v_r = \dfrac{\Delta\rho \cdot d^2 \omega^2 r}{18 \eta}$ | m/s | | 4 | Cream fat % from mass balance | $F_c = \dfrac{F_m - F_s \cdot (1 - R)}{(1 - R)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{100}$ | % fat (w/w) | | 5 | Skim milk fat % (inverse) | $F_s = \dfrac{F_m - F_c (1 - R)}{(R)}$ | % fat (w/w) | | 6 | Skimming efficiency | $\eta_{skim} = \dfrac{F_m - F_s}{F_m} \times 100$ | **0–100 %** | | 7 | Volumetric skimming efficiency | $E_v = \dfrac{Q_s \cdot \rho_s}{Q_m \cdot \rho_m}$ | **0–1** (often ×100%) | | 8 | Fat loss in skim milk (kg/h) | $\dot{m}_{loss} = Q_s \cdot \rho_s \cdot F_s$ | kg/h | | 9 | Cream flow rate ratio | $R = \dfrac{Q_c}{Q_m}$ (cream fraction) | **0.08–0.12** typical | | 10 | Centrifuge throughput | $Q = V_b \cdot \omega^2 r \cdot \Delta\rho$ | m³/s (theoretical) | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Dairy Industry:** Almost every cream, butter, and standardized milk line uses centrifugal separators (e.g., Alfa Laval, GEA Westfalia, Tetra Pak). - **Edible Oils:** Centrifuges clarify crude vegetable oils from pressed seeds. - **Pharmaceuticals:** Separation of plasma from whole blood. - **Wastewater Treatment:** Sludge dewatering and biosolids concentration. - **Fuel Sector:** Algae biomass harvesting for biofuel production. The **same physics** — a rotating drum + density difference + viscosity — governs all these applications. ## 2.5 Why Clarification vs. Separation Demands Different Designs A **clarifier** does not need to handle two outflows and so it can pack more discs (smaller inter-disc spacing → higher solids capture). A **separator** must allocate two distinct liquid channels separated by **disc holes (alignment notches)** and a **discharge paring disc set** (cream paring disc + skim paring disc). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU BOARD EXAM TRAP:** > Some students write that a clarifier separates cream and skim milk. **This is wrong.** A clarifier's fat composition remains essentially unchanged — only the **insoluble extraneous solids (IES)** are expelled. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Centrifugal Force from Newton's Second Law **Starting point:** A particle executes uniform circular motion at angular velocity $\omega$ on a path of radius $r$. The required centripetal acceleration is $a_c = \omega^2 r$ directed inward. The **centrifugal (inertial reaction) force** experienced by the particle in the rotating reference frame is equal in magnitude and opposite in direction. $$F_c = m \cdot a_c$$ Substituting $a_c = \omega^2 r$: $$\boxed{F_c = m \, \omega^2 r}$$ Substituting $m = \rho V$ (density × volume): $$F_c = \rho V \, \omega^2 r$$ Dividing both sides by $V$ to express force per unit volume (pressure-equivalent): $$\frac{F_c}{V} = \rho \, \omega^2 r$$ This confirms that **centrifugal force density is proportional to $\omega^2$**, hence doubling bowl RPM quadruples the field intensity. ## 3.2 Derivation of Stokes' Migration Velocity Under Centrifugal Field We adapt Stokes' law from the standard gravitational case. In a gravitational field, terminal settling velocity is: $$v_g = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_f) \, g \, d^2}{18 \eta}$$ The only modification required is to replace $g$ with the centrifugal radial acceleration $\omega^2 r$: $$v_r = \frac{(\rho_{serum} - \rho_{fat}) \, \omega^2 r \, d^2}{18 \eta}$$ Letting $\Delta\rho = \rho_{serum} - \rho_{fat}$: $$\boxed{v_r = \frac{\Delta\rho \, \omega^2 r \, d^2}{18 \eta}}$$ **Physical interpretation step-by-step:** 1. The **buoyant** force on the globule is reduced by $V \rho_{serum} \omega^2 r$ (outward, opposing migration since fat is lighter). 2. The **weight-like** centrifugal force on fat is $V \rho_{fat} \omega^2 r$ (outward). 3. The **net driving force** is therefore $V \Delta\rho \, \omega^2 r$ (inward). 4. **Drag** on the globule (Stokes regime) is $3\pi \eta d \, v_r$. 5. At terminal velocity, driving force = drag force, leading to the boxed expression above. ## 3.3 Derivation of Fat Percentage in Cream (Mass Balance) **Given:** - Whole milk inlet fat % = $F_m$ - Skim milk fat % = $F_s$ - Cream fat % = $F_c$ - Cream fraction by mass = $R = \dfrac{m_c}{m_m}$ **Mass balance on fat entering = fat leaving:** $$m_m \cdot F_m = m_c \cdot F_c + m_s \cdot F_s$$ Mass balance on total mass: $$m_m = m_c + m_s$$ Substituting $m_s = m_m - m_c$ into the fat balance: $$m_m \cdot F_m = m_c \cdot F_c + (m_m - m_c) \cdot F_s$$ Dividing through by $m_m$: $$F_m = \frac{m_c}{m_m} \cdot F_c + \left(1 - \frac{m_c}{m_m}\right) \cdot F_s$$ Let $R = m_c / m_m$: $$F_m = R \cdot F_c + (1 - R) \cdot F_s$$ Solving for $F_c$: $$R \cdot F_c = F_m - F_s(1 - R)$$ $$\boxed{F_c = \frac{F_m - F_s(1 - R)}{R}}$$ Similarly, solving for $F_s$: $$\boxed{F_s = \frac{F_m - R \cdot F_c}{1 - R}}$$ ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Problem:** A separator receives whole milk at 4.0 % fat and produces cream at 40 % fat with a cream fraction of 12 % (by mass). Determine (a) skim milk fat %, and (b) fat loss rate if the plant processes 10,000 kg/h of milk. ### Part (a) — Skim milk fat % Using $F_s = \dfrac{F_m - R \cdot F_c}{1 - R}$: $$F_s = \frac{4.0 - 0.12 \times 40}{1 - 0.12}$$ $$F_s = \frac{4.0 - 4.8}{0.88} = \frac{-0.8}{0.88}$$ $$F_s = -0.909 \ \%$$ **Result is impossible** → this tells us the cream fraction of 12 % is **too low** to reach 40 % cream fat. We must iteratively find a feasible $R$. Iterating: try $R = 0.10$: $$F_s = \frac{4.0 - 0.10 \times 40}{0.90} = \frac{0}{0.90} = 0.00 \ \%$$ Try $R = 0.095$: $$F_s = \frac{4.0 - 0.095 \times 40}{0.905} = \frac{4.0 - 3.8}{0.905} = \frac{0.2}{0.905} = 0.221 \ \%$$ **Conclusion:** At cream fraction ≈ 9.5 %, cream at 40 % fat and skim at 0.22 % fat is achievable from 4 % milk. ### Part (b) — Fat loss rate $$\dot{m}_{loss} = Q_s \cdot F_s$$ Skim milk flow = $10{,}000 \times (1 - 0.095) = 9{,}050 \text{ kg/h}$ $$\dot{m}_{loss} = 9{,}050 \times 0.00221 = 20.0 \text{ kg/h}$$ **Fat loss = 20 kg/h** — this is the engineering quantity to **minimise** for profitability. ## 3.5 Derivation of Separation Efficiency (Skimming Efficiency) Separation efficiency quantifies how effectively the centrifuge removes fat from whole milk: $$\eta_{skim} = \frac{\text{Fat entering} - \text{Fat leaving in skim}}{\text{Fat entering}} \times 100 \ \%$$ $$\eta_{skim} = \frac{F_m - F_s}{F_m} \times 100 \ \%$$ For our worked example: $$\eta_{skim} = \frac{4.0 - 0.221}{4.0} \times 100 = 94.475 \ \%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Industrial Benchmark:** Modern hermetic separators achieve skimming efficiencies of **99.5 % to 99.8 %** — meaning skim milk fat content is typically **0.04–0.07 %**. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Centrifugal Separator ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Milk Inlet
4 percent Fat 50 deg C] --> B[Distributor
Centre Feed Tube] B --> C[Disc Stack Bowl
6000 to 9000 times g] C --> D{Density Based
Splitting Zone} D --> E[Cream Paring Disc
Top Outlet] D --> F[Skim Milk Paring Disc
Bottom Outlet] E --> G[Cream Receiver
40 percent Fat] F --> H[Skim Milk Receiver
0.05 percent Fat] C --> I[Solid Discharge
Sludge Nozzle] I --> J[Sludge Collection Pit] C --> K[Drive System
Gear Motor 7.5 kW] K --> C ``` ## 4.2 Clarifier vs. Separator — Functional Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ClarifierPath[Clarifier Path] A1[Milk In] --> B1[Disc Stack
No Disc Holes] B1 --> C1[Single Outlet
Clarified Milk] B1 --> D1[Sludge Eject
IES Particles] end subgraph SeparatorPath[Separator Path] A2[Milk In] --> B2[Disc Stack
With Disc Holes] B2 --> C2[Cream Outlet
Lighter Phase] B2 --> D2[Skim Milk Outlet
Heavier Phase] end ``` ## 4.3 Process Flow Inside the Centrifuge Bowl (Radial View) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BowlSection[Centrifuge Bowl Radial Cross Section] direction LR AXIS[CENTRE AXIS
Cream Accumulates] -->|inward migration| FAT[FAT GLOBULES
rho 0.93 g per cm cubed] FAT --> CREAM[CREAM LAYER] SERUM[SKIM MILK SERUM
rho 1.035 g per cm cubed] -->|outward migration| WALL WALL[BOWL WALL
Heaviest Phase] end G[Centrifugal Field
omega squared r] -->|drives outward| SERUM G -->|drives inward| FAT ``` ## 4.4 Working Component Architecture of Industrial Separator ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph BowlAssembly[Disc Bowl Assembly] DS[Disc Stack 60 to 150 Discs] --> DD[Distributor Cone] DD --> SB[Sliding Bowl Bottom] SB --> SJ[Sludge Discharge Ports] end subgraph DriveAssembly[Drive Assembly] GM[Gear Motor 5 to 15 kW] --> VB[Vertical Shaft] VB --> BR[Brake System] BR --> SB end subgraph FrameAssembly[Frame Assembly] FR[Chassis Frame] --> COL[Collecting Pans] COL --> COUT[Cream Outlet Pipe] COL --> SOUT[Skim Milk Outlet Pipe] end ``` ## 4.5 Material Flow Diagram of a Dairy Separation Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR RM[Raw Milk Receiving
Bulk Tank] --> PF[Plate Preheater
to 50 to 55 deg C] PF --> SEP[Centrifugal Separator
Alfa Laval Type] SEP --> CR[Cream Pasteuriser] SEP --> SM[Skim Milk Pasteuriser] CR --> CST[Cream Storage Tank] SM --> SST[Skim Milk Storage Tank] CST --> P1[Packaging Line Cream] SST --> P2[Packaging Line Toned Milk] ``` ## 4.6 Annotated List of Separator Parts | Component | Engineering Function | |---|---| | **Bowl Shell** | Houses rotating assembly, withstands ~10,000 × g | | **Disc Stack** | Reduces settling distance, increases throughput | | **Disc Holes (Alignment Notches)** | Channels cream upward and skim downward | | **Cream Paring Disc** | Extracts cream under suction | | **Skim Milk Paring Disc** | Extracts skim milk under suction | | **Distributor** | Delivers whole milk to bowl interior | | **Solenoid Valve** | Controls periodic sludge ejection | | **Top Bearing** | Supports vertical shaft top end | | **Bottom Bearing** | Supports vertical shaft, absorbs vertical thrust | | **Drive Motor** | 5–25 kW depending on capacity | # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Part A questions test **Remember / Understand (Bloom Level 1–2)**. Two-mark answer + one-mark diagram or definition = 3 marks. ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between a clarifier and a centrifugal separator in a dairy plant.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Clarifier | Separator | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Removes **insoluble extraneous solids (IES)** from milk | Splits milk into **cream** and **skim milk** | | **Number of Outlets** | Single clarified milk outlet | Two outlets: cream + skim milk | | **Effect on Fat %** | **No significant change** in fat composition | Cream fat % rises sharply (~40–45%) | | **Disc Geometry** | Discs have **no alignment holes** | Discs have **alignment holes** for phase separation | | **Operating Regime** | Often **self-cleaning** with periodic sludge ejection | Continuous hermetic operation | **(1 mark for purpose, 1 mark for table/differences, 1 mark for disc geometry or fat effect statement.)** ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define "Separation Efficiency" in a centrifugal separator. State its typical industrial range.** **Model Answer:** Separation efficiency (also called **skimming efficiency**) is the fraction of total fat entering the separator that is **successfully transferred into the cream phase**, leaving the skim milk with the lowest possible fat residue. $$\eta_{skim} = \frac{F_m - F_s}{F_m} \times 100 \ \%$$ **Industrial range:** Modern self-cleaning separators achieve **99.5 – 99.8 %**, corresponding to skim milk fat content of **0.04–0.07 %**. **(1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark numerical range.)** ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Module Pattern (14 marks):** Each long answer carries **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Students must answer **either OR**. Both parts below are full independent questions. --- ### 🔷 Question A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **principle of centrifugal separation** in a dairy plant. With the help of a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of a **hermetic centrifugal separator**. **(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **factors affecting the fat percentage of cream** obtained from a centrifugal separator. #### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks **Principle (3 marks):** Centrifugal separation is based on the principle that when milk is subjected to high angular velocity inside a sealed rotating bowl, the **density gradient** between fat globules ($\rho \approx 0.93$ g/cm³) and skim milk serum ($\rho \approx 1.035$ g/cm³) causes the heavier serum to migrate outward while lighter fat concentrates near the axis. The migration velocity follows Stokes' law adapted for centrifugal field: $$v_r = \frac{\Delta\rho \, \omega^2 r \, d^2}{18 \eta}$$ where $\Delta\rho = \rho_{serum} - \rho_{fat}$. **Construction & Working (4 marks):** | Component | Function | |---|---| | Bowl shell | Houses disc stack; withstands high g-forces | | Disc stack (60–150 conical discs) | Reduces inter-disc gap, accelerating separation | | Distributor cone | Injects milk tangentially into bowl interior | | Top/bottom paring discs | Pump out cream and skim milk under suction | | Sliding bowl bottom | Ejects accumulated sludge periodically via water nozzle | | Drive motor + vertical shaft | Rotates bowl at 5,000–7,500 rpm | **Working Sequence:** 1. Pre-heated milk (50–55 °C) enters through **centre feed tube**. 2. Milk is distributed into **disc stack** through distributor cone. 3. Heavy skim milk migrates **outward** through disc holes to bowl periphery. 4. Cream migrates **inward** toward bowl axis. 5. Cream exits via top paring disc; skim exits via bottom paring disc. 6. Sludge is intermittently discharged through water-operated nozzles. **(Block diagram showing flow counts as 1 mark.)** #### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks **Factors affecting cream fat percentage:** 1. **Cream flow rate / cream fraction (R)** — Higher cream fraction → **lower fat %** in cream (more cream, same total fat). 2. **Whole milk fat content** — Richer milk → higher cream fat %. 3. **Bowl speed (rpm)** — Higher ω → better separation → higher cream fat %. 4. **Milk temperature** — Higher T → lower viscosity → better separation → higher cream fat %. 5. **Disc spacing & number** — Tighter spacing → better separation. 6. **Back pressure / cream screw setting** — Adjustable back-pressure disc governs cream fat %. 7. **Feed rate** — Excessive feed rate reduces residence time → lower cream fat %. **(Allocate ~1 mark per factor, with 1 mark for the closing synthesis statement.)** --- ### 🔷 Question B — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a) [7 marks]** With neat sketches, differentiate between a **clarifier** and a **separator**. Describe the **clarification of milk** in detail. **(b) [7 marks]** A dairy plant processes 20,000 kg of whole milk per hour with 4.2 % fat. The separator produces cream at 45 % fat and skim milk at 0.05 % fat. Calculate: - (i) Cream fraction and skim milk fraction - (ii) Hourly fat loss in skim milk - (iii) Skimming efficiency #### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks **Clarifier vs. Separator (3 marks):** A **clarifier** removes IES (somatic cells, dirt, dust) using a disc stack with **no alignment holes** and a single clarified milk outlet. It does not separate fat and skim phases. A **separator** uses discs with **alignment holes** to produce two phase-separated outlets: cream (top) and skim milk (bottom). **Clarification Process (4 marks):** 1. Milk heated to **32–38 °C** to reduce viscosity (lower than separation temperature). 2. Milk enters clarifier through **centre feed**. 3. **Solid impurities** (higher density than milk) migrate outward under centrifugal force. 4. Impurities accumulate in **sludge space** at bowl periphery. 5. Clarified milk exits from **top paring disc** at essentially the same fat composition as the inlet. 6. Sludge is discharged **periodically** (every 15–60 minutes) through self-cleaning nozzles or manual desludging. **Applications:** Removes up to **95 % of somatic cells**, significantly improves **microbial quality**, and extends shelf-life of pasteurised milk. #### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $F_m = 4.2 \ \%$, $F_c = 45 \ \%$, $F_s = 0.05 \ \%$, $m_m = 20{,}000$ kg/h **Step 1 — Find cream fraction $R$ from mass balance:** $$F_m = R \cdot F_c + (1 - R) \cdot F_s$$ $$4.2 = R \cdot 45 + (1 - R) \cdot 0.05$$ $$4.2 = 45R + 0.05 - 0.05R$$ $$4.2 - 0.05 = 44.95 \, R$$ $$4.15 = 44.95 \, R$$ $$R = \frac{4.15}{44.95} = 0.0923$$ **Cream fraction $R = 9.23 \ \%$** (1 mark) **Skim milk fraction** $= 1 - 0.0923 = 0.9077$ or **90.77 %** (1 mark) **Step 2 — Skim milk mass flow:** $$m_s = 0.9077 \times 20{,}000 = 18{,}154 \text{ kg/h}$$ **Step 3 — Fat loss rate:** $$\dot{m}_{loss} = m_s \times F_s = 18{,}154 \times \frac{0.05}{100}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{m}_{loss} = 9.08 \text{ kg/h}}$$ (2 marks) **Step 4 — Skimming efficiency:** $$\eta_{skim} = \frac{F_m - F_s}{F_m} \times 100 = \frac{4.2 - 0.05}{4.2} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{skim} = 98.81 \ \%}$$ (2 marks) **Verdict:** This is acceptable but below modern hermetic standards (99.5 %+), suggesting **maintenance needed** (slightly worn discs or cold feed milk). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S PITFALL ALERT — Where students LOSE marks:** > > 1. **Confusing clarifier and separator:** Many students write that a clarifier also separates cream. **It does not.** Always state that a clarifier *only* removes IES without altering fat %. (**Lose 2 marks** if conflated.) > > 2. **Units in $v_r$ formula:** Writing $v_r$ in cm/s without specifying the unit conversion of $r$, $d$, and $\eta$ leads to a **dimensional error** and **-1 mark penalty**. > > 3. **Cream fraction vs. cream fat %:** Do NOT confuse $R = m_c/m_m$ with the fat percentage $F_c$. These are entirely different quantities. The cream fat percentage is a *composition*, while the cream fraction is a *flow ratio*. > > 4. **Skimming efficiency formula:** Writing "efficiency = $F_s/F_m$" instead of $(F_m - F_s)/F_m$ reverses the answer. Check the **direction of subtraction** carefully. > > 5. **Forgetting the viscosity term:** In essay questions on factors affecting cream fat %, many students omit **temperature / viscosity** as a factor. Always mention that **higher temperature lowers viscosity** and improves separation. > > 6. **Skipping intermediate mass-balance steps:** In numerical problems, jumping from "Given" to "Final Answer" without showing algebraic substitution **loses up to 3 marks** for working. Always write the mass balance equation first. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **🚀 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST (Module 3 — Centrifugation Subtopics)** - **Centrifugation** = density-based separation in a high-g rotating bowl. - **Clarifier** removes **IES** (somatic cells, dirt); **does NOT** change fat %. - **Separator** splits milk into **cream + skim milk**; uses discs with **alignment holes**. - **Centrifugal force:** $F_c = m\omega^2 r$ — proportional to **$\omega^2$**, so doubling RPM quadruples force. - **Stokes' migration velocity:** $v_r = \dfrac{\Delta\rho \, \omega^2 r \, d^2}{18 \eta}$ — quadratic in fat globule diameter $d$. - **G-force / Separation factor** $Z = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g}$ is **dimensionless**; industrial range = **5,000–10,000**. - **Cream fat % mass balance:** $F_m = R \cdot F_c + (1 - R) \cdot F_s$. - **Skimming efficiency:** $\eta_{skim} = \dfrac{F_m - F_s}{F_m} \times 100 \ \%$ — modern hermetic ≥ **99.5 %**. - **Factors affecting cream fat %:** (i) cream fraction R, (ii) milk fat, (iii) bowl speed, (iv) temperature (viscosity), (v) disc geometry, (vi) back-pressure setting, (vii) feed rate. - **Fat loss in skim milk:** $\dot{m}_{loss} = Q_s \cdot F_s$ (kg/h) — to be **minimised**. - **Operating temperature:** **50–55 °C** (separation); **32–38 °C** (clarification) — higher T → lower viscosity → better separation. - **Fat globule density:** **0.91–0.93 g/cm³**; **Serum density:** **1.030–1.036 g/cm³**. - **Average fat globule diameter:** **3–4 µm**; range **0.2–15 µm**. - **Industrial benchmark for skim milk fat:** **0.04–0.07 %** (vs. legal max 0.5 % for "skimmed milk" under FSSAI). - **Disc stack** = 60–150 conical discs; reduces inter-disc distance, accelerating separation. - **Hermetic vs. Open type separators:** Hermetic seals milk inlet/outlet against air ingress → less oxidation and higher efficiency. - **Self-cleaning separators** discharge sludge every 15–60 minutes via water-operated nozzle ports.

topics.title: Construction of Separator Components – Bactofuge Treatment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT615 - DAIRY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Construction of Separator Components – Bactofuge Treatment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Centrifugal Separator (Cream Separator)

A **centrifugal separator** in dairy technology is a high-speed rotational device that utilizes the principle of centrifugal force to separate two immiscible liquids of different densities (typically milk fat globules from skim milk) and to remove solid impurities (somatic cells, dust, particles) simultaneously. In a modern dairy plant, the separator is the heart of the fluid milk processing line.

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> *A centrifugal separator is a mechanically driven, sanitary-design bowl assembly in which milk is subjected to an enhanced gravitational field (typically 5000–10,000 × g) created by rotation at 4000–7500 RPM, causing phase separation based on density differentials between fat (≈ 0.91 g/cm³), skim milk (≈ 1.035 g/cm³), and sludge particles (≈ 1.05–1.40 g/cm³).*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – The "Dairy Spin Dryer"

Imagine you have a glass of milk left on a table. Within hours, cream slowly rises to the top because fat is lighter than water — this is **gravity clarification** (Stokes' law under normal gravity). Now imagine spinning that same glass at 7000 RPM. The cream is "thrown outward" or "pushed inward" thousands of times faster than gravity alone could ever achieve. This is exactly what a separator does: it is a **salad spinner for milk on industrial steroids**, replacing the gentle 1 × g gravitational pull with thousands of g-forces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The separator performs *three* simultaneous functions: (1) **Cream separation** (fat vs. skim), (2) **Clarification** (removal of solid impurities/sludge), and (3) **Standardization** (adjusting fat content by controlled remix of cream and skim streams).

---

## 1.3 The Bactofuge – Definition and Purpose

A **bactofuge** is a specialized high-speed centrifugal separator engineered specifically to remove **bacterial cells, spores (especially *Bacillus cereus* and *Clostridium* spores), and somatic cells** from milk. It is *not* primarily a cream separator — it is a **biosecurity device** that complements pasteurization.

**Formal Definition (KTU Standard):**
> *A bactofuge is a hermetically sealed, steam-sterilizable centrifugal separator operating at ultra-high rotational speeds (9000–14,000 × g) and elevated temperatures (55–72 °C), designed to reduce the bacterial and spore load of milk by 90–99 % through density-based centrifugal ejection, producing a low-bacteria "main stream" and a concentrated bacterial "bactofugate" stream representing 2–3 % of feed volume.*

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy – "Bacterial Whirlpool"

Think of a swimming pool full of tiny dust particles. If you drained the pool through a fast-spinning funnel, the dust would be flung to the outer edge and trapped, while clean water exited through the center. A bactofuge does the same for milk, but the "dust" is invisible *Bacillus* spores. The key difference: spores are only **slightly denser** than milk (≈ 1.05–1.20 g/cm³ vs. 1.035 g/cm³), so the bactofuge must spin much **faster** and use **special wide-angle disc stacks** to catch these sub-micron particles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Bactofuges Matter (Cheese Industry Context):** Spore-formers survive pasteurization and cause **late-blowing defect** in hard and semi-hard cheeses. Bactofugation removes 90–99 % of spores *before* cheesemaking, dramatically extending cheese shelf life and quality — a critical concept in KTU 2024 Dairy Technology Module 3.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Bowl rotational speed (separator) | 4000 – 7500 | **RPM** |
| Bowl rotational speed (bactofuge) | 9000 – 14,000 | **RPM** |
| Effective g-force (separator) | 5000 – 10,000 | **× g** |
| Effective g-force (bactofuge) | 9000 – 14,000 | **× g** |
| Milk density | 1.030 – 1.035 | **g/cm³** |
| Milk fat density (liquid) | 0.910 – 0.915 | **g/cm³** |
| Bacterial spore density | 1.05 – 1.20 | **g/cm³** |
| Somatic cell density | 1.08 – 1.10 | **g/cm³** |
| Operating temperature (separator) | 40 – 55 | **°C** |
| Operating temperature (bactofuge) | 55 – 72 | **°C** |
| Steam sterilization temperature | 110 – 125 | **°C** |
| Bactofugate volume fraction | 2 – 3 | **% of feed** |
| Spore/bacteria removal efficiency | 90 – 99 | **%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Density differential (Δρ) is the master variable.** Every separator design decision — bowl speed, disc angle, disc spacing — is engineered to *maximize the response of particles to the imposed centrifugal acceleration* given their Δρ relative to skim milk.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing Physics – Why Centrifugation Works

### 2.1.1 Centrifugal Acceleration
A particle in a rotating bowl of radius *r* at angular velocity *ω* experiences an outward acceleration:

$$a_c = \omega^2 \, r$$

The corresponding **g-force** (relative to Earth's gravity) is:

$$Z = \frac{\omega^2 r}{g} = \frac{(2\pi N/60)^2 \, r}{g}$$

where:
- $N$ = rotational speed in **RPM**
- $r$ = bowl radius in **m**
- $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
- $Z$ = dimensionless g-multiplier

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A separator bowl of 0.30 m radius spinning at 6500 RPM produces $\omega = 680.7$ rad/s, yielding $a_c = 1.39 \times 10^5 \; \text{m/s}^2 \approx 14{,}200 \times g$. This is why a normal separator at 6500 RPM can outperform hours of gravity separation.

### 2.1.2 Sedimentation Velocity (Stokes' Law Adapted)
The terminal velocity of a particle of radius $R_p$ and density $\rho_p$ settling through a fluid of density $\rho_f$ and viscosity $\mu$ under a centrifugal field is:

$$v_s = \frac{2 R_p^2 \, (\rho_p - \rho_f) \, \omega^2 r}{9 \mu}$$

For a fat globule in milk:
- $R_p = 1\text{–}5 \; \mu m$
- $(\rho_p - \rho_f) \approx -0.12 \; \text{g/cm}^3$ (negative, so it migrates **inward**)
- $\mu$ of milk at 50 °C ≈ 0.9 cP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The negative $\Delta \rho$ for fat means fat globules migrate **inward** (toward the bowl axis), while heavier particles (somatic cells, spores, dust) migrate **outward**. This is the foundation of simultaneous separation and clarification.

---

## 2.2 The Disc Stack Theory

Modern separators do not rely on the bowl wall for separation. Instead, they use a **stack of conical discs** (typically 30–150 discs) that split the milk into thin films, drastically reducing the distance a particle must travel to be "captured."

### 2.2.1 Equivalent Settling Distance
With disc spacing $h$ at half-angle $\alpha$ to the vertical, the equivalent settling distance is reduced from the bowl radius $r$ to approximately:

$$s_{eq} = \frac{h}{\cos \alpha}$$

For typical values $h = 0.5$ mm and $\alpha = 45°$:
$$s_{eq} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{\cos 45°} \approx 0.707 \; \text{mm}$$

This is **400× shorter** than the bowl radius, making separation feasible for sub-micron particles.

### 2.2.2 Number of Discs
The number of discs $n$ is determined by:

$$n = \frac{r_2 - r_1}{h} \cdot \frac{1}{\tan \alpha} + 1$$

where $r_1$ = inner disc radius, $r_2$ = outer disc radius, $h$ = vertical disc spacing.

---

## 2.3 Bactofuge-Specific Theory

A bactofuge must remove particles as small as 0.5–2 µm (bacterial spores) with Δρ as low as **0.015–0.165 g/cm³**. Three design strategies are employed:

1. **Higher g-force** (9000–14,000 × g): the squared dependence on ω means a 2× speed increase yields 4× sedimentation velocity.
2. **Wider disc spacing** (1.0–2.0 mm vs. 0.4–0.8 mm in cream separators): to handle higher solids concentration in the bactofugate.
3. **Larger disc angle** (closer to vertical): improves discharge of sticky spore-laden material.
4. **Steam sterilization (SIP)**: prevents the bactofuge itself from becoming a recontamination source.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_c = \omega^2 r$ | Centrifugal acceleration | m/s² |
| 2 | $Z = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g}$ | g-force multiplier | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity from RPM | rad/s |
| 4 | $v_s = \dfrac{2 R_p^2 \Delta\rho \, \omega^2 r}{9 \mu}$ | Stokes' sedimentation velocity (centrifugal) | m/s |
| 5 | $s_{eq} = \dfrac{h}{\cos \alpha}$ | Equivalent settling distance in disc | m |
| 6 | $n = \dfrac{(r_2 - r_1)}{h \, \tan \alpha} + 1$ | Number of discs | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta_{sep} = 1 - e^{-(v_s \, t \, A_{disc} / V_{film})}$ | Separation efficiency (exponential model) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\dot{V}_{bactofugate} = 0.02 \text{ to } 0.03 \times \dot{V}_{feed}$ | Bactofugate flow as fraction of feed | m³/h |
| 9 | $\eta_{bactofuge} = 1 - \dfrac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}$ | Bactofuge spore removal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Q_{max} = \dfrac{v_s \, A_{effective}}{2}$ | Maximum throughput (KQ factor analogy) | m³/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols Used:** $\omega$ angular velocity, $N$ RPM, $r$ radius, $R_p$ particle radius, $\Delta\rho$ density difference, $\mu$ viscosity, $h$ disc spacing, $\alpha$ disc half-angle, $\eta$ efficiency, $C$ concentration, $Q$ volumetric flow rate.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Centrifugal/Bactofuge? |
|---|---|
| Fluid milk standardization | Single-step fat content control to ±0.05 % |
| Cheese milk (Swiss, Gouda) | Bactofugation prevents **late-blowing** by *Clostridium tyrobutyricum* |
| ESL (Extended Shelf Life) milk | Bactofugation + pasteurization → 21–28 day shelf life without UHT taste |
| Whey separation in cheese plants | Removes fines, residual fat, and curd particles |
| Infant formula base | Reduces microbial load before further processing |
| Cream for butter / ice cream | Primary fat concentration step |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Centrifugal Acceleration from First Principles

A particle of mass $m$ moving in a circular path of radius $r$ at angular velocity $\omega$ traces an arc. The centripetal acceleration is required to keep the particle on the curved path.

**Step 1:** The arc length per unit time is $v = \omega r$. This is the tangential velocity.

**Step 2:** The centripetal acceleration is defined as the rate of change of the direction of the velocity vector. For uniform circular motion, the magnitude of this acceleration is:

$$a_c = \frac{v^2}{r}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $v = \omega r$:

$$a_c = \frac{(\omega r)^2}{r} = \omega^2 r$$

**Step 4:** Expressing in terms of RPM $N$ (revolutions per minute), where $\omega$ is in rad/s:

$$\omega = 2\pi \frac{N}{60} = \frac{\pi N}{30}$$

**Step 5:** Therefore:

$$a_c = \left(\frac{\pi N}{30}\right)^2 r = \frac{\pi^2 N^2 r}{900}$$

**Step 6:** The dimensionless g-force multiplier $Z$:

$$Z = \frac{a_c}{g} = \frac{\pi^2 N^2 r}{900 \, g}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example – Calculating g-Force of a Separator Bowl

> **Problem:** A separator bowl has an effective radius $r = 0.28 \; m$ and rotates at $N = 6500 \; RPM$. Calculate the g-force experienced at the outer wall.

**Step 1:** Convert RPM to angular velocity.

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 6500}{60} = \frac{40840.7}{60} = 680.68 \; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute centrifugal acceleration.

$$a_c = \omega^2 r = (680.68)^2 \times 0.28$$

Computing $(680.68)^2$:

$$(680.68)^2 = 463{,}325 \; (\text{approximately})$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by $r$:

$$a_c = 463{,}325 \times 0.28 = 129{,}731 \; \text{m/s}^2$$

**Step 4:** Convert to g-force using $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$:

$$Z = \frac{129{,}731}{9.81} \approx 13{,}224 \times g$$

**Final Answer:** $Z \approx 13{,}224 \times g$ (i.e., the wall experiences acceleration ~13,200 times gravity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always convert RPM to rad/s first, square the value, then multiply by radius. Keep 3–4 significant figures. The final g-force in a typical separator is on the order of **10⁴ × g**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Stokes' Law in a Centrifugal Field

For a spherical particle settling under gravity, Stokes' law gives:

$$v_g = \frac{2 R_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{9 \mu}$$

**Step 1:** The only difference in a centrifugal field is that $g$ is replaced by $\omega^2 r$. Thus:

$$v_s = \frac{2 R_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) \omega^2 r}{9 \mu}$$

**Step 2:** Define the **settling factor** $K$ as the ratio of centrifugal to gravitational settling:

$$K = \frac{v_s}{v_g} = \frac{\omega^2 r}{g} = Z$$

This shows that the centrifugal settling rate is exactly $Z$ times faster than gravity settling — a powerful intuition for KTU answers.

**Step 3:** For a fat globule of $R_p = 3 \; \mu m = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $\rho_p = 915 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_f = 1035 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.9 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, and $Z = 13{,}224$:

$$v_s = \frac{2 \times (3 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (-120) \times 13{,}224 \times 9.81}{9 \times 0.9 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 4:** Numerator (magnitude):

$$2 \times 9 \times 10^{-12} \times 120 \times 13{,}224 \times 9.81 = 2 \times 9 \times 120 \times 13{,}224 \times 9.81 \times 10^{-12}$$

Compute step by step:
- $2 \times 9 = 18$
- $18 \times 120 = 2160$
- $2160 \times 13{,}224 = 28{,}563{,}840$
- $28{,}563{,}840 \times 9.81 = 280{,}210{,}970.4$
- Times $10^{-12}$: $2.802 \times 10^{-1}$ (units consistent with m/s after division)

**Step 5:** Denominator:

$$9 \times 0.9 \times 10^{-3} = 8.1 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 6:** Final velocity:

$$v_s = \frac{0.2802}{8.1 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 34.6 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Interpretation:** A 3 µm fat globule theoretically migrates at 34.6 m/s in the centrifugal field. In practice, viscosity, particle interactions, and the disc stack geometry reduce this to a much lower *effective* velocity, but the calculation validates the immense power of centrifugal separation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Disc Stack Number of Discs

**Step 1:** Discs are conical with half-angle $\alpha$ (measured from vertical axis). Vertical spacing between adjacent disc surfaces is $h$.

**Step 2:** Consider two adjacent discs. The vertical gap between them at radius $r$ is:

$$\Delta r_{\text{vertical}} = \frac{h}{\tan \alpha}$$

**Step 3:** The radial span covered by $n$ discs from inner radius $r_1$ to outer radius $r_2$:

$$n - 1 = \frac{r_2 - r_1}{\Delta r_{\text{vertical}}} = \frac{(r_2 - r_1) \tan \alpha}{h}$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $n$:

$$n = \frac{(r_2 - r_1) \tan \alpha}{h} + 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical sanity check:** $r_1 = 0.05$ m, $r_2 = 0.25$ m, $\alpha = 45°$, $h = 0.5$ mm $= 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m.
> $$n = \frac{(0.25 - 0.05) \times \tan 45°}{5 \times 10^{-4}} + 1 = \frac{0.20 \times 1}{5 \times 10^{-4}} + 1 = 400 + 1 = 401$$
> This overstates the disc count because $\tan 45° = 1$ gives the maximum; real separators use $\alpha = 30°$–$50°$ and $h = 0.4$–$0.8$ mm yielding 80–150 discs. The formula is the correct master equation for KTU problems.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Separator vs. Bactofuge

| Design Parameter | Cream Separator | Bactofuge | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rotational speed (RPM) | 4000 – 7500 | 9000 – 14,000 | Higher speed needed for small Δρ |
| Effective g-force (×g) | 5,000 – 10,000 | 9,000 – 14,000 | Compensates for low spore Δρ |
| Bowl design | Open or hermetic | **Hermetic** (mandatory) | Prevents aerosol recontamination |
| Disc half-angle α | 30° – 45° | 40° – 50° | Better sludge discharge of viscous bactofugate |
| Disc spacing (mm) | 0.4 – 0.8 | 1.0 – 2.0 | Handles higher solids in bactofugate |
| Operating temperature (°C) | 40 – 55 | 55 – 72 | Lower milk viscosity for small particles |
| Sterilization | CIP (clean-in-place) | **SIP** (steam-in-place, 110–125 °C) | Prevents recontamination of sterile milk |
| Bactofugate stream | Not applicable | 2 – 3 % of feed | Concentrated bacterial/somatic cells |
| Removal efficiency (fat) | 99.5 % + | N/A (mixed with skim stream) | — |
| Removal efficiency (bacteria) | 60 – 70 % incidental | **90 – 99 %** | Core performance metric |
| Typical throughput | 5,000 – 50,000 L/h | 5,000 – 30,000 L/h | Slightly lower for bactofuge |

---

## 3.6 Worked Problem: Bactofuge Removal Efficiency

> **Problem:** A bactofuge processes 10,000 L/h of cheese milk. The inlet total plate count (TPC) is 50,000 CFU/mL and the outlet TPC is 2,000 CFU/mL. The bactofugate stream is 3 % of feed at a TPC of 1,600,000 CFU/mL. (a) Calculate the overall spore removal efficiency. (b) Verify mass balance. (c) Calculate the equivalent g-force if bowl radius is 0.18 m and speed is 12,000 RPM.

### (a) Removal Efficiency

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}} = 1 - \frac{2000}{50000} = 1 - 0.04 = 0.96$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 96 \%}$$

### (b) Mass Balance Verification

Inlet bacterial load: $50{,}000 \; \text{CFU/mL} \times 10{,}000 \; \text{L/h} = 5 \times 10^{11} \; \text{CFU/h}$

Outlet (skim) load: $2000 \times 9700 = 1.94 \times 10^{10} \; \text{CFU/h}$

Bactofugate load: $1{,}600{,}000 \times 300 = 4.8 \times 10^{8} \times 1000 = 4.8 \times 10^{11} \; \text{CFU/h}$ *(Note: 300 L/h × 1000 mL/L = 300,000 mL/h; then 1.6 × 10⁶ × 300,000 = 4.8 × 10¹¹ CFU/h)*

Sum: $1.94 \times 10^{10} + 4.8 \times 10^{11} = 4.994 \times 10^{11} \approx 5.0 \times 10^{11}$ ✓

**Mass balance verified within rounding error.**

### (c) g-Force Calculation

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 12000}{60} = \frac{75398.2}{60} = 1256.6 \; \text{rad/s}$$

$$a_c = \omega^2 r = (1256.6)^2 \times 0.18 = 1{,}579{,}041 \times 0.18 = 284{,}227 \; \text{m/s}^2$$

$$Z = \frac{284{,}227}{9.81} \approx 28{,}976 \times g$$

$$\boxed{Z \approx 28{,}976 \times g}$$

This is typical of industrial bactofuges (Westfalia, Tetra Pak Bactofuge series).

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Cream Separator Construction

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the **construction components of a centrifugal cream separator** and their functional interactions. This is the architecture mapping the engineering flow inside the bowl and its ancillary systems.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Drive Motor 7.5 to 30 kW] --> B[Vertical Hollow Spindle]
    B --> C[Bowl Body Cast Stainless Steel 316L]
    C --> D[Disc Stack 80 to 150 Conical Discs]
    C --> E[Top Disc Caul or Ring]
    C --> F[Bottom Disc Distributor]
    G[Milk Inlet from Balance Tank] --> H[Inlet Disc Centripetal Pump]
    H --> I[Feed Holed in Distributor]
    I --> D
    D --> J[Skim Milk Channel Outward to Paring Disc]
    D --> K[Fat Cream Channel Inward to Paring Disc]
    J --> L[Skim Outlet Centripetal Pump]
    K --> M[Cream Outlet Centripetal Pump]
    C --> N[Sludge Holding Space Outer Periphery]
    O[Water or Steam CIP Inlet] --> C
    P[Frame and Bearings] --> A
    Q[Brake System] --> A
    R[Pressure Gauge and Sight Glass] --> L
    R --> M
    S[Control Panel PLC Based] --> A
    S --> Q
```

### 4.1.1 Component Function Table

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Drive Motor | Provides rotational energy; typically 7.5 – 30 kW electric motor |
| Hollow Spindle | Vertical shaft conveying milk in and products out |
| Bowl Body | Houses the disc stack; precision-machined 316L stainless steel |
| Disc Stack | Performs actual separation via thin-film settling |
| Top Disc (Caul) | Seals the disc stack and directs fat inward |
| Inlet Distributor | Distributes milk evenly to all disc spaces |
| Paring Discs | Centripetal pumps that discharge skim and cream under pressure |
| Sludge Holding Space | Outer periphery where heavy solids accumulate |
| CIP/SIP Inlet | Allows clean-in-place and steam-in-place sterilization |
| Brake System | Stops bowl for manual cleaning or maintenance |
| PLC Control | Monitors vibration, temperature, pressure, and flow |

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Bactofuge Treatment System

The following diagram represents the **complete bactofuge treatment process flow** as integrated into a cheese-milk pasteurization line.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Milk Storage Tank] --> B[Plate Heat Exchanger PHE Regeneration Section]
    B --> C[Bactofuge Preheater 62 to 72 C]
    C --> D[Bactofuge Hermetic High Speed]
    D --> E[Main Stream Low Bacteria 97 to 98 percent]
    D --> F[Bactofugate Stream 2 to 3 percent of feed]
    F --> G[Batch Sterilizer or PHE 130 C for 4 s]
    G --> H[Aseptic Recombine into Main Stream]
    E --> I[Vacuum Deaerator Optional]
    I --> J[Pasteurizer HTST 72 C 15 s]
    J --> K[Cheese Milk Storage Tank]
    K --> L[Cheese Vat]
    H --> J
```

### 4.2.1 Process Description

1. **Raw milk** enters the regeneration section of the PHE and is preheated.
2. Milk is heated to **62 – 72 °C** in the bactofuge preheater (high temperature reduces viscosity and improves separation efficiency).
3. Milk enters the **hermetic bactofuge** where the bowl operates at 9000 – 14,000 × g.
4. **Main stream** (low-bacteria milk, 97 – 98 %) exits via the centripetal pump.
5. **Bactofugate** (2 – 3 % of feed, highly concentrated in spores) is diverted.
6. Bactofugate is **sterilized** at 130 °C for 4 s in a separate small heat exchanger.
7. Sterilized bactofugate is **recombined aseptically** with the main stream to preserve nutrients.
8. Combined milk is deaerated and sent to the **HTST pasteurizer** (72 °C / 15 s) for final safety.
9. Treated milk proceeds to the **cheese vat**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Notice that the bactofuge is *not* a substitute for pasteurization — it is a **complementary pre-treatment**. The combined effect (bactofugation + HTST) yields a 3 – 4 log reduction in spores, compared to ~1 log from HTST alone. This is critical for hard and semi-hard cheese safety.

---

## 4.3 Self-Cleaning vs. Solids-Ejecting Bactofuge Architecture

Modern bactofuges have a self-cleaning bowl that periodically ejects accumulated sludge without disassembly. The functional block diagram is:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Continuous Feed] --> B[Conical Disc Stack Bowl]
    B --> C[Sludge Collection Peripheral Chamber]
    C --> D[Tangential Nozzle or Piston Discharge]
    D --> E[Sludge Outlet Periodic]
    B --> F[Main Stream Continuous]
    B --> G[Bactofugate Continuous or Intermittent]
    H[Hydraulic Timer PLC] --> D
```

| Mode | Discharge Mechanism | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Manual cleaning** | Bowl disassembly | Small dairies, infrequent use |
| **Partial discharge** (water flush) | Bowl opens hydraulically for ~0.2 s | Medium dairies |
| **Self-cleaning with nozzles** | Tangential nozzles eject solids | Large cheese plants |
| **Self-cleaning with piston** | Sliding piston opens bowl bottom | Largest plants (Tetra Pak, GEA) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define a centrifugal separator and state the three simultaneous functions it performs in a dairy plant.**

**Model Answer:**

A centrifugal separator is a high-speed rotational device that exploits the density difference between milk constituents by generating enhanced gravitational fields (5000 – 10,000 × g) inside a rotating bowl.

The three simultaneous functions are:
1. **Cream separation** – divides whole milk into cream (fat-rich) and skim milk based on fat-vs-skim density difference.
2. **Clarification** – removes solid impurities such as somatic cells, dust, dirt, and bacteria from the outer periphery as sludge.
3. **Standardization** – by controlling the back-pressure on the cream outlet, the fat content of the standardized milk can be precisely adjusted to a target value (e.g., 3.5 % or 4.5 %).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for three functions)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between a cream separator and a bactofuge based on bowl design, speed, and end use.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Cream Separator | Bactofuge |
|---|---|---|
| **Bowl speed** | 4000 – 7500 RPM | 9000 – 14,000 RPM |
| **g-force** | 5,000 – 10,000 × g | 9,000 – 14,000 × g |
| **Bowl design** | Open or hermetic | Hermetic, steam-sterilizable (SIP) |
| **Disc spacing** | 0.4 – 0.8 mm | 1.0 – 2.0 mm |
| **End use** | Cream-skim separation, standardization | Removal of bacteria and spores |
| **Operating temp.** | 40 – 55 °C | 55 – 72 °C |

A bactofuge is essentially a *high-speed, sanitary, hermetic* separator engineered for **bacterial reduction (90 – 99 %)**, while a cream separator is designed primarily for **fat fractionation** with bacterial removal as a secondary benefit.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for table, 1.5 for closing summary)*

---

## 5.2 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Each Part B question offers internal choice. Below are TWO alternative 14-mark questions. Each is divided into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

**Q: With the help of a neat labeled diagram, describe the construction of a centrifugal cream separator. Explain the role of the disc stack in enhancing separation efficiency. [14 marks]**

#### Part (a) – Construction of the Separator (7 marks)

**Components and their functions:**

1. **Bowl (Rotor):** Precision-machined stainless steel (316L) hollow body that rotates at 4000 – 7500 RPM. The bowl houses the disc stack and forms the separation chamber.

2. **Disc Stack:** 80 – 150 conical discs, typically spaced 0.4 – 0.8 mm apart, with a half-angle of 30° – 45° to the vertical axis. Each disc has spacer strips (cauls) on its top surface to maintain the precise gap.

3. **Top Disc and Bottom Disc (Distributor):** The top disc closes the stack; the bottom distributor receives the incoming milk and feeds it into the disc spaces through radial holes.

4. **Hollow Spindle (Shaft):** Vertical rotating shaft that conveys milk in and discharges cream and skim out. It runs in bearings within the frame.

5. **Drive Motor and Gearbox:** Electric motor (7.5 – 30 kW) coupled to the spindle via a gear or direct-drive coupling.

6. **Paring Discs (Centripetal Pumps):** Stationary discs located on top of the bowl that convert the rotating liquid into pressurized flow for discharge.

7. **Inlet and Outlet Pipes:** Sanitary tri-clamp pipes for feed milk, skim milk, and cream.

8. **Sludge Holding Space:** The outermost annular region of the bowl where heavy solids accumulate.

9. **Frame, Cover, and Safety Interlocks:** Provide structural support and operator safety.

10. **Brake and Control System:** Allows bowl to be stopped safely; PLC monitors speed, vibration, and temperature.

**Construction Material:** All milk-contact parts are SS 316L with surface roughness Ra ≤ 0.8 µm for CIP effectiveness.

#### Part (b) – Role of the Disc Stack (7 marks)

**Why the disc stack is essential:**

1. **Reduces settling distance:** In an empty bowl, a fat globule would have to travel the entire bowl radius (~0.25 m) to reach the cream layer. The disc stack limits the travel distance to the disc spacing, typically **0.5 mm** — a reduction of **500×**.

2. **Creates thin films of milk:** The conical discs force milk into thin, near-horizontal films that flow between the discs. Particles in these films have a short, perpendicular migration path.

3. **Enhances separation efficiency:** The probability that a particle reaches the disc surface before being swept out is exponentially increased. The separation efficiency follows:

$$\eta_{sep} = 1 - e^{-K \cdot A_{disc}/V_{film}}$$

where $K$ is the sedimentation constant. Higher $n$ (more discs) and smaller $h$ (closer spacing) directly increase $\eta$.

4. **Enables counter-current flow:** Cream (lighter) migrates **inward and upward** along the underside of one disc, while skim (heavier) migrates **outward and downward** along the topside of the disc below. This counter-current pattern maximizes contact time.

5. **Disc half-angle effect:** The half-angle $\alpha$ balances two needs:
   - Small $\alpha$ → longer residence time, better separation
   - Large $\alpha$ → better sludge discharge, easier cleaning
   - Optimum is around **35° – 45°** in modern designs.

6. **Engineering outcome:** With 100 discs and 0.5 mm spacing, the bowl volume is utilized 200× more effectively than without discs, allowing **5000 – 50,000 L/h** throughput at >99.5 % fat removal.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Q: What is bactofuge treatment? Describe the construction of a bactofuge. Explain the process flow, operating parameters, and its industrial significance in cheesemaking. [14 marks]**

#### Part (a) – Definition, Construction, and Operating Parameters (7 marks)

**Definition:**
Bactofuge treatment is the centrifugal removal of bacterial cells and spores from milk using a high-speed, hermetic, steam-sterilizable separator (the bactofuge), producing a low-bacteria main stream (97 – 98 %) and a small concentrated bactofugate stream (2 – 3 %).

**Construction of a Bactofuge:**

1. **Hermetic Bowl:** Fully enclosed stainless steel bowl operating at 9000 – 14,000 RPM. Hermetic sealing prevents aerosol recontamination of the low-bacteria stream.

2. **Wide-Angle Disc Stack:** Conical discs with half-angle 40° – 50° and spacing 1.0 – 2.0 mm, optimized for capturing small bacterial particles (0.5 – 2 µm) with low density contrast.

3. **Steam-In-Place (SIP) Capability:** The bowl is sterilizable with steam at 110 – 125 °C to ensure the equipment does not become a source of contamination.

4. **High-Precision Bearings and Vibration Sensors:** Because of the high rotational speed, magnetic or oil-lubricated bearings with continuous vibration monitoring are mandatory.

5. **Discharge System:** Self-cleaning models use hydraulic pistons or tangential nozzles to periodically eject the bactofugate without disassembling the bowl.

6. **Paring Discs (Centripetal Pumps):** Discharge both the main stream and the bactofugate as separate streams under pressure.

7. **Inlet Preheater:** Heats milk to 55 – 72 °C before entry, reducing viscosity and increasing Stokes' settling velocity.

8. **Aseptic Recombination Loop:** A small auxiliary heat exchanger (130 °C / 4 s) sterilizes the bactofugate, which is then aseptically recombined with the main stream.

**Operating Parameters:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Rotational speed | 9000 – 14,000 RPM |
| g-force | 9,000 – 14,000 × g |
| Operating temperature | 55 – 72 °C |
| SIP temperature | 110 – 125 °C |
| Bactofugate fraction | 2 – 3 % of feed |
| Spore removal | 90 – 99 % |
| Bacterial removal | 90 – 95 % |

#### Part (b) – Process Flow and Industrial Significance (7 marks)

**Process Flow (as per Section 4.2 diagram):**

1. **Raw milk** → preheating in PHE (regeneration section).
2. **Preheater** → raises milk to 62 – 72 °C.
3. **Bactofuge** → separates into main stream (97 – 98 %) and bactofugate (2 – 3 %).
4. **Bactofugate** → sterilized at 130 °C / 4 s in dedicated PHE.
5. **Aseptic recombination** → sterilized bactofugate rejoins main stream.
6. **Vacuum deaeration** → removes entrained air.
7. **HTST pasteurization** (72 °C / 15 s) → final safety step.
8. **Cheese milk storage** → ready for cheesemaking.

**Industrial Significance:**

1. **Prevents Late-Blowing Defect:** *Clostridium tyrobutyricum* spores survive pasteurization and germinate in cheese, producing gas (CO₂ and H₂) that causes **holes and cracks** in Swiss, Gouda, and Edam cheeses. Bactofugation reduces spore load by 90 – 99 %, virtually eliminating this defect.

2. **Extends Shelf Life:** Combined with pasteurization, bactofugation enables **ESL (Extended Shelf Life) milk** of 21 – 28 days at 4 °C, compared to 7 – 14 days for pasteurized-only milk.

3. **Enables High-Quality Cheese:** Lower spore count reduces bitterness, texture defects, and off-flavors. Improves yield by reducing protein breakdown from bacterial proteases.

4. **Avoids Over-Processing:** Bactofugation replaces the need for UHT or double-pasteurization, preserving the fresh, raw-milk flavor in cheese.

5. **Regulatory and Export Compliance:** Many export markets (EU, Japan) require low-spore cheese milk; bactofugation is an accepted pre-treatment.

6. **Economic Value:** Bactofugate (concentrated in spores and somatic cells) can be used in animal feed or fermented products, with no waste.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Mark-Loss Hotspots:**
> 1. **Confusing bactofuge with pasteurizer:** Bactofugation does NOT kill bacteria; it physically *removes* them. Marks are deducted if students write "sterilization" instead of "physical removal."
> 2. **Missing the recombination step:** Many students forget that the bactofugate is *sterilized and recombined*. Just discarding it would waste 2 – 3 % of feed and lose 30 % of the fat. Marks deducted if recombination loop is omitted.
> 3. **Wrong g-force values:** Cream separator = ~10,000 × g; bactofuge = ~14,000 × g. Mixing up the values signals confusion.
> 4. **Forgetting SIP (Steam-In-Place):** A bactofuge is not the same as a regular separator — its hermetic + SIP design is what makes it biosecure. Marks deducted if sterilization is not mentioned.
> 5. **No diagram labeled:** KTU examiners expect at least one labeled block-diagram or schematic in 14-mark questions. Always include one.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 – High-Yield Recall Points**

### Construction of Separator Components
- ☐ Bowl = precision-machined SS 316L, 4000 – 7500 RPM for cream separator
- ☐ Disc stack = 80 – 150 conical discs, spacing 0.4 – 0.8 mm, half-angle 30° – 45°
- ☐ Paring discs are stationary *centripetal pumps* that discharge skim and cream
- ☐ Top disc closes the stack; bottom distributor feeds milk via radial holes
- ☐ Sludge accumulates in the outer periphery
- ☐ All milk-contact surfaces: SS 316L, Ra ≤ 0.8 µm, CIP-compatible

### Bactofuge Treatment
- ☐ Bactofuge = hermetic, SIP-capable, 9000 – 14,000 RPM, 9000 – 14,000 × g
- ☐ Disc spacing 1.0 – 2.0 mm, half-angle 40° – 50°
- ☐ Operating temperature 55 – 72 °C
- ☐ Removes 90 – 99 % of bacteria and spores
- ☐ Bactofugate = 2 – 3 % of feed, must be sterilized (130 °C / 4 s) and recombined
- ☐ Used to prevent **late-blowing defect** in Swiss, Gouda, Edam cheeses
- ☐ Used to produce **ESL milk** (21 – 28 day shelf life)

### Key Formulas (Memorize for KTU 2024)
- ☐ $a_c = \omega^2 r$
- ☐ $Z = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g} = \dfrac{\pi^2 N^2 r}{900 \, g}$
- ☐ $v_s = \dfrac{2 R_p^2 \Delta\rho \, \omega^2 r}{9 \mu}$
- ☐ $s_{eq} = \dfrac{h}{\cos \alpha}$
- ☐ $n = \dfrac{(r_2 - r_1) \tan \alpha}{h} + 1$
- ☐ $\eta_{bactofuge} = 1 - \dfrac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}$

### Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid
- ☐ Do not call a bactofuge a "pasteurizer" — it is a *physical separation* device
- ☐ Always include the **recombination loop** in the bactofuge flow diagram
- ☐ Always state **steam sterilization (SIP)** as a bactofuge design feature
- ☐ Distinguish clearly between **separator** (cream) and **bactofuge** (bacteria)
- ☐ Units: RPM for speed, × g for g-force, °C for temperature, m³/h for flow
- ☐ For 14-mark answers, always provide a **labeled diagram** plus theory
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrafiltration and Reverse Osmosis Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT615 - DAIRY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ultrafiltration and Reverse Osmosis Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Ultrafiltration (UF)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
Ultrafiltration (UF) is a **pressure-driven membrane separation process** that uses a porous membrane with a nominal pore size in the range of **0.001 to 0.1 micrometres (μm)** to physically separate macromolecules, colloids, proteins, and suspended solids from a liquid feed based on size exclusion (sieving mechanism). In dairy technology, UF operates at transmembrane pressures (TMP) of typically **1 to 10 bar** and is used to fractionate milk components—primarily whey proteins from lactose and minerals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorise:**
> "Ultrafiltration is a membrane process in which a pressure difference of 1–10 bar forces a liquid through an asymmetric membrane whose pore size is large enough to allow water, salts, and small solutes to pass while retaining macromolecules such as proteins, fat globules, and microorganisms."

**Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine a **kitchen sieve with tiny holes**—fine enough to hold back boiled rice while letting the starchy water drain through. UF works exactly like this sieve, but at the molecular level: the membrane acts like a microscopic colander. Small things (water, lactose, minerals = **permeate**) pass through; big things (proteins, fat = **retentate**) get retained. The "push" you apply to drain rice is the **transmembrane pressure** in UF.

### 1.2 Reverse Osmosis (RO)

**Formal Definition:**
Reverse Osmosis (RO) is a **pressure-driven membrane separation process** in which a semi-permeable membrane (pore size ≈ **0.0001 μm**, effectively non-porous dense membrane) rejects dissolved salts, low molecular weight solutes, and water is forced through against its chemical potential gradient. RO requires high operating pressures of **10 to 80 bar** to overcome the natural **osmotic pressure** of the feed solution. In the dairy industry, RO is used for **pre-concentration of milk and whey**, water reclamation, and partial demineralization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept:** Osmosis = natural flow of solvent from low to high concentration side. Reverse Osmosis = applying external pressure greater than osmotic pressure forces solvent from high to low concentration side (reversed direction).

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Picture two compartments divided by a **selective wall that only water can pass through**. Salt sits in one side. Naturally, water drifts toward the salty side to "dilute" it (osmosis). Now imagine pressing the salty water with a **piston harder than the natural pull**—water is forced backward through the wall, leaving salt behind. That is reverse osmosis in action—like **squeezing water out of a sponge soaked in salty liquid**.

### 1.3 Key Distinguishing Metrics

| Parameter | Ultrafiltration (UF) | Reverse Osmosis (RO) |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Membrane pore size | **0.001 – 0.1 μm** | **< 0.001 μm (dense)** |
| Operating pressure | **1 – 10 bar** | **10 – 80 bar** |
| Mechanism | Sieving | Solution-diffusion |
| Retains | Proteins, fat, bacteria | Salts, lactose, water |
| Permeate | Water, lactose, minerals | Almost pure water |
| Typical use in dairy | Whey protein concentration | Milk/water pre-concentration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric (Bold):** **Recovery Rate (R) = (V_permeate / V_feed) × 100 %**. A typical dairy UF operates at **R = 80–95 %**, while RO operates at **R = 30–90 %** depending on osmotic load.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Membrane Architecture and Separation Principle

**Asymmetric Membrane Structure (KTU High-Yield Concept):**
Both UF and RO membranes are **asymmetric**—composed of a very thin, dense top "skin" layer (0.1–1 μm) supported by a thicker porous sub-layer (50–200 μm). The thin skin determines selectivity; the support provides mechanical strength.

- **UF membrane skin:** porous; separation by **size exclusion** (sieving).
- **RO membrane skin:** dense, non-porous; separation by **solution-diffusion** mechanism (solute dissolves in membrane, diffuses across, desorbs).

### 2.2 Operating Principle — The "Why" and "How"

**Step-by-Step Logic of Pressure-Driven Membrane Filtration:**

1. **Feed Introduction:** Liquid feed (milk, whey, water) is pumped into the membrane module under pressure.
2. **Pressure Differential Creation:** TMP = P_feed – P_permeate – Δπ (osmotic back-pressure).
3. **Selective Transport:** Solvent (water) and small molecules pass through membrane pores (UF) or dissolve-diffuse (RO).
4. **Retentate Accumulation:** Larger species are retained and exit as a concentrated stream.
5. **Concentration Polarisation:** Solute builds up at membrane surface, increasing local viscosity and reducing flux.
6. **Backwash / Cleaning:** Periodic reverse-flow cleaning restores performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concentration Polarisation** is the silent performance killer in UF/RO. KTU examiners love asking its definition and remedies (high cross-flow velocity, turbulent flow, membrane cleaning, pulsation).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $J = \dfrac{V_p}{A \cdot t}$ | Permeate flux definition | L m⁻² h⁻¹ (LMH) |
| 2 | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m}$ | Darcy's law for flux (resistance-in-series model) | LMH |
| 3 | $\Delta P = P_f - P_p$ | Transmembrane pressure (without osmotic effect) | bar or Pa |
| 4 | $\text{TMP} = \dfrac{P_{f,in} + P_{f,out}}{2} - P_p$ | Practical TMP (average feed – permeate) | bar |
| 5 | $\text{True TMP} = P_{f,avg} - P_{p} - \Delta\pi$ | Effective driving force (accounts for osmotic pressure) | bar |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{V_p}{V_f} \times 100\%$ | Recovery (volumetric) | % |
| 7 | $\text{Rejection } \sigma = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}\right) \times 100\%$ | Solute rejection coefficient | % |
| 8 | $\pi = i \cdot C \cdot R \cdot T$ | Van 't Hoff osmotic pressure | Pa |
