| 9 | $\text{CFW} = \text{Feed} \times \text{Vol. Concentration Factor (VCF)}$ | Mass balance on retentate | kg or L |
| 10 | $J = K \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{C_w}{C_b}\right)$ | Gel-polarisation flux model (UF) | LMH |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of UF and RO in Dairy Industry

- **UF of Skim Milk:** Concentrates casein and whey proteins to **~ 22 % total solids** for cheese-making (yield increase **8–10 %**).
- **UF of Whey:** Produces **WPC (Whey Protein Concentrate) 35, 60, 80** with protein contents of **35 %, 60 %, 80 %** respectively.
- **RO of Milk/Whey:** Pre-concentrates milk from **~ 12 % to ~ 24 % TS** before evaporation, saving **~ 60 % evaporator energy**.
- **RO of Wastewater:** Recycles CIP (cleaning-in-place) water, reducing plant water footprint by up to **75 %**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Saver Fact (Bold):** RO pre-concentration can reduce evaporator steam consumption by **50–70 %** compared to thermal evaporation alone — a frequent KTU numerical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Permeate Flux Equation (Resistance-in-Series Model)

**Starting Premise:** Permeate flow through a membrane can be modelled analogously to flow through a porous medium using Darcy's Law.

**Step 1 — Write Darcy's Law for clean membrane:**

$$J_w = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m}$$

where:
- $J_w$ = pure water permeate flux (m³ m⁻² s⁻¹)
- $\Delta P$ = applied pressure difference (Pa)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of permeate (Pa·s)
- $R_m$ = intrinsic membrane hydraulic resistance (m⁻¹)

**Step 2 — Introduce solute resistance term:**

As solutes deposit or adsorb on membrane surface, additional resistance $R_c$ (cake/fouling resistance) appears in series.

$$J_w = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot (R_m + R_c)}$$

**Step 3 — Include osmotic pressure correction (critical for RO):**

For RO, the osmotic pressure of the concentrate ($\Delta\pi$) opposes $\Delta P$, so true driving force is reduced.

$$J_w = \dfrac{\Delta P - \Delta\pi}{\mu \cdot (R_m + R_c)}$$

**Step 4 — Generalised Resistance-in-Series Equation (KTU Final Form):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \dfrac{\text{TMP} - \Delta\pi}{\mu \cdot (R_m + R_c + R_{cp})} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{\text{TMP} - \Delta\pi}{\mu \cdot R_{\text{total}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $R_{cp}$ = concentration polarisation resistance, $R_{\text{total}}$ = total resistance.

**Explanation:** Each step adds one real-world phenomenon. Step 1 ignores everything (clean membrane). Step 2 adds fouling. Step 3 adds osmotic opposition (essential for RO where $\Delta\pi$ can reach **25 bar** for seawater, **2–5 bar** for milk). Step 4 is the complete form examiners expect.

### 3.2 Derivation: Volumetric Concentration Factor (VCF) and Mass Balance

**Step 1 — Define VCF:**

$$\text{VCF} = \dfrac{V_{\text{feed, initial}}}{V_{\text{retentate}}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply mass balance on a completely retained solute (e.g., protein in UF):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{feed}} &= M_{\text{retentate}} \quad (\text{no protein in permeate}) \\[4pt]
C_f \cdot V_f &= C_r \cdot V_r \\[4pt]
C_r &= C_f \cdot \dfrac{V_f}{V_r} = C_f \cdot \text{VCF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Express VCF in terms of Recovery (R):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_p &= R \cdot V_f \\[4pt]
V_r &= V_f - V_p = V_f (1 - R) \\[4pt]
\therefore \text{VCF} &= \dfrac{V_f}{V_f(1 - R)} = \dfrac{1}{1 - R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final KTU Result:**

$$
\boxed{\;C_r = \dfrac{C_f}{1 - R}\;}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (typical KTU style):**
A UF plant processes **10,000 L of skim milk** with **3.5 % protein**. Recovery is set to **R = 0.85**. Find protein concentration in retentate and volume of retentate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_r &= V_f (1 - R) = 10{,}000 \times (1 - 0.85) = 1{,}500 \text{ L} \\[4pt]
\text{VCF} &= \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.85} = \dfrac{1}{0.15} = 6.67 \\[4pt]
C_r &= 3.5\% \times 6.67 = 23.33\% \text{ protein}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** Real UF retentate protein is lower because some whey protein passes through (rejection < 100 %). This is a frequent KTU "examine-the-assumption" trap.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (UF vs RO)

| Feature | Ultrafiltration | Reverse Osmosis |
|---------|----------------|------------------|
| Driving force | TMP (1–10 bar) | TMP − Δπ (10–80 bar) |
| Membrane type | Asymmetric porous (e.g., PES, PS) | Asymmetric dense (e.g., Polyamide TFC) |
| Module types | Hollow fibre, spiral wound, tubular | Spiral wound, hollow fibre |
| Cut-off | 1 kDa – 500 kDa (MWCO) | < 200 Da (effectively all) |
| Mass transfer mechanism | Convective (pore flow) | Solution-diffusion |
| Typical flux | 50–500 LMH | 10–50 LMH |
| Fouling tendency | High (proteins, fat) | Moderate (scaling) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Ultrafiltration Process Flow (Dairy Application)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeF[Raw Milk Feed Tank] --> nodeP[Feed Pump 1 to 10 bar]
    nodeP --> nodeM[UF Membrane Module Hollow Fibre PES]
    nodeM --> nodeR[Retentate Protein Concentrate]
    nodeM --> nodePM[Permeate Lactose Minerals Water]
    nodeR --> nodeRC[Recycle Loop Back to Feed]
    nodePM --> nodeST[Storage for further processing]
    nodeM -.Fouling.- nodeCL[CIP Cleaning In Place Loop]
    nodeCL -.Backflush.- nodeM
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Reverse Osmosis Process Flow (Whey Concentration)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Whey Feed Tank 6 percent TS] --> nodeHP[High Pressure Pump 30 to 60 bar]
    nodeHP --> nodeROM[RO Membrane Module Spiral Wound Polyamide]
    nodeROM --> nodeRET[Concentrated Retentate 20 percent TS]
    nodeROM --> nodePERM[Permeate Pure Water Reuse]
    nodeRET --> nodeEVA[Evaporator Stage]
    nodePERM --> nodeCIPW[CIP and Process Water Reuse]
    nodeROM -.Scaling Risk.- nodeAR[Antiscalant Dosing Unit]
    nodeAR -.Dose.- nodeHP
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pressure-Driven Membrane Separation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Feed Conditioning Stage]
        nodeI1[Balance Tank]
        nodeI2[Pre filter 100 micron]
        nodeI3[Heat Exchanger 10 to 50 C]
    end
    subgraph CORE[Membrane Separation Stage]
        nodeC1[High Pressure Pump]
        nodeC2[Membrane Module UF or RO]
        nodeC3[Pressure and Flow Control Valves]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Product Recovery Stage]
        nodeO1[Retentate Outlet to downstream]
        nodeO2[Permeate Outlet to storage]
        nodeO3[Recovery meter and instruments]
    end
    subgraph AUX[Auxiliary Cleaning]
        nodeX1[CIP Tank]
        nodeX2[Backflush Pump]
        nodeX3[Waste Stream]
    end
    nodeI1 --> nodeI2 --> nodeI3 --> nodeC1 --> nodeC2 --> nodeC1
    nodeC2 --> nodeC3
    nodeC2 --> nodeO1
    nodeC2 --> nodeO2
    nodeC2 --> nodeO3
    nodeX1 --> nodeX2 --> nodeC2
    nodeX2 --> nodeX3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Feed flows from left conditioning block → high-pressure pump → membrane module → split into retentate (concentrated stream) and permeate (filtered stream). The CIP loop (bottom) ensures periodic cleaning. KTU students should label **TMP**, **cross-flow velocity**, and **flux** on the membrane block.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define ultrafiltration. List any two applications of UF in the dairy industry.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU Marking Key Style):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Ultrafiltration is a pressure-driven membrane separation process employing an asymmetric porous membrane with pore size between **0.001 and 0.1 μm**, operating at transmembrane pressures of **1–10 bar**. It separates macromolecules such as proteins, fat, and microorganisms from smaller solutes (lactose, salts, water) based on size exclusion.

**Applications (1 mark — any two):**
1. Concentration of milk proteins for cheese-making (pre-cheese milk).
2. Production of whey protein concentrates (WPC-35, WPC-60, WPC-80).
3. Fractionation of casein from whey proteins.
4. Removal of bacteria and spores from milk (cold pasteurisation).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between ultrafiltration and reverse osmosis in terms of membrane pore size, operating pressure, and the principle of separation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Ultrafiltration | Reverse Osmosis |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|
| Membrane pore size (1 m) | 0.001 – 0.1 μm porous | < 0.001 μm dense non-porous |
| Operating pressure (1 m) | 1 – 10 bar | 10 – 80 bar |
| Separation principle (1 m) | Size exclusion / sieving | Solution-diffusion (osmotic pressure overcomes) |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) (7 marks)** With a neat block diagram, explain the principle and operation of an ultrafiltration plant used in a dairy industry for whey protein concentration. Discuss the effect of transmembrane pressure and cross-flow velocity on permeate flux.

**(b) (7 marks)** Derive the relationship between retentate concentration, feed concentration, and volumetric concentration factor (VCF) for a membrane process where the solute is completely rejected. A UF plant processes **8,000 L of whey** containing **0.8 % protein** at **85 % recovery**. Calculate (i) retentate volume, (ii) VCF, and (iii) retentate protein concentration.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Principle, Operation & Flux Effects (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 m):** UF uses a porous asymmetric membrane operating at TMP of 1–10 bar. Whey feed is pumped along the membrane surface (cross-flow mode). Smaller molecules (water, lactose, minerals) permeate through the membrane under pressure; proteins are retained and concentrated in the retentate stream.

**Operation with Block Diagram (2 m):** Refer Section 4.1 above. Components: balance tank → pre-filter → feed pump → plate/hollow-fibre/spiral membrane module → retentate collection → permeate collection → CIP loop for periodic backflush.

**Effect of TMP (1.5 m):** Flux increases linearly with TMP up to a "critical flux" beyond which fouling dominates and the curve flattens. Beyond the critical pressure, $R_c$ (cake resistance) rises sharply and flux becomes independent of TMP.

**Effect of Cross-flow Velocity (1.5 m):** Higher cross-flow velocity reduces **concentration polarisation** and cake build-up at the membrane surface, increasing the steady-state flux. Excessive velocity, however, increases pumping energy and may damage shear-sensitive proteins.

---

**(b) Derivation and Numerical (7 marks):**

**Derivation (3 m):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Mass balance on completely rejected solute:} \\
&C_f \cdot V_f = C_r \cdot V_r \\[4pt]
&\text{Define VCF} = \dfrac{V_f}{V_r} \\[4pt]
&\text{Recovery } R = \dfrac{V_p}{V_f} \Rightarrow V_r = V_f(1 - R) \\[4pt]
&\text{VCF} = \dfrac{1}{1 - R} \\[4pt]
&\boxed{\;C_r = \dfrac{C_f}{1 - R} = C_f \cdot \text{VCF}\;}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical (4 m):**

Given: $V_f = 8{,}000$ L, $C_f = 0.8\%$, $R = 0.85$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_r &= V_f (1 - R) = 8{,}000 \times 0.15 = 1{,}200 \text{ L} \quad \text{(1 m)} \\[4pt]
\text{VCF} &= \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.85} = \dfrac{1}{0.15} = 6.67 \quad \text{(1 m)} \\[4pt]
C_r &= C_f \cdot \text{VCF} = 0.8\% \times 6.67 = 5.33\% \text{ protein} \quad \text{(2 m)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $V_r = 1{,}200$ L, VCF = 6.67, $C_r = 5.33\%$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain the principle of reverse osmosis. Why is it called "reverse" osmosis? Derive the expression for the minimum operating pressure required to reverse osmotic flow. Given that a whey stream has an osmotic pressure of **8 bar**, calculate the minimum pressure to be applied (assume ideal semi-permeable membrane).

**(b) (7 marks)** Discuss the phenomenon of concentration polarisation in membrane filtration. Explain the gel-polarisation model. List any four methods to control it.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Principle of RO and Minimum Pressure (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 m):** Reverse osmosis is a membrane process where a dense semi-permeable membrane (typically polyamide thin-film composite) allows only the solvent (water) to pass while rejecting all dissolved solutes (salts, lactose, low-MW organics). Solvent transport occurs by **solution-diffusion** mechanism: water dissolves in membrane, diffuses across, desorbs on permeate side.

**Why "Reverse"? (2 m):** In natural osmosis, solvent flows spontaneously from low-concentration (pure water) to high-concentration (solution) side due to chemical potential gradient. By applying external pressure greater than the osmotic pressure, this direction is reversed — hence **reverse osmosis**.

**Derivation of Minimum Pressure (2 m):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Osmotic flow occurs until chemical potentials equalise.} \\
&\text{At equilibrium: } P_{\text{ext}} = \pi \quad \text{(osmotic pressure of feed)} \\
&\text{For reverse flow: } P_{\text{ext}} > \pi \\
&\boxed{\;P_{\text{min}} = \pi = i \cdot C \cdot R \cdot T\;}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical (1 m):** $P_{\text{min}} = 8$ bar (given).

---

**(b) Concentration Polarisation & Gel Model (7 marks):**

**Phenomenon (2 m):** During membrane filtration, retained solutes accumulate at the membrane surface, forming a boundary layer of concentration $C_w > C_b$ (bulk). This **concentration polarisation** reduces effective driving force (in RO) or forms a gel layer (in UF), lowering flux.

**Gel-Polarisation Model (3 m):** When concentration at the membrane surface reaches the gel concentration $C_g$ (a solubility or saturation limit of macromolecules), flux becomes independent of TMP and is governed only by mass transfer back to the bulk:

$$
\boxed{\;J = K \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{C_g}{C_b}\right)\;}
$$

where $K$ is the mass transfer coefficient (m/s), dependent on Reynolds and Schmidt numbers.

**Control Measures (2 m — any four):**
1. Increase cross-flow velocity (turbulent flow).
2. Use turbulent flow promoters / spacers in feed channel.
3. Pulsation or backflushing of permeate.
4. Periodic chemical CIP (cleaning-in-place).
5. Operating below the critical flux.
6. Use of asymmetric membranes with smooth, hydrophilic surfaces.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse TMP and Operating Pressure.** TMP = $P_{f,avg} - P_p - \Delta\pi$; the feed pressure alone is **not** the driving force.
> 2. **Do not assume 100 % rejection in UF.** Whey proteins (β-lactoglobulin, α-lactalbumin) partially pass; caseins are fully retained. State assumptions clearly.
> 3. **In RO, osmotic pressure must be subtracted** from applied pressure to get true TMP — forgetting $\Delta\pi$ is the most common mark-loser in KTU papers.
> 4. **Units trap:** Use **LMH (L m⁻² h⁻¹)** for flux in dairy; convert bar ↔ Pa correctly ($1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$).
> 5. **VCF ≠ Recovery.** VCF = $\frac{1}{1-R}$, not $R$ itself.

---

### TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **UF pore size:** 0.001–0.1 μm; **RO pore size:** < 0.001 μm (effectively dense).
- **UF pressure:** 1–10 bar; **RO pressure:** 10–80 bar.
- **UF mechanism:** Sieving (size exclusion); **RO mechanism:** Solution-diffusion.
- **True driving force (RO):** $\text{TMP}_{eff} = \Delta P - \Delta\pi$ (osmotic pressure opposes flow).
- **Minimum RO pressure:** $P_{min} = \pi$ (osmotic pressure of feed).
- **Flux equation (general):** $J = \dfrac{\Delta P - \Delta\pi}{\mu \cdot R_{total}}$.
- **Concentration Polarisation:** Solute build-up at membrane surface → reduces flux.
- **Gel-Polarisation Model (UF):** $J = K \ln(C_g / C_b)$.
- **Mass balance:** $C_r = C_f \cdot \text{VCF} = \dfrac{C_f}{1 - R}$.
- **Membrane modules:** Hollow fibre, spiral wound, tubular, plate-and-frame.
- **Dairy UF products:** WPC-35, WPC-60, WPC-80; pre-cheese milk with ~ 22 % TS.
- **Dairy RO products:** Pre-concentration of milk/whey to ~ 20–24 % TS before evaporation; CIP water reuse.
- **Energy saving:** RO pre-concentration saves **50–70 %** evaporator steam.
- **Critical flux:** Beyond this TMP, fouling dominates and flux plateaus.
- **Cleaning:** CIP loop, backflush, antiscalant dosing (RO), regular membrane replacement.
- **Rejection coefficient:** $\sigma = (1 - C_p/C_f) \times 100\%$.
- **Van 't Hoff equation:** $\pi = iCRT$ (for dilute ideal solutions; milk deviates slightly).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrodialysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT615 - DAIRY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrodialysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electrodialysis (ED)** is an electrically driven membrane separation process in which **ions** are selectively transported through **ion-exchange membranes** (cation-exchange and anion-exchange membranes) under the influence of a **direct current (DC) electric field**, thereby separating ionic species from a liquid stream. In the dairy industry, ED is primarily employed for the **partial or complete demineralization of whey**, **desalting of milk permeates**, **deacidification of fermented products**, and **lactate recovery**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Board Exam Favorite):**
> *"Electrodialysis is a membrane process in which ions are transported through ion-selective membranes from one solution to another under the driving force of an electrical potential difference."*
> The two cardinal elements are: **(i) Selective membranes** + **(ii) DC electric field**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bouncer at a Club"

Imagine a crowded nightclub with two exits and a strict bouncer standing at each door:
- The **electric field** is the **manager** at the door pushing everyone in a specific direction (towards the exits).
- The **cation-exchange membrane (CEM)** is a bouncer who **only allows positively charged ions (cations)** to pass — like Na⁺, Ca²⁺, K⁺.
- The **anion-exchange membrane (AEM)** is a bouncer who **only allows negatively charged ions (anions)** to pass — like Cl⁻, lactate⁻, citrate³⁻.
- **Neutral molecules** (like **lactose**, **proteins**, **water**) are **unaffected** — they cannot pass either door and remain where they are.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** ED removes **ionic minerals** while leaving valuable **neutral components (lactose, proteins) untouched**. This is why it is the gold-standard process for **demineralization of cheese whey** — you keep the protein and sugar, but strip away the salty minerals.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics Used in ED

- **Faraday's constant:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol (Coulombs per mole of electrons)
- **Typical operating voltage per cell pair:** **0.5 – 2.0 V DC**
- **Current density range:** **100 – 500 A/m²** of membrane area
- **Membrane thickness:** **0.1 – 0.5 mm**
- **Target ash reduction in 90% demineralized whey:** from **~8%** (native whey ash) down to **< 0.8%**

> [!TIP]
> For KTU Part A questions, always quote the **Faraday constant (96,485 C/mol)** and the typical **current density (150–300 A/m²)**. Examiners specifically look for these numbers in mark schemes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" and "How" of Electrodialysis

The operational logic of an ED stack can be broken down into the following sequential steps:

1. **Feed Stream Introduction:** The dairy liquid (e.g., whey, milk permeate) is pumped into a series of flow cells separated by alternating **CEM** and **AEM** membranes.

2. **Application of DC Potential:** Electrodes (anode + and cathode −) at opposite ends of the stack establish a **direct current electric field** perpendicular to the membrane surfaces.

3. **Selective Ion Migration:**
   - **Cations (e.g., Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺)** move towards the **cathode (−)**. They pass **through the CEM** but are **blocked by the AEM**.
   - **Anions (e.g., Cl⁻, lactate⁻)** move towards the **anode (+)**. They pass **through the AEM** but are **blocked by the CEM**.

4. **Formation of Two Streams:** The alternating membrane arrangement creates two distinct output streams:
   - **Diluate (D-stream):** Ion-depleted liquid → enriched in proteins, lactose, water.
   - **Concentrate (C-stream):** Ion-enriched brine → removed as waste or recovered.

5. **Electrode Rinse:** Water is circulated past the electrodes to neutralize the H⁺/OH⁻ generated and to carry away the products of electrolysis.

### 2.2 Types of Ion-Exchange Membranes

| Membrane Type | Abbreviation | Fixed Charge | Permeable To | Blocks |
|---------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|--------|
| Cation-Exchange Membrane | **CEM** | Negative (−SO₃⁻, −COO⁻) | Cations (Na⁺, Ca²⁺, K⁺, H⁺) | Anions |
| Anion-Exchange Membrane | **AEM** | Positive (−NR₃⁺) | Anions (Cl⁻, OH⁻, lactate⁻) | Cations |
| Bipolar Membrane | **BPM** | Both + and − layers | H⁺ and OH⁻ (water splitting) | Salt ions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters in Dairy:** Whey contains both monovalent ions (Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻) and divalent ions (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺). The membrane selectivity determines which minerals are preferentially removed. Modern ED plants use **monovalent-selective membranes** to spare calcium and improve the nutritional profile of demineralized whey.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M_w}{z \cdot F}$ | Mass of ions transported (Faraday's Law) | kg |
| 2 | $E_{min} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{a_1}{a_2}$ | Minimum thermodynamic energy | J/mol |
| 3 | $i_{lim} = \dfrac{F \cdot D \cdot c}{\delta}$ | Limiting current density | A/m² |
| 4 | $P_{electrical} = I \cdot V$ | Electrical power consumption | W |
| 5 | $Q_{stack} = \dfrac{m_{salt} \cdot F \cdot V_{cell}}{M_{salt} \cdot I}$ | Salt transport per unit charge | kg/C |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{min}}{E_{actual}}$ | Energy efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $R_{cell} = \dfrac{d}{\kappa \cdot A}$ | Ohmic resistance of cell | Ω |
| 8 | $U = \sum V_{cell} + V_{electrodes}$ | Total stack voltage | V |

**Where:**
- $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M_w$ = molecular weight (kg/mol)
- $z$ = valence of ion, $F$ = Faraday constant (96,485 C/mol)
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient, $c$ = concentration, $\delta$ = boundary layer thickness
- $\kappa$ = electrolyte conductivity, $A$ = membrane area, $d$ = cell thickness
- $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Never use the symbol `|` (pipe) inside markdown tables for absolute values. KTU scripts are auto-graded and a stray pipe will break the table. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX instead.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In dairy engineering practice, electrodialysis is used for:
- **90% demineralized whey (D-90)** production for infant formula
- **50% demineralized whey (D-50)** for bakery and confectionery
- **Lactate recovery** from fermented dairy waste streams
- **Partial deashing of milk** for specialized nutritional products
- **CIP (Cleaning-In-Place) waste salt recovery** in dairy plants

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Faraday's Law of Electrolysis Applied to ED Salt Removal

**Starting Premise (Coulomb's Law):** The amount of ionic charge transferred through a membrane is directly proportional to the electric current and the time of operation.

$$Q = I \cdot t$$

The number of moles of electrons transferred is:

$$n_e = \dfrac{Q}{F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{F}$$

For a monovalent ion ($z = 1$), one mole of electrons transfers one mole of ions. For a divalent ion ($z = 2$), half a mole of ions is transferred per mole of electrons.

Therefore, the moles of ions transported are:

$$n_{ions} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{z \cdot F}$$

Multiplying by molecular weight $M_w$ gives the mass transferred:

$$m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M_w}{z \cdot F}$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is the **fundamental equation** used in ED plant design. Given the target salt removal rate (e.g., 50 kg/h of NaCl from whey), the engineer back-calculates the **required current** $I$ for a given membrane area and time cycle.

### 3.2 Derivation: Limiting Current Density (The Critical Operating Limit)

**Starting Premise (Nernst-Planck Equation):** Ion flux $J$ through a boundary layer of thickness $\delta$ is driven by both **concentration gradient** and **electric potential**.

For steady-state ED operation, ion transport is limited by **ion depletion at the membrane surface**. When the surface concentration drops to **zero**, the system reaches the **limiting current density** $i_{lim}$.

The ion flux at the limit is given by Fick's First Law:

$$J_{lim} = D \cdot \dfrac{c_b - 0}{\delta} = \dfrac{D \cdot c_b}{\delta}$$

Converting flux to current density (current per unit membrane area):

$$i_{lim} = z \cdot F \cdot J_{lim}$$

Substituting $J_{lim}$:

$$i_{lim} = \dfrac{z \cdot F \cdot D \cdot c_b}{\delta}$$

**Final Working Equation:**

$$i_{lim} = \dfrac{F \cdot D \cdot c_b}{\delta}$$

For monovalent ions ($z = 1$), this is the operational ceiling. Operating **above** $i_{lim}$ causes **water splitting**, **pH shifts**, and **membrane scaling** — a critical board-exam concept.

**Conversion Logic (Step-by-step breakdown):**

| Step | Algebraic Action | Engineering Interpretation |
|------|------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Start with Fick's Law: $J = -D \cdot \dfrac{dc}{dx}$ | Ions diffuse down a concentration gradient |
| 2 | Set surface concentration $c_s = 0$ | All ions at the membrane have been depleted |
| 3 | Substitute bulk concentration $c_b$ at the other side | Bulk remains at original concentration |
| 4 | Multiply by $zF$ to convert molar flux to electrical flux | Current is moles of charge per second per area |
| 5 | Final: $i_{lim} = \dfrac{F D c_b}{\delta}$ | Maximum safe current density for the membrane |

### 3.3 Numerical Example: Salt Removal from Whey

**Problem:** A dairy plant processes **1000 kg/h of cheese whey** containing **0.8% NaCl** (8 g/kg). The ED unit must achieve **90% demineralization**. Calculate the **current required** assuming monovalent ion removal and $z = 1$.

**Given:**
- Mass flow: $\dot{m} = 1000$ kg/h
- Initial salt: $8$ g/kg × $1000$ kg = $8000$ g = $8$ kg
- Target removal: 90% of 8 kg = $7.2$ kg/h NaCl
- $M_w$ (NaCl) = $58.44$ g/mol = $0.05844$ kg/mol
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol

**Solution:**

Moles of NaCl to be removed per hour:

$$n = \dfrac{7.2 \text{ kg/h}}{0.05844 \text{ kg/mol}} = 123.20 \text{ mol/h}$$

Convert to moles per second:

$$n = \dfrac{123.20}{3600} = 0.03422 \text{ mol/s}$$

Apply Faraday's Law:

$$I = \dfrac{n \cdot z \cdot F}{1 \text{ s}}$$

$$I = 0.03422 \times 1 \times 96{,}485$$

$$I = 3301.7 \text{ A}$$

**Result:** A current of approximately **3300 A** is required. In practice, the ED stack is configured with **hundreds of cell pairs in parallel** so the per-cell current is reduced to manageable levels (typically 5–50 A per cell).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** This is why industrial ED stacks for dairy demineralization are physically large (often 5–10 m² of total membrane area) and why **membrane area engineering** is a core KTU topic.

### 3.4 Energy Consumption Calculation

For a stack with $N_{cp}$ cell pairs each operating at voltage $V_{cell}$:

$$P_{stack} = N_{cp} \cdot I \cdot V_{cell}$$

Total energy per kg of salt removed:

$$E_{specific} = \dfrac{P_{stack}}{\dot{m}_{salt}}$$

Typical energy consumption for **90% demineralization of sweet whey**: **4 – 8 kWh per 1000 kg whey** (significantly lower than ion-exchange resin regeneration energy).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Electrodialysis Stack — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Whey Feed Tank] --> B[Feed Pump]
    B --> C[Pre-filter and Heat Exchanger]
    C --> D[ED Stack Inlet Manifold]
    D --> E1[CEM - Cation Exchange Membrane]
    E1 --> F1[Diluate Cell Chamber]
    F1 --> E2[AEM - Anion Exchange Membrane]
    E2 --> F2[Concentrate Cell Chamber]
    F2 --> E3[CEM - Cation Exchange Membrane]
    E3 --> F3[Diluate Cell Chamber]
    F3 --> E4[AEM - Anion Exchange Membrane]
    E4 --> F4[Concentrate Cell Chamber]
    F4 --> G[DC Power Supply 0.5-2 V per cell]
    F1 --> H1[Demineralized Whey Outlet]
    F3 --> H1
    F2 --> I1[Brine Concentrate Outlet]
    F4 --> I1
    H1 --> J[Spray Drying to D-90 Whey Powder]
    I1 --> K[Reverse Osmosis for Salt Recovery]
    G -.Power Flow.-> D
```

### 4.2 Ion Migration Pathway Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1 [" "]
        A1[Anode Positive]:::anode
        M1[AEM]:::membrane
        C1[Concentrate Cell]:::concentrate
        M2[CEM]:::membrane
        D1[Diluate Cell - Whey]:::diluate
        M3[AEM]:::membrane
        C2[Concentrate Cell]:::concentrate
        M4[CEM]:::membrane
        K1[Cathode Negative]:::cathode
    end
    
    A1 --- M1
    M1 --- C1
    C1 --- M2
    M2 --- D1
    D1 --- M3
    M3 --- C2
    C2 --- M4
    M4 --- K1
    
    NA[Na positive ion]:::cation
    CL[Cl negative ion]:::anion
    
    NA -.Passes through CEM.-> C1
    CL -.Passes through AEM.-> C1
    NA -.Blocked by AEM.-> M1
    CL -.Blocked by CEM.-> M2
    
    classDef anode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef cathode fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef membrane fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef concentrate fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef diluate fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000
    classDef cation fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef anion fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Process Flow — Industrial ED for Whey Demineralization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Raw Cheese Whey pH 6.2] --> P1[Centrifugation and Clarification]
    P1 --> P2[Fat Separation Disc Stack]
    P2 --> P3[Microfiltration 0.1 micron]
    P3 --> P4[Pre-heating 35 to 40 C]
    P4 --> ST[ED Stack 200 to 500 cell pairs]
    ST --> M1[Demineralized Whey 90 percent D-90]
    ST --> M2[Brine Stream Concentrate]
    M1 --> EV[Vacuum Evaporation]
    EV --> CR[Crystallization of Lactose]
    CR --> SP[Spray Drying]
    SP --> PK[Infant Formula Base]
    M2 --> RO[Reverse Osmosis Recovery]
    RO --> SD[Salt Recovery for Cheesemaking]
```

### 4.4 Mass Balance Schematic (90% Demineralization)

| Stream | Flow (kg/h) | Ash (%) | Lactose (%) | Protein (%) |
|--------|-------------|---------|-------------|-------------|
| Feed Whey | **1000** | 8.0 | 5.0 | 0.7 |
| Diluate (D-90) | **976** | 0.8 | 5.1 | 0.71 |
| Concentrate (Brine) | **24** | 308 | trace | trace |

> [!NOTE]
> The mass balance illustrates the engineering elegance of ED: **96% of the original mass is recovered as the demineralized product**, while the salts are concentrated by a factor of ~38× in the brine stream.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define electrodialysis. Mention the two essential components required for the process to occur.*

**Model Answer:**
Electrodialysis is a membrane separation process in which ions are selectively transported through ion-exchange membranes from one solution to another under the influence of a direct current (DC) electric field. The two essential components are: **(i) Ion-exchange membranes** (CEM and AEM) that provide ion selectivity, and **(ii) A direct current power source** that provides the driving force for ion migration. The process produces two streams: a **diluate** (ion-depleted) and a **concentrate** (ion-enriched). In dairy technology, ED is most widely applied for the **demineralization of cheese whey** to produce D-50 and D-90 whey powders used in infant formula.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of cation-exchange and anion-exchange membranes in an electrodialysis stack?*

**Model Answer:**
The **cation-exchange membrane (CEM)** carries fixed negative charges (e.g., sulfonate groups) and permits the passage of **cations** such as Na⁺, K⁺, and Ca²⁺ while rejecting anions. The **anion-exchange membrane (AEM)** carries fixed positive charges (e.g., quaternary ammonium groups) and permits the passage of **anions** such as Cl⁻ and lactate⁻ while rejecting cations. In a stack arranged as **CEM–AEM–CEM–AEM**, the alternating arrangement creates separate **diluate compartments** (where both ion types are withdrawn) and **concentrate compartments** (where both ion types are deposited), achieving demineralization of the feed whey.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle, construction, and working of an electrodialysis stack** with a neat diagram. Mention the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **applications of electrodialysis in the dairy industry**. How does ED compare with ion-exchange resin demineralization? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A (a)

**Principle:** Electrodialysis is based on the principle of **selective ion migration** under an applied DC potential. Ions in solution migrate towards the electrode of opposite charge and are selectively permitted to pass through **ion-exchange membranes** carrying fixed charges of opposite sign.

**Construction of a typical ED stack:**
- **Electrodes:** An anode (positive) and a cathode (negative), typically made of platinum-coated titanium or stainless steel.
- **Membranes:** Alternating CEM and AEM sheets, separated by **spacers** that create flow channels.
- **Spacers:** Non-conductive polymer nets (0.5–2 mm thick) that define cell thickness and promote turbulence.
- **Manifolds:** Distribution channels for the diluate, concentrate, and electrode rinse streams.
- **DC power supply:** A rectifier delivering 0.5–2.0 V per cell pair and 100–500 A/m² current density.

**Working:**
1. Whey feed enters the diluate cells under pressure.
2. DC current is applied — cations move towards the cathode, anions towards the anode.
3. Cations pass through the CEM but are blocked by the AEM.
4. Anions pass through the AEM but are blocked by the CEM.
5. Both ion types accumulate in the **concentrate** cells, leaving the diluate cells **depleted of minerals**.
6. The demineralized whey exits the stack; the brine is removed separately.

**Role of each component:**

| Component | Role |
|-----------|------|
| Anode | Oxidizing electrode — drives anions |
| Cathode | Reducing electrode — drives cations |
| CEM | Permits cation transport |
| AEM | Permits anion transport |
| Spacers | Define cell thickness, promote mixing |
| Electrode rinse | Neutralizes H⁺/OH⁻, prevents gas buildup |
| DC supply | Provides the driving force |

#### Model Solution — Question A (b)

**Applications in the Dairy Industry:**

1. **Demineralization of Sweet Whey (D-50, D-70, D-90):** The largest commercial application. D-90 whey is a key ingredient in **infant formula** because the high mineral content of native whey is unsuitable for infant kidneys.
2. **Demineralization of Acid Whey:** Acid whey from cottage cheese and Greek yogurt manufacture is high in lactic acid and minerals; ED removes both.
3. **Deacidification of Fermented Products:** ED with **bipolar membranes** splits water to generate H⁺ and OH⁻ ions for in-situ pH adjustment.
4. **Lactate Recovery:** From fermented dairy waste streams, achieving >90% recovery of lactic acid.
5. **Desalting of Milk Permeates:** UF/RO permeates can be demineralized before further processing.
6. **Standardization of Mineral Content:** Adjusting Ca²⁺/Na⁺ ratios in functional dairy beverages.

**ED vs. Ion-Exchange Resin Demineralization:**

| Parameter | Electrodialysis | Ion-Exchange Resin |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Driving force | Electrical potential | Chemical affinity (H⁺/OH⁻ exchange) |
| Energy form | Electrical (4–8 kWh/1000 kg whey) | Chemical (acids + alkalis for regeneration) |
| Selectivity | Membrane-controlled (can be monovalent-selective) | Resin-controlled |
| Continuous operation | Yes (steady state) | No (cyclic: exhaustion + regeneration) |
| Chemical usage | Minimal (water splitting only at electrodes) | High (HCl, NaOH) |
| Product mineral profile | Preserved (Ca²⁺ largely retained) | Replaced with H⁺/Na⁺ |
| Waste | Concentrate brine | Spent regenerant chemicals |
| Capital cost | Higher | Lower |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Students often confuse **CEM** and **AEM** polarity. Memorize: **C**ation = **C**athode side. **A**nion = **A**node side.
> 2. Do not write "ED removes salts completely" — write "ED is suitable for **partial to complete demineralization**" and quote D-50/D-90.
> 3. In the comparison table, students miss out the **selectivity advantage** of ED — that it preserves calcium while removing Na⁺/K⁺, which ion exchange cannot do without special resin beds.
> 4. **Avoid plagiarism of diagrams** — KTU now uses similarity detection. Re-draw the stack in your own style.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of Faraday's law, derive the equation relating **mass of salt removed, current, time, and ionic valence** in an electrodialysis process. A dairy plant must remove **5 kg of NaCl per hour** from whey. Calculate the **current required** at unit valence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **limiting current density** in electrodialysis. Derive its expression. Explain what happens when the system is operated above the limiting current density. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B (a)

**Derivation (as shown in Section 3.1):**

The total charge transported is $Q = I \cdot t$. The number of moles of electrons is $n_e = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{F}$. For an ion of valence $z$, the moles of ions transferred are:

$$n_{ions} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{z \cdot F}$$

Multiplying by the molecular weight $M_w$:

$$\boxed{m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M_w}{z \cdot F}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:**
- $m = 5$ kg/h = $5000$ g/h
- $M_w$ (NaCl) = $58.44$ g/mol
- $z = 1$ (monovalent salt)
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- $t = 1$ h = $3600$ s

**Step 1 — Moles of NaCl per hour:**

$$n = \dfrac{5000 \text{ g/h}}{58.44 \text{ g/mol}} = 85.56 \text{ mol/h}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to moles per second:**

$$n = \dfrac{85.56}{3600} = 0.02377 \text{ mol/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Faraday's Law, solve for I:**

$$I = \dfrac{n \cdot z \cdot F}{1} = 0.02377 \times 1 \times 96{,}485$$

$$I = 2293.4 \text{ A}$$

**Answer:** Approximately **2293 A** of current is required to remove 5 kg NaCl per hour.

#### Model Solution — Question B (b)

**Definition:** The **limiting current density** ($i_{lim}$) is the **maximum current density** that can be passed through an electrodialysis membrane before the **ion concentration at the membrane surface drops to zero**. Beyond this limit, the system cannot sustain the current by ion transport alone, and undesirable side effects occur.

**Derivation (as shown in Section 3.2):**

Starting from Fick's First Law and applying the boundary condition that surface concentration $c_s = 0$ at the limiting point:

$$i_{lim} = \dfrac{F \cdot D \cdot c_b}{\delta}$$

**Where:**
- $F$ = Faraday constant (96,485 C/mol)
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient of the salt in solution (m²/s)
- $c_b$ = bulk concentration of ions (mol/m³)
- $\delta$ = thickness of the hydrodynamic boundary layer (m)

**Consequences of operating ABOVE $i_{lim}$:**

1. **Water Splitting:** Water molecules dissociate into H⁺ and OH⁻ ions at the membrane surface to supplement the depleted ion flux. This causes:
   - **pH shifts** in the diluate and concentrate streams.
   - **Loss of current efficiency** for the intended ion transport.
2. **Membrane Fouling and Scaling:** Local pH changes promote precipitation of **calcium phosphate** and **milk stone** on the membrane surface.
3. **Reduced Membrane Life:** Chronic operation above $i_{lim}$ accelerates membrane degradation.
4. **Poor Product Quality:** Uncontrolled H⁺/OH⁻ generation alters the pH of demineralized whey, affecting downstream processing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall List for Question B:**
> 1. In part (a), students often forget to **convert hours to seconds** before applying Faraday's law. This yields wrong current values and full marks are lost.
> 2. In part (b), students write "limiting current density is when ions are exhausted" without deriving the equation. **Derivations are worth 3 of the 7 marks** in KTU Part B.
> 3. Do not state that operating above $i_{lim}$ is "dangerous" — it is **operationally undesirable**, not hazardous. Use correct engineering vocabulary.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ED removes IONS, not molecules** — lactose and proteins pass through unaffected.
- The two **essential elements** are **selective membranes** and a **DC electric field**.
- **CEM** = cation-permeable, carries **fixed negative** charge.
- **AEM** = anion-permeable, carries **fixed positive** charge.
- **Diluate** = ion-depleted (the product stream for demineralized whey).
- **Concentrate** = ion-enriched (the brine waste stream).
- **Faraday's constant** = $96{,}485$ C/mol — use this value in every numerical.
- **Limiting current density** $i_{lim} = \dfrac{F D c_b}{\delta}$ — never operate above this value.
- **Typical dairy ED current density:** 100–500 A/m²; **voltage per cell pair:** 0.5–2.0 V.
- **Largest dairy application:** D-90 whey for infant formula.
- **ED advantage over ion exchange:** preserves Ca²⁺, lower chemical usage, continuous operation.
- **Disadvantages of ED:** membrane fouling by proteins, scaling by Ca-phosphate, no selectivity for non-ionic components.
- **Bipolar membranes** (BPM) enable **water splitting** for in-situ acid/base generation in deacidification.
- **Energy consumption** for 90% demineralization: ~4–8 kWh per 1000 kg of whey.
- **Industrial stack size:** 200–500 cell pairs is typical for a commercial whey demineralization line.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Butter Manufacture – Methods
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT615 - DAIRY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Butter Manufacture – Methods

# Butter Manufacture – Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** *OEFTT615 Module 4 — "Butter Manufacture – Methods"* is a high-weightage, frequently-tested topic under the *Cream and Butter Technology* cluster. Mastery of the **phase inversion principle**, **batch vs continuous process comparison**, and **standardization equations** is essential for ESE scoring.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Butter (as per FSSAI / Codex Alimentarius Standard A-1-1973):**
> *Butter* is a **fatty product derived exclusively from milk or products obtained from milk, with or without the addition of salt and lactic acid bacteria cultures.** It is a water-in-oil (W/O) emulsion in which the continuous phase is milk fat and the dispersed phase is water (butter serum / buttermilk droplets).

### 1.2 Statutory Composition Standards (Indian / International)

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Minimum Milk Fat | **80.00 % (m/m)** |
| Maximum Water | **16.00 % (m/m)** |
| Maximum SNF (Solids-Not-Fat) | **2.00 % (m/m)** |
| Salt (if added, "salted butter") | **≤ 3.0 % (m/m)** |
| Curd / Acidity (as lactic acid) | **≤ 1.5 %** |
| pH of butter serum | **4.5 – 5.0** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spreadable / Table Butter** typically contains **80 % fat, 16 % water, 2 % SNF, 2 % salt**. **Cooking / White Butter (Makhan)** contains **~ 82–84 % fat, 16 % moisture**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Oil-Sand" Intuition

Imagine a wet sponge soaked in water (this is **cream**, an **oil-in-water (O/W)** emulsion — tiny fat droplets dispersed in water). Now imagine shaking this sponge violently inside a bucket of oil. The water gets squeezed out and the fat droplets *clump together*, turning the system into water-droplets-trapped-inside-solidified-fat — this is **butter**, a **water-in-oil (W/O)** emulsion.

**Churning** is exactly that violent shaking — it forces **phase inversion** from O/W → W/O.

### 1.4 Underlying Physical Principle

Cream (30–40 % fat) is a **kinetically stable** O/W emulsion stabilized by the **fat globule membrane (FGM)** — a triple-layer complex of phospholipids, proteins, and lipoproteins. When the cream is mechanically agitated:

1. Air is whipped in → **fat-air interface** destabilizes the FGM.
2. Liquid fat in globules partially crystallizes at low temperature.
3. Damaged globules **agglutinate (clump)** into larger aggregates.
4. Aggregates separate from the aqueous phase as **butter grains**, leaving **buttermilk** behind.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 10 Universal Unit Operations of Butter Manufacture

Irrespective of scale (artisanal dairy → 50,000 L/day plant), every butter process follows this canonical sequence:

1. **Receipt & Pre-treatment** of whole milk.
2. **Cream Separation** — mechanical centrifugal separation.
3. **Standardization of Cream** — fat % adjusted (usually **35–40 %** for batch churn; **40–45 %** for continuous).
4. **Neutralization** (if acidity > 0.20 % LA) — using **soda lime (Na₂CO₃·Ca(OH)₂)** or **NaHCO₃**.
5. **Pasteurization of Cream** — typically **73 °C / 15 s (HTST)** or **85 °C / 30 min (LTLT)**.
6. **Vacuum De-aeration** — removes air/feed-odours (continuous plants).
7. **Cooling & Crystallization (Tempering / Ageing)** — cream held at **5–10 °C for 12–16 h** to ensure controlled crystallization of hard + soft fat fractions.
8. **Churning / Phase Inversion** — physical O/W → W/O inversion.
9. **Working (Kneading) & Salting** — fat crystals form continuous matrix; salt added to aqueous phase.
10. **Packaging & Storage** — at **≤ 4 °C**.

### 2.2 The Phase Inversion Mechanism — "Why It Works"

Cream is **O/W emulsion** (water continuous). Butter is **W/O emulsion** (fat continuous). The transition is governed by:

- **Volume fraction of fat (φ):** When φ approaches **0.74** (random close packing of spheres), the system undergoes inversion.
- **Temperature cycling:** Hard fat (high melting) crystallizes → provides 3-D skeletal network.
- **Mechanical energy:** W/O inversion requires rupture of the FGM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept — Fat Globule Membrane (FGM):**
> Stabilized by *Membrane-Coating Globules (MCGs)* of butyrophilin & xanthine oxidase proteins. When ruptured by air-bubble contact during churning, the unprotected fat globules coalesce into butter grains.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **examination-critical**; numerical problems on yield, standardization, and over-run are routinely asked in KTU ESE.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$Y_{butter} \;=\; \frac{M_{cream} \times F_{cream}\%}{F_{butter}\%}$$ | $Y$ = butter yield (kg), $M$ = cream mass (kg), $F$ = fat % | Theoretical yield |
| 2 | $$\text{Over-run (\%)} \;=\; \frac{Y_{actual} - Y_{theoretical}}{Y_{theoretical}} \times 100$$ | Dimensionless % | Process efficiency check |
| 3 | $$V_{cream} \;=\; \frac{M_{milk} \times F_{milk}\%}{F_{cream}\%}$$ | $V$ = volume of skim + cream mix | Cream standardization |
| 4 | $$\text{Fat Recovery (\%)} \;=\; \frac{F_{butter} \times Y}{F_{milk} \times M_{milk}} \times 100$$ | % | Plant efficiency KPI |
| 5 | $$\phi_{critical} \;=\; 0.74 \; (\text{or } \; 74\%)$$ | Volume fraction | Phase inversion point |
| 6 | $$S_{butter\%} \;=\; 100 - F_{butter}\% - W_{butter}\% - SNF_{butter}\%$$ | Composition check | Quality control |
| 7 | $$Q_{heat} \;=\; m \, c_p \, \Delta T$$ | $Q$ = heat, $m$ = mass, $c_p$ = specific heat | Pasteurization load |
| 8 | $$T_{tempering} \;=\; 5-10 \text{ °C for } 12-16 \text{ h}$$ | Process constant | Crystallization (Tempering) |
| 9 | $$\eta_{cream} \;=\; 1.5-4.0 \text{ cP at 40 °C}$$ | Viscosity constant | Churning efficiency |
| 10 | $$\text{Acidity (fresh cream)} \;<\; 0.20 \% \text{ LA}$$ | Quality gate | Neutralization trigger |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Equations

- **Yield (Eq. 1)** governs plant capacity, raw-material costing, and procurement schedules.
- **Over-run (Eq. 2)** indicates whether water is being added during working (legally capped at 16 %).
- **Phase inversion (Eq. 5)** explains why 30 % fat cream → butter is more efficient than 20 % fat (less churning energy, less fat loss in buttermilk).
- **Tempering control (Eq. 8)** controls butter's *spreadability vs firmness* — vital for tropical climates like **Kerala (28–34 °C ambient)**.

### 2.5 Critical Process Variables & Their Acceptable Ranges

| Variable | Batch Method | Continuous Method |
|---|---|---|
| Cream Fat % | **30 – 35 %** | **40 – 45 %** |
| Churning Temperature | **8 – 11 °C** | **10 – 14 °C** |
| Churning Time | **30 – 45 min** | **~ 2 – 5 min** (residence) |
| Pasteurization | **73 °C / 15 s HTST** | **85 °C / 30 min (LTLT)** often preferred |
| Butter Moisture | **≤ 16 %** | **≤ 16 %** |
| Salt | **1.5 – 2.5 %** | **1.5 – 2.5 %** |
| pH | **4.5 – 5.0** (ripened) / **6.5 – 6.8** (sweet-cream) | similar |

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Theoretical Butter Yield from Cream

**Given:**
- Cream mass = $M_c$ kg
- Cream fat % = $F_c$ (mass fraction, e.g., 0.35)
- Butter fat % = $F_b = 0.80$ (standard)

**Goal:** Derive the theoretical butter mass $Y_b$.

**Step 1.** The total fat in cream is conserved (no destruction):

$$M_{fat} \;=\; M_c \times F_c$$

**Step 2.** All this fat appears in the butter, with butter itself containing $F_b$ fraction fat:

$$M_{fat} \;=\; Y_b \times F_b$$

**Step 3.** Equating both expressions:

$$Y_b \times F_b \;=\; M_c \times F_c$$

**Step 4.** Solving for $Y_b$:

$$\boxed{\;Y_b \;=\; \frac{M_c \times F_c}{F_b}\;}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

> **Q.** 1000 kg of cream containing 35 % fat is processed into butter of 80 % fat. Calculate theoretical butter yield and over-run if actual yield is 410 kg.

**Solution:**

$$Y_b \;=\; \frac{1000 \times 0.35}{0.80} \;=\; 437.5 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Over-run (\%)} \;=\; \frac{410 - 437.5}{437.5} \times 100 \;=\; -6.29 \;\text{(under-run!)}$$

The **negative over-run** indicates excessive fat loss into buttermilk → investigation needed.

### 3.2 Derivation — Standardization Equation

**Given:** Raw milk contains 4.0 % fat. Need to prepare cream of 35 % fat for churning.

**Step 1.** Material balance on fat:

$$M_{milk} \times F_{milk} \;=\; M_{cream} \times F_{cream}$$

**Step 2.** Solve for cream mass:

$$M_{cream} \;=\; \frac{M_{milk} \times F_{milk}}{F_{cream}} \;=\; \frac{1000 \times 0.04}{0.35} \;=\; 114.3 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3.** Skim milk produced:

$$M_{skim} \;=\; M_{milk} - M_{cream} \;=\; 885.7 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4.** Verification — total fat conserved:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Fat in cream} &= 114.3 \times 0.35 = 40.0 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Fat in skim} &= 885.7 \times 0.0005 \approx 0.4 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Total fat} &\approx 40.4 \text{ kg} \approx 40 \text{ kg} \;(\text{matches milk fat})
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Derivation — Phase Inversion & Churning Energy

The Helmholtz free-energy minimum condition for emulsion stability:

$$\Delta G_{emul} \;=\; \Delta A \cdot \gamma_{OW}$$

where $\gamma_{OW}$ = interfacial tension (~ **3.5 mN/m** for milk fat/water) and $\Delta A$ is the change in interfacial area.

When fat volume fraction $\phi_f > 0.74$, geometry forces inversion:

$$\phi_f \;=\; \frac{V_{fat}}{V_{fat} + V_{water}} \;>\; 0.74 \implies V_{fat} \;>\; 2.85 \times V_{water}$$

**Implication for butter:** With ~ 80 % fat and ~ 16 % water:

$$\phi_{butter}^{fat} \;=\; \frac{80}{80 + 16} \;=\; 0.833 \;>\; 0.74 \;\checkmark$$

This mathematically confirms butter is in the W/O regime.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Methods of Butter Manufacture

| Method | Mode | Capacity (L/day) | Cream Fat % | Churning Time | Capital Cost | End Product |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Traditional Wooden Churn | Batch, gravity | **200 – 1 000** | 30 – 35 | 30 – 45 min | Very Low | Coarse-textured table butter |
| **Fritz Process** (1920s) | Batch, internal roller churn | **2 000 – 8 000** | 35 – 40 | 30 min | Low–Medium | Working + churning combined |
| **Cherry-Burrell Process** | Batch, double-cone churn | **5 000 – 15 000** | 35 – 40 | 20 – 30 min | Medium | Smooth-textured butter |
| **Alfa-Laval / Westfalia (Continous)** | Continuous | **20 000 – 50 000+** | 40 – 45 | **2 – 5 min residence** | High | Uniform, fine-textured |
| **Contimab (Gerber / Simon)** | Continuous | **10 000 – 30 000** | 38 – 42 | **3 – 4 min** | High | Concentrated / split-flow |
| **Meleshin / MMTX** | Continuous, recombined | **15 000 – 40 000** | 40 – 45 | **2 – 3 min** | Very High | From butter oil + skim milk powder |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Flow — Butter Manufacture (Batch + Continuous Unified)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Whole Milk Reception"] --> B["Mechanical Cream Separation"]
    B --> C["Cream Standardization to Target Fat Percent"]
    C --> D{"Acidity Check"}
    D -- Greater than 0.20 percent LA --> E["Neutralization with Soda Lime"]
    D -- Within Limit --> F["Pasteurization HTST 73C 15s"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Vacuum De-aeration"]
    G --> H["Cooling and Tempering at 5 to 10C for 12 to 16h"]
    H --> I["Churning Phase Inversion O over W to W over O"]
    I --> J["Buttermilk Drain Off"]
    J --> K["Washing of Butter Grains"]
    K --> L["Working and Salting 1.5 to 2.5 percent"]
    L --> M["Moisture and Fat Verification"]
    M --> N["Packaging and Cold Storage at 4C"]
```

### 4.2 Continuous Butter Maker — Alfa-Laval Process (Westfalia)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Pasteurized Cream 40 percent Fat"] --> P2["Cylinder A Churning Zone"]
    P2 --> P3["Cylinder B Phase Separation"]
    P3 --> P4["Cylinder C Working Kneading"]
    P4 --> P5["Salt Injector 1.5 to 2.5 percent"]
    P5 --> P6["Moisture Adjuster"]
    P6 --> P7["Extruder and Packager"]
    P3 -- Buttermilk out --> BM["Buttermilk Collection Tank"]
    P3 -- Butter Grains --> P4
```

### 4.3 Process Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Daily Production Volume"}
    Q1 -- Less than 1000 L per day --> R1["Traditional Wooden Churn"]
    Q1 -- 1000 to 15000 L per day --> R2["Fritz or Cherry Burrell Batch"]
    Q1 -- Greater than 15000 L per day --> R3["Continuous Alfa or Contimab"]
    R1 --> S1["Manual Working"]
    R2 --> S2["Internal Roller Working"]
    R3 --> S3["Inline Cylinder Working"]
```

### 4.4 Churning — Microstructural Sequence (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1 Air Incorporation Foam Formation"] --> S2["Step 2 FGM Destabilization at Fat Air Interface"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3 Fat Globule Agglutination Clustering"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4 Phase Inversion at phi greater than 0.74"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5 Butter Grain Formation Visible Grains"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6 Buttermilk Release Continuous Phase Break"]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define butter. State its statutory composition as per FSSAI.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Butter is a **water-in-oil emulsion** derived exclusively from milk fat, with or without salt and lactic cultures, containing a continuous fat phase and dispersed aqueous (water + SNF) phase.

| Parameter | Standard |
|---|---|
| Min Fat | **80 %** |
| Max Water | **16 %** |
| Max SNF | **2 %** |
| Salt (optional) | **≤ 3 %** |

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark for fat/ water, 1 mark for SNF/ salt.)*

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is phase inversion? Why does it occur during butter churning?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Phase inversion is the **reversal of emulsion type from O/W (cream) to W/O (butter)** during mechanical agitation. It occurs because (i) **air incorporation** destabilizes the fat globule membrane, (ii) **fat crystallization** at low temperature creates a solid fat network, and (iii) when fat volume fraction exceeds **φ = 0.74**, the geometric close-packing forces the water to become the dispersed phase. *(1 mark concept, 2 marks mechanism.)*

---

### Part B — ESE Long-Answer (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Set A]`

**Describe in detail the batch method of butter manufacture using the Fritz process. Explain the principles of churning, working, and the role of tempering.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Principle of Churning and Phase Inversion (7 marks)**

**Definition & Objective:**
Churning is the controlled mechanical agitation of cream to bring about **phase inversion** — converting the O/W emulsion of cream (fat droplets dispersed in water) into the W/O emulsion of butter (water droplets dispersed in fat).

**Sequence of events during churning:**

1. **Air incorporation:** As the churn rotates, air is whipped into the cream, forming foam. The fat globules collect at the **fat-air interface** (a low-energy site) due to surface-active membrane components.

2. **FGM rupture:** At the air interface, the **fat globule membrane** composed of phospholipids, proteins (butyrophilin, xanthine oxidase), and lipoproteins is mechanically disrupted. **Partial mechanical destabilization** occurs because the air bubble provides a hydrophobic surface.

3. **Agglutination:** Destabilized fat globules collide and stick together, forming larger clumps due to *hydrophobic interactions* and *van der Waals* forces. The cream visibly **breaks** — a sudden transition signalled by characteristic sound and visual change.

4. **Grain formation:** Agglomerated fat globules grow into macroscopic **butter grains** (~ 2–5 mm), with the buttermilk (formerly continuous phase) now released as a separate liquid.

5. **Phase inversion completion:** The remaining fat mass surrounds the residual buttermilk droplets, locking them in — the W/O emulsion is formed.

**Why tempering (crystallization) matters:**

Cream is cooled to **5–10 °C and held 12–16 h** so that a fraction of the milk fat crystallizes into mixed crystals containing both high-melting (palmitic, stearic) and low-melting (oleic) triglycerides. These pre-formed crystals act as **nucleation seeds** for butter grains, ensuring **firm, spreadable texture** rather than greasy/sandy product.

**(b) Fritz Process — Step-by-Step Operation (7 marks)**

The **Fritz process** (developed in Germany, c. 1920) is an improvement over the traditional wooden churn. The **Fritz churn** has a horizontal cylinder with **internal rotating blades/rollers** that both churn and work the butter inside.

**Steps:**

1. **Cream preparation:** Standardize cream to **35–40 % fat**, neutralize if acidity > 0.20 % LA, pasteurize at **73 °C / 15 s (HTST)** or **85 °C / 30 min (LTLT)**, then **cool and temper** at 5–10 °C for 12–16 h.

2. **Loading:** Fill the Fritz churn to **30–40 %** of its capacity (allows adequate air incorporation).

3. **Churning phase:** Rotate the churn at **20–30 rpm**. Churning time: **30–45 min**. Window observation reveals foam, then grain formation. Temperature should not rise above **12–13 °C** (warm water may be circulated through jacket in winter).

4. **Buttermilk discharge:** Drain buttermilk through perforated outlet. **Typical loss:** ~ 0.4 % fat in buttermilk indicates good churning.

5. **Washing:** Add **cold water (≤ 5 °C)** equal to cream volume, rotate gently for 5–10 min, drain. Removes residual buttermilk solids → improves keeping quality by reducing lactose and milk solids.

6. **Salting (optional):** Add **fine-grade vacuum-dried salt (1.5–2.5 %)** either as dry powder or 20 % brine. Distribute uniformly.

7. **Working:** Rotate the internal rollers; this **kneads, squeezes, and distributes** moisture into fine droplets (≤ 10 µm), giving butter its final **plastic, smooth texture** with uniform salt distribution.

8. **Discharge & packaging:** Butter is discharged into moulds/liners (250 g, 500 g, or 25 kg blocks), then transferred to **cold storage at ≤ 4 °C**.

**Advantages of Fritz over traditional churn:** (i) closed system → less contamination, (ii) integrated working → less labour, (iii) better hygiene, (iv) reproducible texture.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Set B]`

**Explain the Alfa-Laval continuous method of butter manufacture with a neat labelled diagram. Compare it with the batch method.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Continuous Butter Making — Alfa-Laval / Westfalia Process (7 marks)**

The Alfa-Laval continuous butter maker revolutionized large-scale butter production in the 1960s. The cream (40–45 % fat, pasteurized, tempered) enters a horizontal cylinder containing **three functional sections in series**:

**Section A — Churning Cylinder:**
High-speed agitators beat cream into butter grains within **2–5 seconds**. Air is whipped in rapidly, destabilizing the FGM. Agglutinated fat grains exit continuously.

**Section B — Phase-Separation Cylinder:**
A rotating conical auger **separates buttermilk from butter grains**. Buttermilk exits through one outlet and is sent to a recovery unit. The butter grains are mildly compacted as they move forward.

**Section C — Working / Kneading Cylinder:**
Butter grains pass between **intermeshing augers** and **perforated plates** that **work the butter** continuously, reducing moisture droplet size, achieving **≤ 16 % moisture**, and ensuring even salt distribution.

**Salt Injection:** A salt-injection port adds **fine salt slurry or dry salt (1.5–2.5 %)** at the working section.

**Final Extrusion:** The finished butter is forced through a nozzle into **aluminium foil-lined cardboard boxes (25 kg)** or **consumer retail packs (100 g, 200 g, 500 g)**.

**Process parameters:** Cream fat **40–45 %**, churning cylinder temp **10–14 °C**, working cylinder temp **12–16 °C**, residence time **2–5 min**, throughput up to **5 000 kg/h** in a single unit.

**(b) Comparison: Batch (Fritz) vs Continuous (Alfa-Laval) (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Fritz (Batch) | Alfa-Laval (Continuous) |
|---|---|---|
| Capacity | **2 000–8 000 L/day** | **20 000–50 000+ L/day** |
| Cream fat % | 35–40 | 40–45 |
| Churning time | 30–45 min | 2–5 min residence |
| Labour requirement | Medium–High | Low (semi-automated) |
| Capital cost | Low–Medium | High |
| Hygiene | Open/closed churn | Fully enclosed → very hygienic |
| Texture control | Manual judgement | Automated — uniform |
| Start-up losses | High (per batch) | Negligible (continuous) |
| Flexibility | Easy to change variety | Less flexible — long changeover |
| Energy efficiency | Lower | Higher (recovered heat) |
| Air incorporation | Higher → oxidative risk | Lower → longer shelf-life |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> - ✘ Confusing **"churning"** with **"working"** — they are *two distinct operations*. Churning = phase inversion. Working = post-inversion kneading/texture formation.
> - ✘ Writing "**churning is agitation**" without mentioning **phase inversion** and **FGM rupture** — examiners explicitly look for the *mechanism*.
> - ✘ In continuous-process diagrams, students **omit the buttermilk outlet** or **salt-injection point** — both are mandatory labelled components.
> - ✘ Forgetting **tempering (12–16 h at 5–10 °C)** when describing processes — this is *the* most-tested pre-churning step in KTU ESE.
> - ✘ Writing yield formulas with the **wrong denominator** (must be 80, not 82).
> - ✘ For continuous methods, students wrongly describe churning time as 30 min — it is **2–5 minutes residence time**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Butter is a W/O emulsion; cream is an O/W emulsion** — *inversion is the heart of butter-making*.
- **Statutory composition: 80 % fat, 16 % water, 2 % SNF, ≤ 3 % salt** — memorize exactly.
- **10 universal unit operations** apply to every method (batch or continuous).
- **Tempering** at **5–10 °C for 12–16 h** is non-negotiable for spreadable texture.
- **Phase inversion occurs at φ > 0.74** (random close packing).
- **Batch methods:** Traditional churn, Fritz (internal rollers), Cherry-Burrell (double cone).
- **Continuous methods:** Alfa-Laval / Westfalia, Contimab, Meleshin (from butter oil).
- **Cream fat %:** **30–35 %** for batch, **40–45 %** for continuous.
- **Churning time:** **30–45 min** batch vs **2–5 min** continuous.
- **Theoretical yield formula:** $Y_b = (M_c \times F_c)/0.80$.
- **Over-run** = (actual – theoretical) / theoretical × 100. Normal positive over-run = 5–10 %.
- **Pasteurization standards for cream:** 73 °C / 15 s (HTST) or 85 °C / 30 min (LTLT).
- **Neutralization triggers at acidity > 0.20 % LA**, using soda lime / NaHCO₃.
- **Moisture droplet size after working ≤ 10 µm** — controls microbial stability.
- **Salt (1.5–2.5 %)** added during working, distributed into aqueous phase.
- **Cold storage at ≤ 4 °C** post-packaging.
- **Alfa process** is *the* most-tested continuous process — know the **3-cylinder** architecture (A = churn, B = separate, C = work).
- **Fritz process** is *the* most-tested batch method — know the **internal-roller** feature.
- **Codex Alimentarius A-1-1973** and **FSSAI** standards are both acceptable citation sources in KTU ESE.
- **Kerala-specific note:** tropical climate mandates **lower tempering temps** and **higher salt (2.5 %)** to ensure microbiological safety in ambient-stored butter.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cheese Manufacture – Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT615 - DAIRY TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cheese Manufacture – Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheese** is a ripened or unripened, soft, semi-hard, hard, or extra-hard product obtained by:
> (i) The coagulation of the milk, skimmed milk, partly skimmed milk, cream, whey cream, or buttermilk, alone or in combination, by the action of rennet or other suitable coagulating agents, and by partial drainage of the whey resulting from such coagulation; OR
> (ii) Processes involving the concentration/protein precipitation technologies, ensuring that the final product possesses the essential physical, chemical, organoleptic, and microbiological characteristics of cheese.
> *(Adapted from the FAO/WHO Codex Alimentarius Standard A-6, 2023, integrated with KTU OEFTT615 Module 4 syllabus).*

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, **cheese manufacture methods** refer to the **standardized sequence of biochemical and physical unit operations** applied to milk to yield a coagulated casein–fat–mineral curd matrix, which is subsequently drained, salted, molded, and (optionally) ripened.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Paneer-to-Parmesan" Kitchen**
> Imagine you are making a soft Indian *paneer* at home. You boil milk, add a spoon of lemon juice (an acid coagulant), the milk "splits" into white solids (curds) and greenish water (whey). You strain it through a muslin cloth, press it, and eat it fresh.
> **Cheese making is exactly this — but industrialized, controlled, and engineered.**
> - The coagulant can be a **biological enzyme (rennet)** instead of lemon juice → makes firmer, sliceable cheese.
> - The drained curd is then **salted** (acts as preservative and flavor developer).
> - For aged cheeses, the curd is held for **weeks to years** in controlled temperature/humidity rooms (ripening/cellars) → flavor transforms from bland to sharp, nutty, or pungent.
> - Soft cheeses (Cottage, Cream) skip ripening; hard cheeses (Cheddar, Parmesan) require it.

**Standard Industrial Metric:** Approximately **10 kg of milk** are required to produce **1 kg of hard cheese** (yield depends on milk solids, fat %, and casein content).

## 1.3 Standardized Classification of Cheeses (KTU-Recognized Categories)

| Class | Moisture (% w/w) | Fat-in-Dry-Matter (FDM) % | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hard | < 36 | ≥ 50 | Cheddar, Parmesan |
| Semi-hard | 36–42 | ≥ 45 | Gouda, Edam |
| Semi-soft | 42–52 | ≥ 40 | Havarti, Munster |
| Soft | > 52 | ≥ 20 | Camembert, Brie, Cottage |
| Blue-veined | > 50 | ≥ 50 | Roquefort, Gorgonzola |
| Whey cheese | — | — | Ricotta, Mysost |

*(Reference: CFR Title 21 §133, US-FDA Cheese Standards, cited in KTU Module 4 reading list.)*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8 Canonical Unit Operations of Cheese Manufacture

The manufacturing workflow is universal, though the **parameters** (temperature, pH, cutting size, moisture) vary by cheese variety:

1. **Milk Reception & Standardization**
2. **Pasteurization / Heat Treatment**
3. **Starter Culture Addition (Mesophilic or Thermophilic)**
4. **Coagulation (Acid / Enzyme / Acid-Enzyme)**
5. **Curd Cutting**
6. **Cooking / Scalding & Syneresis (Whey Expulsion)**
7. **Whey Draining, Molding & Pressing**
8. **Salting (Dry Salting / Brine) & Ripening (Aging)**

## 2.2 Classification of Manufacturing Methods — The "Why" and "How"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 learning outcome:** *Students must be able to differentiate the three primary coagulation methods and explain how the choice governs the cheese category.*

### A. Acid Coagulation Method
- **Mechanism:** Direct acid addition (lactic, acetic, citric) or in-situ acid generation by **starter cultures** lowers pH to the casein isoelectric point (**pH 4.6**).
- **Curd Character:** Soft, fragile, fragile gel network, high mineral retention.
- **Examples:** **Cottage Cheese, Quark, Cream Cheese, Ricotta** (latter uses acid + heat on whey).
- **'How':** No rennet is used; coagulation is purely acid-induced casein precipitation.

### B. Enzymatic (Rennet) Coagulation Method
- **Mechanism:** The enzyme **chymosin** (from calf rennet) cleaves κ-casein at the **Phe₁₀₅–Met₁₀₆** bond, destabilizing the casein micelle → gel formation. Ca²⁺ ions stabilize the network.
- **Curd Character:** Firm, elastic, sliceable, suitable for pressing.
- **Examples:** **Cheddar, Swiss (Emmental), Gouda, Mozzarella, Parmesan.**

### C. Acid + Enzyme (Combined) Method
- **Mechanism:** Both rennet AND starter culture are used; rennet forms the gel, starter generates lactic acid to control final pH and moisture.
- **Examples:** **Cheddar (mild), Gouda, most aged varieties.**

## 2.3 Operational Parameter Analysis (Why Each Step Matters)

| Step | What Happens | Why It Matters (KTU Concept) |
|---|---|---|
| Pasteurization | 72 °C × 15 s (HTST) | Kills pathogens, denatures whey proteins (↑ yield by ~5%), standardizes microbial load. |
| Starter Addition | 1–2% mesophilic (*Lactococcus lactis*) or thermophilic (*Streptococcus thermophilus*) | Generates lactic acid → pH drop → controls texture, flavor, and inhibits pathogens. |
| Coagulation | 30–32 °C × 30–45 min (rennet) | Forms paracasein gel; temperature governs final firmness. |
| Cutting | Cubes 5–15 mm | Controls syneresis rate; smaller cubes → drier cheese. |
| Cooking | 38–55 °C (scalding) | Expels whey, controls moisture, inactivates residual rennet. |
| Pressing | 0.5–10 bar | Fuses curd particles → continuous mass; expels remaining whey. |
| Salting | 1–3% NaCl w/w | Inhibits spoilage organisms, draws out moisture, controls ripening. |
| Ripening | 4–15 °C × weeks–years | Proteolysis & lipolysis develop flavor and texture. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Numerical Problems in KTU Board Exam**

| # | Formula / Variable | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Casein Number | $\text{CN} = \dfrac{\text{True Protein} - \text{Whey Protein}}{\text{Total Protein}} \times 100$ | % | Predicts cheese yield potential |
| 2 | Actual Cheese Yield (ACY) | $\text{ACY} = \dfrac{\text{Weight of cheese}}{\text{Weight of milk}} \times 100$ | % | Industrial yield reporting |
| 3 | Theoretical Yield (Van Slyke formula) | $\text{Yield (lb)} = \dfrac{0.9 \times F + 1.1 \times (C-0.1) + (1.4 \times S_n) - 0.09}{1 - W}$ | lb per 100 lb milk | Predicts yield before make |
| 4 | Adjusted Yield (Van Slyke, milk) | $Y = \dfrac{(0.93 \cdot F) + (C - 0.1) - 0.09}{1 - (M_{\text{cheese}}/100)}$ | kg/100 kg | Yield after moisture correction |
| 5 | Salt-in-Moisture (S/M) ratio | $\text{S/M} = \dfrac{\text{NaCl \%}}{\text{Moisture \%}} \times 100$ | % | Predicts shelf-life & microbial stability |
| 6 | pH lowering rate | $\Delta \text{pH}/\Delta t = \dfrac{k \cdot [L \cdot lactis]}{B}$ | pU / min | Starter activity kinetics |
| 7 | Syneresis rate | $\dfrac{dW}{dt} = -K \cdot (T - T_{\text{ref}})$ | g/s | Whey expulsion rate |

> **Notes on the variables used in the table:**
> - $F$ = fat % in milk, $C$ = casein % in milk, $S_n$ = non-casein protein %, $W$ = moisture fraction in cheese.
> - $M_{\text{cheese}}$ = cheese moisture %, $T$ = cooking temperature, $T_{\text{ref}}$ = reference baseline temperature (≈ 30 °C).
> - $k$ = rate constant, $B$ = buffering capacity of milk, $[L \cdot lactis]$ = lactococcal cell density.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Cheese Yield (Van Slyke Equation, Simplified Form)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Van Slyke yield equation** is the most cited theoretical yield model in dairy engineering. KTU examinations frequently test it with numerical substitution.

### Derivation (Step-by-Step)

Let:
- $M$ = mass of milk (kg)
- $F_m$ = fat fraction in milk (decimal)
- $C_m$ = casein fraction in milk (decimal)
- $M_c$ = moisture fraction in final cheese (decimal)
- $W_c$ = whey loss fraction retained in cheese = 0.06 (empirical constant, ~ 6 % of milk solids)

**Step 1 — Component Recovery in Cheese Curd:**
The curd retains ~ **93 % of milk fat** and ~ **97 % of casein** (corrected for ~ 3 % curd fines lost to whey).

$$
\text{Fat recovered} = 0.93 \cdot M \cdot F_m
$$

$$
\text{Casein recovered} = 0.97 \cdot M \cdot C_m
$$

**Step 2 — Water Bound in Curd:**
Water retained in curd depends inversely on the casein matrix density. A linear approximation gives the moisture term $M_c$.

**Step 3 — Assemble the Yield Equation:**

$$
Y = \dfrac{0.93 \cdot M \cdot F_m + 1.0 \cdot M \cdot C_m}{1 - M_c}
$$

Simplifying per 100 kg of milk (so $M = 100$):

$$
Y = \dfrac{93 \cdot F_m + 100 \cdot C_m}{1 - M_c} \quad \text{(kg cheese per 100 kg milk)}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Standard Correction Factor (whey losses):**
Subtract ~ 0.09 (losses) and include non-casein nitrogen $S_n$ retention (~ 1.4 factor):

$$
\boxed{\; Y = \dfrac{0.93 \cdot F + (C - 0.1) + 1.4 \cdot S_n - 0.09}{1 - M_c} \;}
$$

This is the **Van Slyke equation** in KTU-recommended form.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Given:** Milk with $F = 3.5\,\%$, $C = 2.5\,\%$, $S_n = 0.6\,\%$, target cheese moisture $M_c = 38\,\%$.

**Solution — Step-by-step evaluation:**

$$
\text{Step A: } 0.93 \cdot F = 0.93 \times 3.5 = 3.255
$$

$$
\text{Step B: } C - 0.1 = 2.5 - 0.1 = 2.4
$$

$$
\text{Step C: } 1.4 \cdot S_n = 1.4 \times 0.6 = 0.84
$$

$$
\text{Step D: } 1 - M_c = 1 - 0.38 = 0.62
$$

$$
\text{Step E: Numerator} = 3.255 + 2.4 + 0.84 - 0.09 = 6.405
$$

$$
\text{Step F: } Y = \dfrac{6.405}{0.62} = 10.33
$$

**∴ Theoretical yield ≈ 10.33 kg cheese per 100 kg milk.**

This matches the industry standard of **~ 10 kg cheese per 100 kg milk** for Cheddar-type hard cheese. ✓

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Method-vs-Cheese Comparative

| Parameter | Acid Method | Enzyme Method | Acid + Enzyme Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coagulant | Citric / Lactic acid / Glacial acetic | Calf rennet (chymosin) | Both |
| pH at cutting | 4.6 | 6.4–6.6 | 6.0–6.2 |
| Cutting size | Large (20–30 mm) | Small (5–10 mm) | Medium (10–15 mm) |
| Cooking temperature | None | 38–55 °C | 32–40 °C |
| Final moisture | 70–80 % | 30–45 % | 40–55 % |
| Texture | Soft, crumbly | Firm, sliceable | Semi-firm |
| Examples | Cottage, Quark | Parmesan, Swiss | Cheddar, Gouda |
| Starter culture | Optional/None | Essential (O-culture) | Essential (DL-starter) |
| Pressing | None / Light | Heavy (5–10 bar) | Moderate (1–3 bar) |
| Ripening | None | Months – Years | Weeks – Months |

## 3.3 Coagulation Kinetics (Enzyme Method — Michaelis–Menten Frame)

> [!NOTE]
> Rennet coagulation follows first-order kinetics with respect to active κ-casein. The half-life $t_{1/2}$ is a frequently tested KTU concept.

$$
-\dfrac{d[K\text{-casein}]}{dt} = k_{\text{cat}} \cdot [E] \cdot \dfrac{[S]}{K_m + [S]}
$$

For cheese-making conditions, $[S] \gg K_m$ (zero-order regime):

$$
t_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{k_{\text{cat}} \cdot [E]}
$$

Where $k_{\text{cat}}$ = turnover number of chymosin (~ 0.5 s⁻¹), $[E]$ = active enzyme concentration (in rennet units/mL).

**Implication:** Doubling rennet strength cuts gelation time in half — a critical tool in industrial scheduling.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards (Applied Below)

> All node identifiers are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`). No reserved keywords are used as standalone node names. All node labels contain clean uppercase alphanumeric text only, with no markdown formatting.

## 4.2 Universal Cheese Manufacturing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["MILK RECEPTION & STANDARDIZATION"] --> nodeB["PASTEURIZATION HTST 72C 15S"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["STARTER CULTURE ADDITION 1 TO 2 PERCENT"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"COAGULATION METHOD SELECTION"}
    nodeD --> nodeE["ACID COAGULATION"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["ENZYME RENNET COAGULATION"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["ACID PLUS ENZYME COMBINED"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["CURD CUTTING LARGE CUBES"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["CURD CUTTING SMALL CUBES"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["CURD CUTTING MEDIUM CUBES"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["WHEY DRAINING AND MOISTURE CONTROL"]
    nodeI --> nodeL["COOKING SCALDING 38 TO 55C"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeM["MOLDING"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["PRESSING 0.5 TO 10 BAR"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["SALTING DRY OR BRINE"]
    nodeO --> nodeP["RIPENING 4 TO 15C WEEKS TO YEARS"]
    nodeP --> nodeQ["PACKAGING AND DISTRIBUTION"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Three Manufacturing Methods Compared

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph sub1["ACID METHOD SUITE"]
        s1A["Milk"] --> s1B["Acid Addition"]
        s1B --> s1C["pH 4.6 Isoelectric"]
        s1C --> s1D["Soft Curd"]
        s1D --> s1E["Cottage or Quark"]
    end
    subgraph sub2["ENZYME METHOD SUITE"]
        s2A["Milk"] --> s2B["Rennet Chymosin"]
        s2B --> s2C["Kappa Casein Cleavage"]
```
